topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT414 - NATURE INSPIRED COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ant Colony Optimization (ACO): S-ACO, Ant Colony Optimization Metaheuristic, Combinatorial Optimization, ACO Metaheuristic Problem Solving Using ACO, Local Search Methods, Scope of ACO Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)?

**Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a population-based, **metaheuristic** optimization technique introduced by **Marco Dorigo** in his 1992 Ph.D. thesis, inspired by the foraging behaviour of real ants. It is used to find approximate solutions to difficult **combinatorial optimization problems (COPs)** by simulating the way real ants deposit and follow **pheromone trails** to discover the shortest path between their nest and a food source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECAT414 Module 2):**
> ACO is a probabilistic, constructive metaheuristic in which a colony of artificial ants iteratively build candidate solutions to a combinatorial optimization problem by exploiting a **pheromone model** (learned experience) and **heuristic information** (problem-specific greedy estimates). Over time, pheromone trails reinforce the edges belonging to high-quality solutions via a positive feedback loop, while **pheromone evaporation** ensures exploration and prevents premature convergence to suboptimal solutions.

### Conceptual Analogy — How Do Real Ants Find the Shortest Path?

Imagine a colony of ants placed at a food source with two possible paths back to the nest: a **short path** and a **long path**.

1. Initially, no pheromone is on the ground. Ants choose randomly → roughly 50% on each path.
2. Because the short path is shorter, ants travelling along it complete the round trip **faster**. They return to the food source and lay down a **fresh trail of pheromone**.
3. Soon, the short path accumulates **more pheromone** than the long one, because the ants on the longer path are still en route.
4. New ants sense the stronger trail and are statistically **more likely to follow the short path** (positive feedback / autocatalysis).
5. Meanwhile, pheromone **evaporates** over time (negative feedback), so the longer path gradually fades and the colony converges onto the shortest route.

> [!NOTE]
> Real ants are **almost blind** — they cannot "see" the geometry of the path. They only respond to the local **concentration of pheromone** on the ground. ACO replicates this *stigmergic* (indirect) communication using **artificial pheromone tables** $\tau_{ij}$ stored for each edge of the construction graph.

### 1.2 The Simple Ant Colony Optimization (S-ACO) Algorithm

**S-ACO (Simple ACO)** is the most elementary variant of the ACO family. It was proposed by Dorigo as a stripped-down algorithm to isolate the core idea and to provide a baseline for theoretical analysis. The "S" stands for *Simple*.

**Defining characteristics of S-ACO:**

- The pheromone update is performed **only by the best ant** of the current iteration (or the best-so-far ant), not by every ant.
- A **ranking/greedy selection** rule (often *random proportional* or *pseudo-random proportional*) governs how an ant chooses the next node.
- Pheromone **evaporation** is applied to all edges to avoid unlimited accumulation.
- It does not use any local search (pure construction) — making it the cleanest didactic example.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Why "Simple"?**
> S-ACO is treated as a "minimal" ACO: every additional mechanism (ACS, MMAS, local search, candidate lists) is *added on top* of S-ACO. Therefore, when examiners ask "explain S-ACO", they are testing whether you understand the **bare minimum** pheromone-update + transition-probability machinery before generalising to the full metaheuristic.

### 1.3 The ACO Metaheuristic — A General Framework

The **ACO metaheuristic** is a *high-level, problem-independent algorithmic framework* introduced by Dorigo, Di Caro, and Gambardella (1999) that **unifies all existing ACO variants** (Ant System, ACS, MMAS, S-ACO, …) under a single template. A metaheuristic is a higher-level procedure that orchestrates subordinate heuristics to escape local optima and produce good solutions for hard problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A metaheuristic is NOT a single algorithm** — it is a *recipe book* for designing algorithms. The ACO metaheuristic specifies **three algorithmic activities** that any ACO algorithm must implement:
> 1. **ConstructAntSolutions** — guided by pheromone trails and heuristic information.
> 2. **(Optional) ApplyLocalSearch** — to improve the constructed solutions.
> 3. **UpdatePheromones** — to increase pheromone on good edges and decrease it on bad ones (via evaporation).
>
> Different ACO algorithms differ in the *exact way* these activities are implemented.

### 1.4 Combinatorial Optimization (CO) — The Problem Class ACO Solves

**Combinatorial Optimization Problems (COPs)** are optimisation problems whose feasible solution set $S$ is **discrete** (finite, or countable) and whose objective is to find a solution that **minimises (or maximises)** a cost function $f : S \to \mathbb{R}$.

A combinatorial optimisation problem is formally defined as a triple:
$$P = (S, f, \Omega)$$
where:

- $S$ = the set of all **candidate (feasible) solutions**,
- $f$ = the **objective function** to be minimised (or maximised),
- $\Omega$ = a set of **constraints** that restrict $S$.

Most COPs of practical interest (TSP, QAP, VRP, Job-Shop Scheduling) belong to the class **NP-hard** — no known polynomial-time exact algorithm exists. Therefore, **approximate** metaheuristics like ACO are needed.

> [!NOTE]
> The ACO metaheuristic is designed to be **applicable to any combinatorial optimisation problem** as long as the problem can be represented as a *construction graph* (a weighted graph in which ants build paths) and as long as a meaningful **heuristic value** $\eta_{ij}$ can be associated with each edge.

### 1.5 Local Search Methods — The Refinement Engine

A **local search method** is a procedure that iteratively improves a single solution by exploring its **neighbourhood** $N(s)$ and moving to a better (or sometimes worse, in the case of simulated annealing) neighbour.

In modern ACO algorithms, the **constructed solutions** produced by ants are often **locally optimised** using a local search before the pheromone update. This is the key hybridisation: *ACO provides global exploration, local search provides local exploitation.*

Common local search methods used with ACO include:

- **2-opt, 3-opt, or Lin–Kernighan** moves for the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP).
- **Pairwise interchange** for assignment and scheduling problems.
- **Tabu Search** (TS), **Simulated Annealing** (SA), and **Iterated Local Search (ILS)** as more sophisticated variants.

### 1.6 Scope of ACO Algorithms

ACO has been applied to an unusually wide range of problems because its **pheromone + heuristic** decomposition is highly flexible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Application Domains of ACO (high-yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Routing problems** — Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP), Network/Internet routing.
> 2. **Assignment problems** — Quadratic Assignment Problem (QAP), Graph Colouring.
> 3. **Scheduling** — Job-Shop, Open-Shop, Project Scheduling, Crew Scheduling.
> 4. **Bioinformatics** — Protein Folding, DNA Sequencing, Haplotype Inference.
> 5. **Telecommunication networks** — Connectionless/connection-oriented routing, MPLS.
> 6. **Data mining and machine learning** — Rule induction, classification, clustering.
> 7. **Industrial / engineering design** — VLSI design, load balancing, set covering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model of the Construction Graph

To apply ACO to a problem, we model it as a **fully connected, weighted construction graph** $G = (C, L)$ where:

- $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_{n_c}\}$ is a finite set of **components** (nodes/cities/solution elements).
- $L = \{(c_i, c_j) : c_i, c_j \in C\}$ is the set of **edges (connections)** with associated:
  - $\tau_{ij}$ = **pheromone trail value** on edge $(i,j)$ (mutated over time),
  - $\eta_{ij}$ = **heuristic desirability** of edge $(i,j)$ (problem-dependent, static),
  - $d_{ij}$ = cost / distance of edge $(i,j)$ (often $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$).

> [!NOTE]
> The states of the problem are the **partial solutions** (paths already constructed). An ant moves from state $x = \langle c_i, \dots, c_k \rangle$ to state $y = \langle c_i, \dots, c_k, c_j \rangle$ by adding component $c_j$ to its partial path.

## 2.2 The Ant's Stochastic Decision Rule (Random Proportional Rule)

When an ant $k$ at node $i$ must choose the next node $j$ from its **feasible neighbourhood** $N_i^k$ (the set of not-yet-visited nodes), it uses the **Random Proportional Transition Rule (RP)**:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\displaystyle\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}} & \text{if } j \in N_i^{k} \\[1.0em]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Interpretation of each symbol:**

- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ — amount of pheromone currently on edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$.
- $\eta_{ij}$ — heuristic value of edge $(i,j)$, e.g., $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ for TSP.
- $\alpha \geq 0$ — **pheromone influence factor**; controls the relative weight of learned experience.
  - If $\alpha = 0$, only heuristic info is used (degenerate to a greedy heuristic).
  - If $\alpha$ is very large, ants blindly follow the strongest pheromone trail.
- $\beta \geq 0$ — **heuristic influence factor**; controls greediness.
- $N_i^{k}$ — the set of nodes still feasible for ant $k$ at node $i$ (often "unvisited cities").

## 2.3 Pheromone Update Equations

After all $m$ ants have constructed a complete solution in iteration $t$, the **pheromone update** is performed. The generic form is:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \underbrace{(1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t)}_{\text{evaporation}} \;+\; \underbrace{\Delta\tau_{ij}(t)}_{\text{deposition}}
$$

where:

- $\rho \in (0,1]$ is the **evaporation rate** (a hyper-parameter). At each iteration, a fraction $\rho$ of every pheromone value evaporates.
- $1-\rho$ is the **persistence factor**.
- $\Delta\tau_{ij}(t)$ is the **sum of pheromone deposits** made by all ants that used edge $(i,j)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why evaporation?** Without evaporation, pheromone would monotonically accumulate, causing all ants to converge on the first reasonable path found — this is *premature convergence* to a local optimum. Evaporation provides a **forgetting mechanism** that lets the colony explore new paths.

### 2.3.1 S-ACO Pheromone Update (Best-Ant Only)

In **S-ACO**, **only the iteration-best ant** (or sometimes the **best-so-far ant**) deposits pheromone:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{S\text{-}ACO}(t) = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{f(S^{\text{best}})} & \text{if edge }(i,j) \in S^{\text{best}} \\[0.5em]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $f(S^{\text{best}})$ is the cost (length) of the best tour found in the current iteration.

### 2.3.2 Generalised Ant System (AS) Pheromone Update (All Ants)

In the original **Ant System (AS)** — the most general version — **every** ant $k$ deposits pheromone on every edge it traversed, in proportion to the quality of its own solution:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{Q}{f(S^{k})}
$$

where $Q$ is a constant and $f(S^{k})$ is the cost of ant $k$'s solution. The total deposit is:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)
$$

## 2.4 The ACO Metaheuristic — Algorithmic Template

The ACO metaheuristic specifies that any ACO algorithm consists of a loop of three activities, until a termination condition $t \geq t_{\max}$ is met:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pseudo-Structure of the ACO Metaheuristic (KEEP MEMORISING):**
>
> ```
> procedure ACO_Metaheuristic
>     InitialisePheromoneTrails(τ)
>     while (not termination_condition) do
>         ConstructAntSolutions          // Activity 1
>         ApplyLocalSearch (optional)    // Activity 2
>         UpdatePheromones               // Activity 3
>     end while
> end procedure
> ```
>
> The **differences between ACO variants** lie *entirely* in the way these three activities are implemented.

## 2.5 Combinatorial Optimisation — Formal Taxonomy

| Property | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Static vs. Dynamic** | Static: problem data does not change during solution. Dynamic: data evolves over time. | Static → TSP; Dynamic → live network routing |
| **Discrete vs. Continuous variables** | COPs are discrete (or discretised). | QAP, scheduling |
| **NP-hardness** | No polynomial-time exact algorithm is known. | TSP, VRP, Graph Colouring |
| **Constraint type** | Equality, inequality, cardinality. | VRP capacity constraint |
| **Single vs. Multi-objective** | Single: one cost $f$. Multi: vector of costs. | Multi: time + cost + emissions |

A **decision problem** associated with a COP is the question: *"Does there exist a feasible solution with cost $\leq K$?"* — which is typically NP-complete.

## 2.6 Local Search Methods — Mathematical View

A local search algorithm is defined by:

- A **neighbourhood structure** $N : S \to 2^{S}$ — for each solution $s$, $N(s)$ is the set of "neighbour" solutions reachable by a single *move*.
- A **selection rule** for picking the next neighbour (greedy / best-improvement / first-improvement).
- A **stopping criterion** (local optimum, time budget, iteration limit).

The simplest form is **iterative improvement (hill climbing)**:

$$
s' \leftarrow \arg\min_{s \in N(s)} f(s); \quad s \leftarrow s'
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The quality of a local search depends almost entirely on the choice of $N(\cdot)$. For ACO + local search hybrids, the choice of $N(\cdot)$ must respect the **structure of the construction graph** of the underlying problem.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a high-density, board-exam-ready summary. Memorise the symbols $\alpha, \beta, \rho, \tau, \eta$ and the transition rule — these appear in nearly every ACO question.**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Role / Meaning** |
|:-----:|--------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Random Proportional Rule (transition probability) | $p_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ | Probability of ant $k$ moving $i \to j$. |
| 2 | Pheromone persistence | $(1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t)$ | Fraction of old pheromone retained after evaporation. |
| 3 | Pheromone evaporation | $\rho\,\tau_{ij}(t)$ | Fraction of old pheromone lost at iteration $t$. |
| 4 | General pheromone update | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}(t)$ | Master update equation. |
| 5 | S-ACO deposit (best ant) | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} = 1/f(S^{best})$ | Used only on edges of the iteration-best tour. |
| 6 | Ant-System deposit (all ants) | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / f(S^{k})$ | Each ant $k$ deposits in proportion to its tour cost. |
| 7 | Heuristic value (TSP) | $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ | Inverse of edge distance — closer = more attractive. |
| 8 | Pheromone influence parameter | $\alpha \geq 0$ | Weight of learned experience. |
| 9 | Heuristic influence parameter | $\beta \geq 0$ | Weight of problem-specific greediness. |
| 10 | Evaporation rate | $0 < \rho \leq 1$ | Forgetting rate (typical $\rho \approx 0.1$–$0.5$). |
| 11 | Constructive cost (TSP tour) | $L^{k} = \sum_{(i,j) \in T^{k}} d_{ij}$ | Length of the tour built by ant $k$. |
| 12 | Average tour length | $L_{avg} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{k=1}^{m} L^{k}$ | Used to decide when to switch to a new best ant. |
| 13 | Best-so-far cost | $L_{bs}$ | Best tour cost since the start of the run. |
| 14 | Combinatorial optimisation triple | $P = (S, f, \Omega)$ | Search space $S$, objective $f$, constraints $\Omega$. |
| 15 | Neighbourhood structure | $N : S \to 2^{S}$ | Defines reachable neighbour solutions for local search. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility of these formulas:**
> The same template is used to route packets in telecommunication networks (ant = packet, pheromone = routing table entry, $\eta_{ij}$ = link delay), to schedule trains (ant = a sequence of jobs), to fold proteins in bioinformatics, and to allocate crews in airlines. The triplet $(\alpha, \beta, \rho)$ acts as the *knob set* that controls the **exploration vs. exploitation** balance in any ACO application.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Random Proportional Rule (RP Rule)

The RP rule is **not derived** in the strict mathematical sense — it is a **constructive choice model**. However, it can be motivated from the **principle of maximum likelihood under a Boltzmann-style utility function**. We present the standard justification.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions

- Each edge $(i,j)$ has a *utility* to ant $k$ equal to the product of two factors:
  - the **pheromone factor** $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}$ — long-term memory of the colony.
  - the **heuristic factor** $[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$ — short-term, problem-specific preference.
- The ant's choice is a **noisy rational** choice: it picks $j$ with a probability proportional to the utility of $j$, normalised over the feasible set.

### 3.1.2 Construction of the rule

The "utility" of edge $(i,j)$ to ant $k$ at iteration $t$ is defined as:

$$
u_{ij}^{k}(t) = [\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}
$$

To convert this into a valid probability distribution, normalise over the feasible set $N_i^{k}$:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{u_{ij}^{k}(t)}{\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} u_{il}^{k}(t)}
$$

Substituting the utility expression:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}
$$

This is exactly the **random proportional rule** of S-ACO and AS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting cases (often asked in exams):**
> - If $\alpha = 0$: rule collapses to $p_{ij}^{k} = [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta} / \sum [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}$ — purely heuristic, no learning.
> - If $\beta = 0$: rule becomes $p_{ij}^{k} = [\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} / \sum [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}$ — purely pheromone-based, blind to distances.
> - If both $\alpha, \beta \to \infty$: deterministic, the ant always picks the edge with the **highest $\tau_{ij}^{\alpha} \eta_{ij}^{\beta}$**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Master Pheromone Update

We want a discrete-time update that simultaneously (a) **decays** the old pheromone and (b) **adds** new deposits.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Evaporation

The fraction of pheromone that survives on edge $(i,j)$ at the end of iteration $t$ is $(1-\rho)$:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{\text{evap}}(t) = (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t)
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Deposition

Each ant $k$ that traversed edge $(i,j)$ in iteration $t$ deposits $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$. The total deposit on $(i,j)$ is:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Combine

Adding evaporation and deposition:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}(t)
$$

### 3.2.4 S-ACO specialisation

In S-ACO, only the iteration-best ant deposits. Let $k^{\star}$ denote the index of the best ant and $L^{k^{\star}} = f(S^{k^{\star}})$ its tour cost. The deposit on each edge of its tour is:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k^{\star}}(t) = \frac{1}{L^{k^{\star}}}
$$

All other ants deposit $0$. Hence:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}(t) = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{L^{k^{\star}}} & \text{if } (i,j) \in T^{k^{\star}} \\[0.5em]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{L^{k^{\star}}} & \text{if } (i,j) \in T^{k^{\star}} \\[0.5em]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — RP Rule on a 4-City Mini-TSP

This example is *exactly the kind of 5–7 mark calculation* the KTU board sets.

> **Problem.** Four cities $\{A, B, C, D\}$ with distances (symmetric) and current pheromone values as shown. An ant at city $A$ has not yet visited $B, C, D$ (all in $N_A^k$). Parameters: $\alpha = 1, \beta = 2, \rho = 0.1, Q = 1$.

| Edge | $d_{ij}$ | $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ | $\tau_{ij}$ |
|:----:|:--------:|:---------------------:|:-----------:|
| A–B  | 2        | 0.500                 | 0.20        |
| A–C  | 3        | 0.333                 | 0.40        |
| A–D  | 5        | 0.200                 | 0.10        |

### 3.3.1 Compute the numerator for each candidate

$$
u_{AB} = (0.20)^{1} \cdot (0.500)^{2} = 0.20 \cdot 0.25 = 0.0500
$$

$$
u_{AC} = (0.40)^{1} \cdot (0.333)^{2} = 0.40 \cdot 0.1111 = 0.0444
$$

$$
u_{AD} = (0.10)^{1} \cdot (0.200)^{2} = 0.10 \cdot 0.04 = 0.0040
$$

### 3.3.2 Compute the denominator (sum)

$$
\sum = 0.0500 + 0.0444 + 0.0040 = 0.0984
$$

### 3.3.3 Compute transition probabilities

$$
p_{AB} = \frac{0.0500}{0.0984} \approx 0.5081
$$

$$
p_{AC} = \frac{0.0444}{0.0984} \approx 0.4512
$$

$$
p_{AD} = \frac{0.0040}{0.0984} \approx 0.0407
$$

Check: $0.5081 + 0.4512 + 0.0407 = 1.0000$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU answer writing:** The ant is most likely to go to $B$ ($50.8\%$) — it is **close** (high $\eta$) and has **moderate pheromone**. City $D$ is unlikely ($4.07\%$) because it is *both* far and poorly reinforced.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Pheromone Update (S-ACO)

> **Problem.** Suppose the iteration-best ant in the above example produces a tour $T^{k^{\star}} = A \to B \to C \to D \to A$ of total length $L^{k^{\star}} = d_{AB} + d_{BC} + d_{CD} + d_{DA} = 2 + 4 + 6 + 5 = 17$. Compute the updated pheromone on edge $A$–$B$ after this iteration, given $\tau_{AB}^{\text{old}} = 0.20, \rho = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Evaporation:**

$$
(1-\rho)\,\tau_{AB}^{\text{old}} = 0.9 \times 0.20 = 0.180
$$

**Step 2 — Deposition (S-ACO):** The ant traverses $A$–$B$, so it deposits:

$$
\Delta\tau_{AB}^{k^{\star}} = \frac{1}{L^{k^{\star}}} = \frac{1}{17} \approx 0.0588
$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$
\tau_{AB}^{\text{new}} = 0.180 + 0.0588 = 0.2388
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the answer:** Pheromone on $A$–$B$ has **increased from 0.20 → 0.2388**, a $19.4\%$ increase. If a *worse* ant had also used the edge, evaporation would have partially offset the deposit — reinforcing the role of evaporation as a *quality control* filter.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — S-ACO vs. Other ACO Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Comparison Table — Memorise the columns!**

| **Feature** | **S-ACO** | **Ant System (AS)** | **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | **MAX-MIN AS (MMAS)** |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Who deposits pheromone?** | Iteration-best (or best-so-far) ant | All $m$ ants | Iteration-best (global update) + local update by every ant | Iteration-best or best-so-far |
| **Evaporation?** | Yes | Yes | Yes (only in global update) | Yes |
| **Choice rule** | RP | RP | **Pseudo-random proportional** (with $q_0$ parameter) | RP |
| **Local search used?** | No (pure construction) | Optional | Almost always | Almost always |
| **Pheromone bounds** | None | None | $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ | $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ |
| **Year introduced** | 1996 (simplification of AS) | 1991 (original) | 1997 | 1997 |
| **Strength** | Simplicity, didactic clarity | Original baseline, easy to analyse | Strong exploitation via $q_0$ | Avoids stagnation, best known results |
| **Weakness** | Weak performance alone | Slow convergence | Sensitive to $q_0$ | Requires good bounds |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Local Search for Common COPs

| **Problem** | **Best Local Search Companion** | **Move / Neighbourhood** |
|-------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| TSP | 2-opt, 3-opt, Lin–Kernighan | Reverse a sub-tour |
| VRP | 2-opt* (intra-route), relocate (inter-route) | Move a customer between routes |
| QAP | Pairwise swap of two assignments | Swap two facility locations |
| Job-Shop Scheduling | Critical path / swap of adjacent jobs | Exchange two operations |
| Graph Colouring | 1-opt recolour, Kempe chain interchange | Move a node to a different colour class |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General ACO Metaheuristic — High-Level Control Flow

The diagram below captures the canonical three-activity loop of the ACO metaheuristic as specified by Dorigo, Di Caro & Gambardella (1999).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start ACO Metaheuristic]) --> initA[InitialisePheromoneTrails tau_ij with small tau_0]
    initA --> loopCheck{t >= t_max or convergence?}
    loopCheck -- No --> conA[Activity 1: ConstructAntSolutions<br/>m ants build tours using RP rule]
    conA --> locA{Activity 2: Apply Local Search?<br/>Optional}
    locA -- Yes --> lsA[Improve each solution using 2-opt or 3-opt]
    locA -- No --> updA
    lsA --> updA[Activity 3: UpdatePheromones<br/>Evaporate then deposit on best edges]
    updA --> loopCheck
    loopCheck -- Yes --> outA([Output best-so-far solution S_bs])
    outA --> endA([End])

    classDef activity fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class initA,conA,lsA,updA activity
    class loopCheck,locA decision
    class startA,outA,endA terminal
```

## 4.2 S-ACO Algorithm — Detailed Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start S-ACO]) --> initB[Set parameters m, alpha, beta, rho, t_max<br/>Initialise tau_ij = tau_0 for all edges]
    initB --> iterLoop{iteration t = 0, 1, ..., t_max?}
    iterLoop -- Continue --> placeB[Place m ants on randomly chosen start cities]
    placeB --> solLoop{For each ant k = 1, ..., m}
    solLoop -- Yes --> stepB[Ant k builds tour using RP rule<br/>p_ij = tau_ij^alpha x eta_ij^beta / sum tau_il^alpha x eta_il^beta]
    stepB --> solLoop
    solLoop -- No --> evalB[Compute tour length L^k for every ant k]
    evalB --> bestB[Identify iteration-best ant k_star<br/>with minimum L^k_star]
    bestB --> evapB[Evaporate pheromone on all edges<br/>tau_ij = 1 minus rho times tau_ij]
    evapB --> depB[Deposit pheromone only on edges of T^k_star<br/>tau_ij = tau_ij + 1 divided by L^k_star]
    depB --> trackB[Update best-so-far solution S_bs if needed]
    trackB --> iterLoop
    iterLoop -- Stop --> outB([Output best-so-far solution S_bs])
    outB --> endB([End])

    classDef step fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class initB,placeB,stepB,evalB,bestB,evapB,depB,trackB step
    class solLoop,iterLoop dec
    class startB,outB,endB term
```

## 4.3 Ant Solution Construction — Block-Level View

This block diagram shows the inner loop of *one ant* building *one* complete solution in ACO. It corresponds to **Activity 1** of the metaheuristic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    antStart([Ant k starts at node c_start]) --> memoryInit[Initialise visited set V_k = c_start<br/>Set current node i = c_start]
    memoryInit --> visitedCheck{Unvisited nodes<br/>in N_i^k?}
    visitedCheck -- No --> tourDone([Tour T^k complete])
    visitedCheck -- Yes --> compU[Compute utility u_il = tau_il^alpha x eta_il^beta for every l in N_i^k]
    compU --> compP[Compute probabilities p_il = u_il divided by sum of u_ij for j in N_i^k]
    compP --> roll[Roll random number r uniform in 0,1<br/>Select next node j with probability p_ij]
    roll --> moveStep[Move ant k to node j<br/>Add j to V_k and to partial tour]
    moveStep --> visitedCheck
    tourDone --> closeTour[Close the tour: return to start city if required]
    closeTour --> antEnd([Ant k yields solution S^k with cost f_S^k])

    classDef step fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class memoryInit,compU,compP,roll,moveStep,closeTour step
    class visitedCheck dec
    class antStart,tourDone,antEnd term
```

## 4.4 Pheromone Update Block — Evaporation + Deposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    updStart([Start UpdatePheromones]) --> evapLoop[For every edge i,j in construction graph]
    evapLoop --> evapCalc[tau_ij_new_temp = 1 minus rho times tau_ij_old]
    evapCalc --> evapLoop
    evapLoop -- All edges done --> depDecision{Which ACO variant?}
    depDecision -- S-ACO --> depS[Deposit only on edges of iteration-best tour T^k_star<br/>tau_ij = tau_ij + 1 divided by L^k_star]
    depDecision -- AS --> depAS[Deposit on edges of every ant k with weight Q divided by L^k]
    depDecision -- ACS --> depACS[Local update after each ant: tau_ij = 1 minus xi times tau_ij + xi times tau_0<br/>Global update only on best-so-far]
    depS --> boundCheck{Apply pheromone bounds?<br/>tau_min and tau_max}
    depAS --> boundCheck
    depACS --> boundCheck
    boundCheck -- Yes, MMAS --> clamp[Clip tau_ij to tau_min and tau_max]
    boundCheck -- No --> updEnd
    clamp --> updEnd([Pheromone update complete for iteration t])

    classDef step fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class evapLoop,evapCalc,depS,depAS,depACS,clamp step
    class depDecision,boundCheck dec
    class updStart,updEnd term
```

## 4.5 Hybrid ACO + Local Search Block Architecture

This is the **modern standard** — most competitive ACO algorithms (e.g., ACS+LK, MMAS+2-opt) operate as a *construct + improve* pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hybridStart([Start Hybrid ACO + Local Search]) --> con[Activity 1: ConstructAntSolutions<br/>m ants use pheromone and heuristic info]
    con --> lsCheck{Activity 2: ApplyLocalSearch?}
    lsCheck -- Yes --> sel[Select neighbourhood N of choice<br/>e.g., 2-opt for TSP]
    sel --> move[For each constructed solution, perform iterative improvement<br/>Move to better neighbour until local optimum]
    move --> upd
    lsCheck -- No --> upd[Activity 3: UpdatePheromones<br/>on the improved or constructed solution]
    upd --> bsCheck{New best-so-far<br/>solution S_bs found?}
    bsCheck -- Yes --> bsUpdate[Store S_bs and its cost f_S_bs]
    bsCheck -- No --> termCheck
    bsUpdate --> termCheck{Termination condition met?}
    termCheck -- No --> con
    termCheck -- Yes --> hybridEnd([Output S_bs as final solution])

    classDef step fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class con,sel,move,upd,bsUpdate step
    class lsCheck,bsCheck,termCheck dec
    class hybridStart,hybridEnd term
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hybrid ACO + Local Search wins (KTU favourite insight):**
> Pure ACO is a *constructive* metaheuristic — it builds solutions from scratch. Local search is *perturbative* — it polishes existing solutions. Combined, they form a **construct + improve** pipeline that consistently produces state-of-the-art results on TSP, QAP, and VRP benchmarks. This is the standard pattern used in the 2007 IRIDIA ACO algorithms that won multiple international competitions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]
**"Define the ACO metaheuristic. List its three main algorithmic activities."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) metaheuristic** is a *problem-independent framework* proposed by Dorigo, Di Caro, and Gambardella (1999) for designing algorithms to solve **combinatorial optimization problems** by simulating the pheromone-based foraging behaviour of real ants.

The three main algorithmic activities of the ACO metaheuristic are:

1. **ConstructAntSolutions** — A colony of $m$ artificial ants builds candidate solutions by walking on a construction graph, guided by **pheromone trails** $\tau_{ij}$ and **heuristic information** $\eta_{ij}$.
2. **ApplyLocalSearch (Optional)** — The constructed solutions may be improved using a problem-specific local search procedure (e.g., 2-opt for TSP).
3. **UpdatePheromones** — Pheromone values on the edges are **decreased by evaporation** $\rho$ and **increased** on the edges belonging to high-quality solutions.

Different ACO algorithms (AS, ACS, MMAS, S-ACO) differ in how these three activities are implemented. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]
**"What is a combinatorial optimization problem? Give two examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **combinatorial optimization problem (COP)** is an optimization problem in which the set of feasible solutions $S$ is *discrete* (finite or countable) and the goal is to find a solution that **minimises** (or maximises) a cost function $f : S \to \mathbb{R}$ subject to a set of constraints $\Omega$.

Formally, a COP is defined as a triple $P = (S, f, \Omega)$, where:

- $S$ = search space (set of candidate solutions),
- $f$ = objective function to be optimised,
- $\Omega$ = set of constraints.

Most COPs of practical interest (routing, scheduling, assignment) are **NP-hard**, requiring approximate algorithms like ACO.

**Two examples:**

1. **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — find the shortest Hamiltonian cycle visiting all cities.
2. **Quadratic Assignment Problem (QAP)** — assign $n$ facilities to $n$ locations to minimise the flow cost.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester pattern, every Part B question has an *internal choice* — students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are 14-mark questions with two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) metaheuristic** in detail. Describe the three algorithmic activities — *ConstructAntSolutions*, *ApplyLocalSearch*, and *UpdatePheromones* — with suitable diagrams and the relevant mathematical equations for the **random proportional rule** and the **pheromone update**.

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the role of the parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\rho$ in ACO. What happens to the algorithm's behaviour when (i) $\alpha = 0$, (ii) $\beta = 0$, (iii) $\rho = 0$, and (iv) $\rho = 1$?

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

The **ACO metaheuristic** is a high-level, problem-independent algorithmic framework that unifies all ACO variants. It solves combinatorial optimization problems using a colony of artificial ants that build solutions on a **construction graph** $G = (C, L)$ with pheromone values $\tau_{ij}$ and heuristic values $\eta_{ij}$.

**Activity 1 — ConstructAntSolutions:**
For each ant $k$ placed at an initial node, the next node $j$ is chosen from the feasible neighbourhood $N_i^{k}$ using the **Random Proportional (RP) Rule**:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}, \quad j \in N_i^{k}
$$

The ant repeatedly moves until a complete solution $S^{k}$ is formed.

**Activity 2 — ApplyLocalSearch (Optional):**
Each constructed solution may be improved by a local search algorithm (e.g., 2-opt, 3-opt for TSP). The new cost becomes $f_{LS}(S^{k})$.

**Activity 3 — UpdatePheromones:**
Pheromone is updated in two stages — *evaporation* and *deposition*:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}(t)
$$

where $\Delta\tau_{ij}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$ is the total deposit. The loop repeats until a termination condition (e.g., $t \geq t_{\max}$) is met.

(Refer to Section 4.1 flow diagram for the visual representation.) **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

The three ACO parameters control the **exploration vs. exploitation** balance:

| Parameter | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **$\alpha \geq 0$** | Pheromone influence — high $\alpha$ means ants follow trails strongly (exploitation). |
| **$\beta \geq 0$** | Heuristic influence — high $\beta$ means ants greedily prefer the best heuristic edge. |
| **$\rho \in (0,1]$** | Evaporation rate — high $\rho$ means strong forgetting (exploration). |

**Behaviour in limiting cases:**

- **(i) $\alpha = 0$** — The RP rule reduces to a purely **heuristic-driven** distribution. The algorithm degenerates into a multi-start **greedy heuristic** with no learning. No pheromone feedback.
- **(ii) $\beta = 0$** — The rule becomes purely **pheromone-driven**, with $p_{ij} \propto \tau_{ij}^{\alpha}$. The ant is *blind* to the cost $d_{ij}$ — only learned experience matters. This often leads to **stagnation** on early suboptimal paths.
- **(iii) $\rho = 0$** — No evaporation occurs. Pheromone accumulates **monotonically** and the algorithm **prematurely converges** on the first reasonable path (no forgetting).
- **(iv) $\rho = 1$** — All pheromone evaporates instantly at every step. Pheromone becomes a function *only* of the last iteration's best ant — there is no long-term memory. Behaviour is similar to iterated local search with no reinforcement.

A good parameter setting typically uses $\alpha \approx 1$, $\beta \in [2, 5]$, and $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$ for TSP-like problems. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **Simple Ant Colony Optimization (S-ACO)** algorithm with its **pheromone update equation** and the **random proportional transition rule**. Show how the iteration-best ant is selected and how it differs from the original Ant System (AS).

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the role of **local search methods** in modern ACO. Explain 2-opt local search for the Travelling Salesman Problem with a suitable diagram. How does hybridising ACO with local search improve the quality of solutions?

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**S-ACO (Simple Ant Colony Optimization)** is the most elementary ACO variant, designed to isolate the minimum algorithmic machinery needed to demonstrate the **ant-foraging → shortest path** idea.

**S-ACO Pheromone Update:** After all $m$ ants have constructed a tour in iteration $t$, the *iteration-best* ant $k^{\star}$ with the shortest tour length $L^{k^{\star}}$ is selected. **Only this ant** deposits pheromone:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{L^{k^{\star}}} & \text{if edge }(i,j) \in T^{k^{\star}} \\[0.6em]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**S-ACO Random Proportional Rule:** Identical to AS:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}
$$

**Comparison with Ant System (AS):**

| Aspect | **S-ACO** | **Ant System (AS)** |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| Pheromone deposit by | **Only** the iteration-best ant $k^{\star}$ | **All $m$ ants** |
| Deposit formula | $\Delta\tau_{ij} = 1/L^{k^{\star}}$ if $(i,j) \in T^{k^{\star}}$ | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q/L^{k}$ for every ant $k$ |
| Convergence | Faster, more exploitative | Slower, more explorative |
| Local search | None | Often added |
| Use case | Didactic, baseline | Original full version |

The advantage of S-ACO is its **clarity** — the *one-best-ant* rule makes the positive feedback loop visually obvious. Its disadvantage is that good solutions found by non-best ants are wasted, leading to slower exploration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

In modern ACO, the solutions constructed by ants are typically **imperfect** — they contain crossing edges, sub-optimal segments, and minor inefficiencies. **Local search** post-processes each constructed solution to drive it to a *local optimum* in its neighbourhood, dramatically improving final solution quality.

**The 2-opt Local Search for TSP:**

Given a TSP tour, the **2-opt move** removes two non-adjacent edges and reconnects the resulting two path fragments in the opposite way (i.e., one fragment is reversed). This eliminates **edge crossings** which are always sub-optimal in Euclidean TSP.

> **2-opt step (illustrated):**
>
> Old tour: A → B → C → D → E → F → A
>
> Remove edges (B, C) and (E, F); reverse segment C → D → E; reconnect:
>
> New tour: A → B → E → D → C → F → A

The new tour is accepted if its length is shorter. 2-opt is repeated until no improving 2-opt move exists — this is the **2-opt local optimum**.

**Why ACO + Local Search is a winning combination:**

- **ACO provides global exploration** — ants construct diverse solutions that cover the search space.
- **Local search provides local exploitation** — it fine-tunes each solution to a high-quality local optimum.
- **The pheromone is updated on the *improved* solutions** — so the colony learns from *excellent* tours, not mediocre ones.
- The result is a *construct-and-improve* pipeline that consistently matches or beats pure ACO and pure local search on benchmark COPs.

In the literature, this hybridisation is sometimes called **Ant Colony System + 2-opt (ACS-2opt)** or **MAX-MIN Ant System + 3-opt (MMAS-3opt)**, both of which are top performers on TSPLIB benchmarks. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and where the marks get deducted):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the evaporation term** — many students write the pheromone update as $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}$ without the $(1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}$ persistence factor. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing up S-ACO and AS** — S-ACO deposits by *one* ant (best); AS deposits by *all* $m$ ants. Examiners always test this distinction. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting the denominator** in the RP rule. The numerator is $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$, but the *transition probability* requires normalisation over $N_i^{k}$. A common error is to confuse "utility" with "probability". **Lose 2 marks.**
> 4. **Omitting the heuristic value $\eta_{ij}$** — many students write the RP rule with only $\tau_{ij}^{\alpha}$ in the numerator. The presence of $\eta_{ij}^{\beta}$ is what *defines* ACO. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not stating the role of evaporation** — evaporation is *not* a side effect; it is the *forgetting mechanism* that prevents stagnation. Examiners expect this interpretation. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 6. **Confusing the metaheuristic with S-ACO** — the ACO metaheuristic is a *framework* (3 activities); S-ACO is *one* specific algorithm. Examiners will ask "differentiate". **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 7. **Forgetting to close the TSP tour** — in 2-opt explanations, students often leave the new tour as an open path instead of closing it back to the start. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 8. **Not mentioning $Q$** — In AS, the deposit is $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q/L^{k}$ where $Q$ is a constant. Many students drop $Q$ entirely. **Lose 0.5 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

### Foundational Concepts
- [ ] **ACO** is a *population-based, constructive, metaheuristic* for **combinatorial optimization**, inspired by real-ant pheromone trail behaviour.
- [ ] **Combinatorial Optimization Problem** is formally the triple $P = (S, f, \Omega)$; most practical COPs are **NP-hard**.
- [ ] **Metaheuristic** is a problem-independent *high-level framework* that orchestrates subordinate heuristics.

### Algorithms and Equations
- [ ] **S-ACO** is the *minimal* ACO: only the iteration-best ant deposits pheromone.
- [ ] **Ant System (AS)** is the *original* ACO: all $m$ ants deposit.
- [ ] **ACS, MMAS, AS-rank, Elitist-AS** are advanced variants that refine the deposit rule and the choice rule.
- [ ] **Master update equation**: $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}(t)$.
- [ ] **Random Proportional rule**: $p_{ij}^{k} = [\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta} / \sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}$.
- [ ] **S-ACO deposit**: $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} = 1/L^{k^{\star}}$ on edges of the best tour.
- [ ] **AS deposit**: $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q/L^{k}$ summed over all $m$ ants.

### Parameters
- [ ] $\alpha$ controls **pheromone weight** (exploitation of learned experience).
- [ ] $\beta$ controls **heuristic weight** (greedy preference).
- [ ] $\rho$ controls **evaporation rate** (forgetting / exploration).
- [ ] Typical values: $\alpha \approx 1$, $\beta \in [2,5]$, $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$, $Q \approx 1$ or $Q = L_{nn}$ (nearest-neighbour tour length).

### ACO Metaheuristic Structure (3 activities)
- [ ] **Activity 1: ConstructAntSolutions** — ants build solutions using RP rule.
- [ ] **Activity 2: ApplyLocalSearch (Optional)** — 2-opt, 3-opt, or specialised moves.
- [ ] **Activity 3: UpdatePheromones** — evaporate, then deposit on best edges.

### Local Search
- [ ] **2-opt** removes two edges and reverses a sub-tour; eliminates crossings in Euclidean TSP.
- [ ] **3-opt** generalises to three edges; more powerful, more expensive.
- [ ] **Lin–Kernighan (LK)** is a variable-opt depth search; the gold standard for TSP local search.
- [ ] **Hybrid ACO + local search** = *construct + improve*; pheromone is updated on the *improved* solutions.

### Scope and Applications
- [ ] ACO solves **routing, assignment, scheduling, bioinformatics, telecommunications, and data mining** problems.
- [ ] It is *especially* suited for **dynamic, stochastic, or graph-based** problems.
- [ ] Real-world success stories: **AntNet for network routing**, **ACO for train scheduling**, **QAP in hospital layout planning**.

### Mnemonic for the Three Activities
- [ ] **C**onstruct, **L**ocal-search, **U**pdate → **"CLU"** (think "clue trail" — the ants leave a CLU to follow).

> **Final tip:** Always state the **biological inspiration** first, then the **mathematical abstraction**, then the **algorithmic step**. Examiners reward answers that follow this *biology → math → algorithm* structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swarm Robotics: Foraging for Food, Clustering of Objects, Collective Prey Retrieval, Scope of Swarm Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT414 - NATURE INSPIRED COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Swarm Robotics: Foraging for Food, Clustering of Objects, Collective Prey Retrieval, Scope of Swarm Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Swarm Robotics — Formal Definition

**Swarm Robotics** is the study of how a large number of relatively simple, physically embodied agents can be designed to collectively accomplish tasks that are beyond the capabilities of a single agent, using only **local sensing**, **local communication**, and **decentralized control** — without any global supervisor or centralized planner.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, swarm robotics is positioned as the physical, robotic counterpart of **swarm intelligence**. While swarm intelligence (PSO, ACO, ABC) is largely *computational* and *abstract*, swarm robotics binds the same collective principles to **real-world embodied hardware** — motors, wheels, IR sensors, and RF modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim, board-standard):**
> *"Swarm Robotics is an approach to the coordination of large numbers of relatively simple robots, which takes its inspiration from the emergent collective behavior of social insects and other animal societies."*

The five pillars that govern the design of any swarm robotic system (per KTU Module 3 learning outcomes) are:

| # | Pillar | Meaning |
|---|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Robots are autonomous** | Each robot has its own processor, sensors, actuators, and power. |
| 2 | **Robots are few in capabilities** | Each robot is simple, cheap, and expendable (e.g., Kilobot, E-puck). |
| 3 | **Robots are many in number** | Typically **10 to 1000+** units cooperating simultaneously. |
| 4 | **Only local sensing & communication** | No global GPS, no shared map, no central controller. |
| 5 | **Emergent collective behavior** | Group-level intelligence **emerges** from individual rules. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction for KTU exam:**
> - **Swarm Intelligence** → software/algorithmic (e.g., PSO, ABC).
> - **Swarm Robotics** → hardware/embodied realization of the same principles.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Anthill on Wheels"

Imagine an **anthill** with **no manager ant**, **no blueprints**, and **no radio**. Yet thousands of ants coordinate to:
- **Find food** scattered randomly on the ground,
- **Sort dead ants** into clean piles (cemetery behaviour),
- **Cooperate to carry** a large beetle back to the nest.

Replace each ant with a **small wheeled robot (Kilobot-class)**, give it IR sensors instead of antennae, and you have a **swarm robotic system**. Each robot runs a few simple "rules" and the **swarm as a whole** solves problems that no single robot could.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Hook (use this in viva):** Swarm robotics is the engineering translation of "**many dumb things behaving smart collectively**" — the intelligence lives in the **group**, not the **individual**.

---

## 1.3 Why Swarm Robotics? — Motivating Advantages

| Property | Explanation |
|----------|-------------|
| **Robustness** | No single point of failure — losing 30 % of robots does not collapse the mission. |
| **Flexibility** | Same swarm can be re-tasked by simply changing the individual rules. |
| **Scalability** | Performance degrades gracefully when robot count varies from 10 to 10000. |
| **Decentralization** | No communication backbone, no central server required. |
| **Cost** | Each unit is cheap; total system cost can be lower than one sophisticated robot. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "swarm robotics is just multi-robot systems." This is **wrong**. Multi-robot systems may use centralized coordination; **swarm robotics mandates decentralized, local, emergent behavior**.

---

## 1.4 Sub-topics in This Module

This KTU module covers **four canonical swarm behaviours**, all directly observed in nature and replicated in robots:

1. **Foraging for Food** (e.g., search, retrieval, nest return).
2. **Clustering of Objects** (e.g., sorting, aggregation, cemetery formation).
3. **Collective Prey Retrieval** (e.g., cooperative transport of heavy items).
4. **Scope of Swarm Robotics** (industrial, agricultural, defence, medical, search-and-rescue).

Each of these is treated in detail in the sections that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Foraging for Food

### 2.1.1 Definition
**Foraging** is the process by which a swarm of robots **searches**, **detects**, **picks up**, and **transports** items (food/objects) from the environment back to a designated location (nest/home), analogous to ant foraging behaviour.

### 2.1.2 Biological Inspiration
Real ant colonies solve foraging through **stigmergy** — indirect communication via the environment. Ants deposit **pheromones** on the path; others probabilistically follow stronger trails, which evaporate over time.

### 2.1.3 Robot Foraging — Algorithmic Steps (Individual Rule Set)

For each robot `Rᵢ` at time `t`:

1. **Wander / Search Phase** — Execute a random or biased walk until a "food item" is detected via onboard sensors.
2. **Pick-up Decision** — If item detected, grasp it (or push it).
3. **Nest Homing** — Travel toward the nest using a gradient, beacon, or visual marker.
4. **Drop Phase** — Release the item inside the nest zone.
5. **Return to Search** — Resume the wandering state.
6. **Optional Pheromone Marking** — Drop a virtual "pheromone" (RFID tag, light trail, Wi-Fi heat-map, or virtual map broadcast).

### 2.1.4 States of a Foraging Robot (Finite State Machine)

$$
S = \{ S_{search},\; S_{pickup},\; S_{homing},\; S_{drop},\; S_{return} \}
$$

Transition logic (simplified):

$$
R_i(t+1) = \begin{cases}
S_{homing} & \text{if } sensor_{food} = true \\
S_{search} & \text{if } (state = S_{return}) \land (at\;nest) \\
S_{drop} & \text{if } (state = S_{homing}) \land (at\;nest)
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.1.5 Performance Metrics (KTU frequently asked)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|--------|--------|------------|
| **Foraging Rate** | `F` | Items delivered to nest per unit time. |
| **Success Rate** | `η` | (Items delivered) / (Items picked up) × 100 %. |
| **Interference Rate** | `I` | Collisions / total moves. |
| **Energy per Task** | `E/T` | Battery consumed per item delivered. |
| **Scalability Index** | `Sₙ` | Foraging rate with n robots / with 1 robot. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-standard formula for swarm foraging efficiency:**
> $$ \eta_{swarm} = \frac{\text{Items collected by swarm per unit time}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Items theoretically collectable by } R_i} $$
> A well-designed swarm achieves `ηₛᵥₐᵣₘ → 1` as `N` increases (up to physical limits).

### 2.1.6 Challenges in Robot Foraging
- **Interference** — robots blocking each other in dense areas.
- **Pheromone Implementation** — real pheromones are chemical; in robots, designers use *virtual pheromones* (light, RFID, radio strength).
- **Deadlock** at the nest during high traffic.
- **Battery limitation** forces recharging/docking strategies.

---

## 2.2 Clustering of Objects

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Clustering** (or **Aggregation**) is the behaviour in which a swarm of robots **gathers, sorts, or piles** scattered objects into compact groups, mimicking the cemetery/pile-building behaviour of ants and bees.

### 2.2.2 Biological Inspiration
- **Ant cemeteries** — Dead ants are gathered into compact piles by worker ants.
- **Bee clustering** — Bees form clusters to regulate hive temperature.
- **Embryogenesis** — Cells aggregate based on differential adhesion.

### 2.2.3 Algorithmic Rules — The CORGI / Stick-Pulling Family

A canonical clustering algorithm is **Chen & Söffker (2008) CEM** or the simpler probabilistic clustering:

For each robot `Rᵢ`:

$$
P_{pick} = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } k \leq 1 \text{ (item alone, always pick)} \\
1 - \left(\dfrac{k}{k + \alpha + \beta\, t_{since}}\right)^{2} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- `k` = number of neighbouring items detected.
- `t_since` = time since last pick.
- `α, β` = tunable threshold parameters.

This produces **emergent clusters** because picking is more likely for isolated items and less likely near already-formed piles.

### 2.2.4 Clustering Phases (KTU High-Yield)

| Phase | Behaviour | Time Scale |
|-------|-----------|------------|
| **Diffusion** | Robots and items spread out. | 0–T1 |
| **Random Aggregation** | Initial small piles form by chance. | T1–T2 |
| **Cluster Growth** | Probabilistic rule accelerates pile growth. | T2–T3 |
| **Steady State** | Cluster sizes stabilize, no further growth. | T3–∞ |

### 2.2.5 Mathematical Model (Cluster Size Distribution)

The number of clusters of size `n` at time `t` approximately follows:

$$
\dfrac{dC_n}{dt} = J_{n-1} - J_n
$$

where `Jₙ` is the net flux of items leaving clusters of size `n`. Closed-form solutions are intractable, but the **mean cluster size** evolves as:

$$
\langle n(t) \rangle = \langle n(0) \rangle \, e^{\lambda t}
$$

with growth rate `λ` depending on robot count, item density, and `α, β`.

---

## 2.3 Collective Prey Retrieval (Cooperative Transport)

### 2.3.1 Definition
**Collective Prey Retrieval** is the cooperative behaviour in which **multiple robots simultaneously push, pull, or carry** an object (prey) that is too large or heavy for a single robot to move, analogous to ants retrieving large prey items.

### 2.3.2 Biological Inspiration
- Ants cooperate to drag beetles, grasshoppers, and large prey.
- The behaviour requires **no leader** — ants estimate the prey's size via a few rules and self-allocate.

### 2.3.3 The Three Key Problems

| Problem | Description | Biological Example |
|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Detection** | Robots must collectively recognize an item as "too large". | Ants sense weight by tug-of-war. |
| **Recruitment** | Robots must signal others to join. | Ants emit pheromone upon finding prey. |
| **Coordination** | All robots must pull/push in a coherent direction. | Ants align by sensing partner forces. |

### 2.3.4 Mathematical Model — Cooperative Pushing

Consider `N` robots pushing a single object along the x-axis with a resultant force:

$$
F_{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cos\theta_i - \mu m g
$$

Where:
- `fᵢ` = individual pushing force of robot `i`.
- `θᵢ` = angle of force relative to desired direction.
- `μ` = friction coefficient.
- `m` = mass of object.
- `g` = gravitational acceleration.

The object moves iff:

$$
F_{net} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cos\theta_i > \mu m g
$$

**Minimum cooperative size** (critical KTU formula):

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\mu m g}{\langle f \cos\theta \rangle} \right\rceil
$$

### 2.3.5 Klaber's Prey-Retrieval Algorithm (KTU Short Note)

A canonical implementation by **Krieger & Billeter (2000)** uses:
1. Robots wander.
2. On contact with object, push it.
3. If object doesn't move (force threshold not met), **stop and emit a virtual recruitment signal**.
4. Recruited robots approach and add their force.
5. Once moving, robots continuously **align direction** with the global motion vector.

---

## 2.4 Scope of Swarm Robotics

### 2.4.1 Real-World Application Domains

| Domain | Task | Why Swarm? |
|--------|------|------------|
| **Agriculture** | Distributed seeding, weeding, harvesting, crop monitoring. | Cheap, covers large farms. |
| **Search and Rescue** | Rubble exploration, victim localization. | Robust to robot loss in hostile terrain. |
| **Military / Defence** | Reconnaissance, mine clearance, surveillance. | Expendable, no single point of failure. |
| **Medical / Micro** | Targeted drug delivery, nanobots in bloodstream. | Access to confined spaces. |
| **Industrial Cleaning** | Warehouse floor sweeping, oil-spill cleanup. | Parallelism reduces time. |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Forest-fire sensing, marine pollution tracking. | Wide-area coverage, energy efficiency. |
| **Disaster Response** | Volcanic/hazardous zone exploration. | Replace humans in dangerous areas. |
| **Space Exploration** | Planetary surface mapping (NASA's ANTS concept). | No astronaut dependency. |

### 2.4.2 Famous Swarm Robotic Platforms (Board Knowledge)

| Robot | Source | Robot Count | Application |
|-------|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Kilobot** | Harvard | 1024 | Self-assembly, collective motion. |
| **E-puck** | EPFL | Hundreds | Education, research. |
| **Alice** | EPFL | Many | Collective inspection. |
| **s-bot** | EPFL | Many | Cooperative transport. |
| **SwarmBot** | Georgia Tech | 100s | Swarm communication. |
| **Droplet / Zooids** | Stanford | 100s | Haptic displays. |

### 2.4.3 Future Scope (KTU Essay-Level Points)
- **Medical nanobots** — diagnosis and surgery at cellular level.
- **Smart agriculture** — precision farming with thousands of tiny rovers.
- **Smart cities** — distributed sensing for pollution, traffic, energy.
- **Interplanetary missions** — NASA ANTS (Autonomous Nano-Technology Swarm) concept for asteroid belt.
- **Underwater swarm** — ocean-floor mapping and biodiversity.

### 2.4.4 Current Limitations
- **Communication constraints** at scale (bandwidth, range).
- **Localization** without GPS indoors.
- **Energy** — battery life limits mission duration.
- **Testing** — hard to simulate 1000+ robots with high fidelity.
- **Safety and ethical concerns** for nanobots in medicine.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas, symbols, and definitions, ready for last-night revision:**

| Concept | Key Formula / Definition | KTU Use |
|---------|--------------------------|---------|
| Swarm definition | N autonomous agents, local sensing, emergent behaviour | 2-mark |
| Foraging efficiency | `ηₛᵥₐᵣₘ = items collected per unit time / theoretical max` | 3-mark |
| Foraging states | `S = {Search, Pickup, Homing, Drop, Return}` | 3-mark |
| Pheromone evaporation | `τ(t) = τ₀ · e^{-k t}` (analogue) | 3-mark |
| Mean cluster size | `⟨n(t)⟩ = ⟨n(0)⟩ e^{λt}` | 3-mark |
| Probabilistic pick | `P_pick = 1 − (k / (k + α + β t_since))²` | 5-mark |
| Cooperative pushing | `F_net = Σ fᵢ cosθᵢ − μ m g` | 5-mark |
| Min robots for transport | `N_min = ⌈μ m g / ⟨f cosθ⟩⌉` | 5-mark |
| Properties of swarm | Robustness, Flexibility, Scalability, Decentralization | 2-mark |
| Virtual pheromone methods | Light trail, RFID, radio RSSI, bitmap broadcast | 2-mark |
| Stigmergy | Indirect coordination via environment modification | 2-mark |
| Emergence | Global behaviour from local interaction rules | 2-mark |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU 2-Marker:** **"R-F-S-D-E"** — **R**obust, **F**lexible, **S**calable, **D**ecentralized, **E**mergent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Foraging Efficiency Computation

**Problem (KTU-typical 7-mark):**
A swarm of **N = 20** robots forages in a `10 m × 10 m` arena. Each robot finds, on average, **3 food items per minute** when working alone. However, due to inter-robot interference, the actual swarm collection rate is `R_swarm = 45 items/min`. Compute the **swarm foraging efficiency** `ηₛᵥₐᵣₘ`.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the theoretical maximum.**

If robots worked perfectly independently with no interference, the total theoretical collection rate is:

$$
R_{theory} = N \times R_{single} = 20 \times 3 = 60 \;\; \text{items/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the actual rate.**

Given: `R_swarm = 45 items/min`.

**Step 3 — Apply the efficiency formula.**

$$
\eta_{swarm} = \frac{R_{swarm}}{R_{theory}} = \frac{45}{60}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute and convert to percentage.**

$$
\eta_{swarm} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

The swarm achieves 75 % of the theoretical ideal, which is **typical for moderate-density swarms**; with better collision avoidance, it could approach 90 %+.

> **Answer:** `ηₛᵥₐᵣₘ = 0.75` (or **75 %**).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mean Cluster Size Evolution

**Problem:**
A clustering swarm has initial mean cluster size `⟨n(0)⟩ = 2`. The growth rate is `λ = 0.4` per minute. Find the mean cluster size after `t = 5` minutes.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the cluster-growth law.**

$$
\langle n(t) \rangle = \langle n(0) \rangle \, e^{\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\langle n(5) \rangle = 2 \cdot e^{0.4 \times 5} = 2 \cdot e^{2.0}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential.**

$$
e^{2.0} \approx 7.389
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$
\langle n(5) \rangle = 2 \times 7.389 = 14.778
$$

**Step 5 — Round off.**

$$
\langle n(5) \rangle \approx 15 \text{ items per cluster}
$$

> **Answer:** After 5 minutes, average cluster size ≈ **15 items**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cooperative Transport Force Balance

**Problem:**
**6 robots** each capable of pushing with `f = 8 N` at a mean alignment angle `θ = 20°` push a victim/prey object of mass `m = 5 kg` lying on a surface with friction coefficient `μ = 0.3`. Take `g = 9.81 m/s²`. Determine if the object moves, and if so, find the net force.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert angle to radians (for clarity).**

$$
\theta = 20° = 20 \times \frac{\pi}{180} \approx 0.349 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cosine factor.**

$$
\cos 20° \approx 0.9397
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sum of effective horizontal forces.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} f_i \cos\theta_i = 6 \times 8 \times 0.9397 = 48 \times 0.9397
$$

$$
\sum f_i \cos\theta_i = 45.10 \;\; N
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the friction force.**

$$
F_{friction} = \mu m g = 0.3 \times 5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \;\; N
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the net force.**

$$
F_{net} = 45.10 - 14.715 = 30.385 \;\; N
$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**

Since `F_net > 0` (positive), the object **will move** with a net driving force of approximately `30.4 N`.

> **Answer:** `F_net ≈ +30.4 N`; the object **moves**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Minimum Robots for Transport

**Problem:**
Same scenario as above, but this time `m = 50 kg` and `μ = 0.4`. Find the minimum number of robots required to move the object.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Friction force.**

$$
F_{friction} = \mu m g = 0.4 \times 50 \times 9.81 = 196.2 \;\; N
$$

**Step 2 — Effective force per robot (assumed identical alignment).**

$$
\langle f \cos\theta \rangle = 8 \times \cos 20° = 8 \times 0.9397 = 7.518 \;\; N
$$

**Step 3 — Apply minimum-cooperative formula.**

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{F_{friction}}{\langle f \cos\theta \rangle} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{196.2}{7.518} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{196.2}{7.518} \approx 26.10
$$

**Step 5 — Take ceiling.**

$$
N_{min} = 27 \text{ robots}
$$

> **Answer:** At least **27 robots** are needed.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Probabilistic Pick Decision

**Problem:**
A robot observes `k = 3` neighbouring items. Tuning parameters are `α = 1.5` and `β = 0.2`. Time since last pick `t_since = 4 s`. Compute `P_pick`.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
k + \alpha + \beta \, t_{since} = 3 + 1.5 + 0.2 \times 4 = 3 + 1.5 + 0.8 = 5.3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inner ratio.**

$$
\frac{k}{k + \alpha + \beta t_{since}} = \frac{3}{5.3} \approx 0.5660
$$

**Step 3 — Square it.**

$$
\left(0.5660\right)^2 \approx 0.3204
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract from 1.**

$$
P_{pick} = 1 - 0.3204 = 0.6796
$$

> **Answer:** `P_pick ≈ 0.68` (i.e., 68 % chance the robot picks up the item).

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Board-Diagram)

| Behaviour | Triggering Cue | Individual Rule | Emergent Group Result |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Foraging | Sensor detects food | Pick → home → drop | Pheromone trail optimization |
| Clustering | Item density `k` | Probabilistic pick/deposit | Sorted piles / clusters |
| Prey Retrieval | Failed single-robot push | Recruit + align | Coordinated transport |
| Aggregation (cold) | Temperature sensor | Move toward dense swarm | Cluster formation |
| Dispersion | Crowding sensor | Avoid neighbours | Uniform spreading |

---

## 3.7 Transition from Simple Rules to Complex Behaviour — The "How"

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark questions, the board expects you to link:**
> **(a) the biological inspiration, (b) the individual rule, (c) the mathematical model, (d) the emergent outcome, (e) a real robot example.**

The 5-link chain (the "Emergence Chain"):

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Biology} \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{Local Rule} \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{Math Model} \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{Emergent Behaviour} \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{Robot Validation} \\
&\textit{(ant/dead)}\; \quad \textit{(prob. pick)}\; \quad \textit{(P = ...)}\; \quad \textit{(cluster)}\; \quad \textit{(Kilobot demo)}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define swarm robotics. List any four key properties of a swarm robotic system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Swarm robotics is the study of designing and coordinating large numbers of relatively simple, autonomous robots that use **only local sensing, local communication, and decentralized control** to achieve a **collective goal through emergent behaviour**, with no central supervisor.

**Four Key Properties:**
1. **Robustness** — system continues despite individual robot failure.
2. **Scalability** — performance gracefully changes with team size.
3. **Flexibility** — same swarm can solve varied tasks by rule change.
4. **Decentralization** — no central controller; decisions made locally.

> **Self-Assessment Cue:** A complete answer = **1-line definition + 4 properties in bold** (3 marks).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is stigmergy? How is it implemented in robot foraging?**

**Model Answer:**

**Stigmergy** is a form of **indirect coordination** between agents in which the actions of one agent modify the environment, which in turn influences the behaviour of other agents. It was first described by **Pierre-Paul Grassé (1959)** to explain termite nest construction.

**In robot foraging**, stigmergy is implemented via **virtual pheromones** because robots cannot deposit real chemicals. Common methods include:

| Method | Mechanism |
|--------|-----------|
| **Light trails** | Robots project coloured light on the floor. |
| **RFID tagging** | Each item carries an RFID tag with "strength" value. |
| **Radio RSSI heatmap** | Robots broadcast a digital pheromone intensity grid. |
| **Shared bitmap** | Each robot maintains a local map updated via Wi-Fi. |
| **Stigmergic markers** | Physical markers dropped and picked up by robots. |

> **Self-Assessment Cue:** Mention **Grassé** + at least **2 virtual pheromone methods** for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Provided

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Module 3, 14 Marks]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **foraging behaviour** in swarm robotics with reference to its biological inspiration from ants. Describe the individual robot rules and the state machine.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A swarm of **30 robots** forages in a `20 m × 20 m` arena. Each robot independently finds **5 items/min**. Due to inter-robot collisions, the actual swarm rate is `120 items/min`. Compute the **foraging efficiency** and **scalability index**.

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Biological Inspiration (2 marks):**
Ant colonies perform foraging using **pheromone-mediated stigmergy**. An ant that finds food returns to the nest laying down a chemical trail; subsequent ants follow trails with stronger pheromone concentration, reinforcing the path. Pheromones evaporate over time, so shorter paths (which get reinforced faster) eventually dominate.

**Step 2 — Translation to Robots (2 marks):**
In robot swarms, real pheromones are replaced by **virtual pheromones** (light trails, RFID, radio maps). Each robot runs a simple **individual rule set** and the global trail optimization **emerges** from the collective behaviour.

**Step 3 — Individual Robot State Machine (2 marks):**

$$
S_{forager} = \{ S_{search},\; S_{pickup},\; S_{homing},\; S_{drop},\; S_{return} \}
$$

Transitions:
- `S_search → S_pickup` when `sensor_food = TRUE`.
- `S_pickup → S_homing` after grasp.
- `S_homing → S_drop` on reaching nest.
- `S_drop → S_return` after release.

**Step 4 — Algorithmic Pseudocode Style Description (1 mark):**
Loop forever:
- If wandering and no item → random walk.
- If item detected → pick and home.
- If at nest → drop and return.
- (Optional) Deposit virtual pheromone after successful delivery.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute theoretical max rate (2 marks):**
$$
R_{theory} = N \times R_{single} = 30 \times 5 = 150 \;\; \text{items/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute efficiency (2 marks):**
$$
\eta_{swarm} = \frac{R_{swarm}}{R_{theory}} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute scalability index (2 marks):**
$$
S_n = \frac{R_{swarm}}{R_{single}} = \frac{120}{5} = 24
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret (1 mark):**
- Efficiency is **80 %** — typical of moderately dense swarms.
- Scalability index **24** (less than N = 30) shows the swarm is *sub-linear* due to interference; ideal would be 30.

> **Final Answers:** `η = 0.80`, `Sₙ = 24`.

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Module 3, 14 Marks]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **clustering behaviour** in swarm robotics. Derive the **mean cluster size growth equation** `⟨n(t)⟩ = ⟨n(0)⟩ e^{λt}` and state the assumptions.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Eight robots, each capable of pushing **10 N** at a mean alignment angle of **30°**, are to transport an object of **mass 20 kg** with surface friction coefficient **μ = 0.4**. Take **g = 9.81 m/s²`. Determine whether 8 robots are sufficient; if not, compute the **minimum number required**.

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define clustering (2 marks):**
Clustering (or aggregation) is the **swarm behaviour in which robots gather, sort, or pile items** in the environment into compact groups, mimicking **ant cemetery** or **embryonic cell sorting** behaviour.

**Step 2 — Probabilistic individual rule (2 marks):**
$$
P_{pick} = 1 - \left(\frac{k}{k + \alpha + \beta t_{since}}\right)^2
$$
Isolated items are picked with high probability; items in dense clusters are left alone → emergent sorting.

**Step 3 — Derive the mean cluster size equation (2 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
- Items are picked at average rate `λ` per item.
- Cluster size grows **exponentially** during the early phase.
- No item destruction; no robots leaving the area.

The differential equation for the expected cluster size is:

$$
\frac{d\langle n \rangle}{dt} = \lambda \langle n \rangle
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\frac{d\langle n \rangle}{\langle n \rangle} = \lambda \, dt
$$

Integrating from `0` to `t`:

$$
\int_{\langle n(0) \rangle}^{\langle n(t) \rangle} \frac{dn}{n} = \lambda \int_0^t dt
$$

$$
\ln \langle n(t) \rangle - \ln \langle n(0) \rangle = \lambda t
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
\boxed{\langle n(t) \rangle = \langle n(0) \rangle \, e^{\lambda t}}
$$

**Step 4 — Phase summary (1 mark):**
Diffusion → Random Aggregation → Cluster Growth → Steady State.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute friction force (2 marks):**
$$
F_{friction} = \mu m g = 0.4 \times 20 \times 9.81 = 78.48 \;\; N
$$

**Step 2 — Effective force per robot (2 marks):**
$$
f_{eff} = 10 \times \cos 30° = 10 \times 0.8660 = 8.660 \;\; N
$$

**Step 3 — Total available force from 8 robots (1 mark):**
$$
F_{available} = 8 \times 8.660 = 69.28 \;\; N
$$

**Step 4 — Compare (1 mark):**
$$
F_{available} = 69.28 \, N \; < \; F_{friction} = 78.48 \, N
$$
Therefore **8 robots are NOT sufficient**.

**Step 5 — Compute minimum N (1 mark):**
$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{78.48}{8.660} \right\rceil = \lceil 9.06 \rceil = 10
$$

> **Final Answer:** 8 robots are **insufficient**; minimum **10 robots** are required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing swarm robotics with multi-robot systems** — swarm robotics mandates **local-only** coordination. If you write "centralized planner", you'll lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to define "emergence"** — board examiners specifically look for the word *emergent* in any 14-mark answer.
> 3. **Skipping the biological link** — every swarm behaviour MUST be tied to its natural inspiration (ants, bees, fish, birds).
> 4. **Mathematical errors in force/cluster equations** — make sure units (N, kg, m/s²) are carried; a missing `g` costs full marks in derivation.
> 5. **Not drawing the FSM or block diagram** — board answer sheets have a **drawing section**; expect -2 marks if absent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this box and stick it on your wall the night before the exam.**

### ✅ Definitions
- [ ] Swarm robotics = many simple, local, decentralized, emergent robots.
- [ ] Stigmergy = indirect coordination via environment modification.
- [ ] Emergence = global pattern from local interactions.
- [ ] Virtual pheromone = non-chemical trail used by robots.
- [ ] Cooperative transport = multi-robot joint push/pull/carry.

### ✅ Properties of a Swarm (R-F-S-D-E mnemonic)
- [ ] **R**obust, **F**lexible, **S**calable, **D**ecentralized, **E**mergent.

### ✅ Behaviour — Math Link
- [ ] Foraging: `η = R_swarm / R_theory`.
- [ ] Clustering: `⟨n(t)⟩ = ⟨n(0)⟩ e^{λt}`.
- [ ] Picking: `P_pick = 1 − (k / (k + α + β t))²`.
- [ ] Transport: `F_net = Σ fᵢ cosθᵢ − μ m g`.
- [ ] Min robots: `N_min = ⌈μ m g / ⟨f cosθ⟩⌉`.

### ✅ Foraging FSM
- [ ] `Search → Pickup → Homing → Drop → Return → Search`.

### ✅ Clustering Phases
- [ ] Diffusion → Aggregation → Growth → Steady State.

### ✅ Prey Retrieval — 3 Key Problems
- [ ] Detection, Recruitment, Coordination.

### ✅ Application Domains
- [ ] Agriculture, Search and Rescue, Medical, Defence, Industrial, Environmental, Space.

### ✅ Famous Robots (Name ONE if asked)
- [ ] Kilobot (Harvard), E-puck (EPFL), s-bot (EPFL), SwarmBot (Georgia Tech), Alice (EPFL).

### ✅ Examples of Virtual Pheromones
- [ ] Light trails, RFID, Radio RSSI, Shared bitmap, Physical markers.

### ✅ Common KTU Buzzwords
- [ ] "**Local sensing, no global planner**"
- [ ] "**Emergent behaviour**"
- [ ] "**Stigmergic communication**"
- [ ] "**Cost-effective, fault-tolerant**"
- [ ] "**Inspiration from ant/bee colonies**"

> **Final Note:** In a 14-mark answer, follow the **Emergence Chain** (Biology → Rule → Model → Emergence → Robot Validation) for guaranteed full marks. Combine the **mathematical derivation** with the **state diagram** in every long answer.
