topics.title: Social Adaptation of Knowledge: Particle Swarm, Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Particle Swarms for Dynamic Optimization Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT414 - NATURE INSPIRED COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Social Adaptation of Knowledge: Particle Swarm, Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Particle Swarms for Dynamic Optimization Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Social Adaptation of Knowledge?

**Social Adaptation of Knowledge** is a population-based meta-heuristic paradigm in which candidate solutions (called *particles*) learn not only from their own past experience but also from the experiences of their neighbors and the group as a whole. Each individual continuously updates its own belief (position) by balancing three competing forces: **inertia (self-experience)**, **cognition (personal best)**, and **social influence (group best)**.

In nature, social adaptation is observed in bird flocks, fish schools, insect swarms, and human societies — where the collective intelligence of the group far exceeds the intelligence of any individual. Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is the canonical computational realization of this principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECAT414 - Module 3):**
> *"Social Adaptation of Knowledge refers to the mechanism by which a population of agents (particles) iteratively refine their search trajectories through self-learning and peer learning, mimicking the emergent collective intelligence observed in natural swarms."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍱

Imagine a **flock of birds searching for food in a vast field** where food sources keep shifting (this represents the *objective function* in a dynamic environment).

- Each bird is a **particle** in the search space.
- Each bird has its own memory of the best food spot it has ever found — its **pbest** (personal best).
- Every bird knows the best food spot found by *any* bird in the flock — the **gbest** (global best).
- Each bird adjusts its flight direction and speed (its **velocity**) by combining:
  1. Its current momentum (where it was heading anyway) → **inertia**
  2. A pull toward its own best memory → **cognitive component**
  3. A pull toward the flock's best-known spot → **social component**

Just as a flock converges on food faster than a lone bird could, the swarm converges on the optimum faster than a solitary search.

> [!NOTE]
> **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** is a population-based stochastic optimization technique inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling, formalized by **James Kennedy and Russell Eberhart in 1995**.

## 1.3 Terminology Snapshot

| KTU Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Swarm | $S$ | The complete population of particles |
| Particle | $i$ | A single candidate solution |
| Position vector | $\vec{x}_i$ | Current location in the search space |
| Velocity vector | $\vec{v}_i$ | Rate of change of position |
| Personal best | $\vec{p}_i$ | Best position the particle has ever visited |
| Global best | $\vec{g}$ | Best position discovered by the entire swarm |
| Inertia weight | $w$ | Controls the influence of previous velocity |
| Cognitive coefficient | $c_1$ | Weight of self-experience |
| Social coefficient | $c_2$ | Weight of group experience |
| Random vectors | $\vec{r}_1, \vec{r}_2$ | Uniform random numbers in $[0,1]^d$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metric used in literature:** $c_1 + c_2 \le 4.0$ (Clerc & Kennedy convergence criterion) and $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ for stable swarm behavior.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Forces Acting on Every Particle

The movement of each particle in PSO is governed by three competing social-cognitive forces, all of which operate simultaneously in **d-dimensional search space**:

### 🟥 Force 1: Inertia (Self-Momentum)
$$\vec{v}_i^{inertia}(t+1) = w \cdot \vec{v}_i(t)$$

**Why it exists:** A bird that was flying north at high speed does not stop abruptly. The **inertia weight $w$** lets the particle continue along its current trajectory, preserving the momentum of the search. It is the *conservative* force.

- If $w$ is too large → particles overshoot the optimum (exploration).
- If $w$ is too small → particles stagnate, lose diversity (exploitation).
- A common KTU-style strategy is **linearly decreasing inertia** (Shi & Eberhart, 1998):

$$w(t) = w_{max} - \frac{(w_{max} - w_{min}) \cdot t}{T_{max}}$$

where $T_{max}$ is the maximum number of iterations. Typical values: $w_{max} = 0.9$, $w_{min} = 0.4$.

### 🟨 Force 2: Cognitive Component (Personal Memory)
$$\vec{v}_i^{cog}(t+1) = c_1 \cdot \vec{r}_1 \odot \left( \vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t) \right)$$

**Why it exists:** Every individual has a private memory of "the best place *I* ever found." This represents **narcissistic / introspective learning**. The coefficient $c_1$ scales the strength of self-confidence. $\vec{r}_1$ is a vector of uniformly distributed random numbers in $[0,1]$, drawn independently for each dimension $d$, ensuring stochasticity.

### 🟦 Force 3: Social Component (Peer Influence)
$$\vec{v}_i^{soc}(t+1) = c_2 \cdot \vec{r}_2 \odot \left( \vec{g}(t) - \vec{x}_i(t) \right)$$

**Why it exists:** The particle is also pulled toward the best position discovered by the swarm (or its neighborhood). This is **cultural / collaborative learning**. The coefficient $c_2$ scales the strength of conformity. The symbol $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) multiplication.

## 2.2 The Canonical PSO Update Equations

The complete PSO update mechanism combines all three forces:

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w \cdot \vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 \cdot \vec{r}_1 \odot \left( \vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t) \right) + c_2 \cdot \vec{r}_2 \odot \left( \vec{g}(t) - \vec{x}_i(t) \right)
$$

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

where $t$ is the current iteration, $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$ is the particle index, and $N$ is the swarm size.

## 2.3 Algorithm Execution Pipeline

The PSO algorithm follows a tight, deterministic inner loop with one stochastic perturbation per particle per dimension:

1. **Initialization Phase** ($t = 0$):
   - Initialize each particle's position $\vec{x}_i(0) \sim U(\vec{x}_{min}, \vec{x}_{max})$.
   - Initialize each particle's velocity $\vec{v}_i(0) \sim U(-V_{max}, V_{max})$.
   - Set $\vec{p}_i(0) = \vec{x}_i(0)$.
   - Set $\vec{g}(0) = \arg\min_{i} f(\vec{x}_i(0))$.

2. **Iterative Update Phase** (for $t = 0, 1, \ldots, T_{max} - 1$):
   - For every particle $i$, sample $\vec{r}_1, \vec{r}_2 \in [0,1]^d$.
   - Update velocity $\vec{v}_i(t+1)$ using the canonical equation.
   - Clamp velocity: $\vec{v}_i(t+1) \in [-V_{max}, V_{max}]^d$.
   - Update position: $\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)$.
   - Clamp position: $\vec{x}_i(t+1) \in [\vec{x}_{min}, \vec{x}_{max}]^d$.
   - Evaluate fitness $f(\vec{x}_i(t+1))$.
   - Update personal best: if $f(\vec{x}_i(t+1)) < f(\vec{p}_i(t))$, then $\vec{p}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t+1)$, else $\vec{p}_i(t+1) = \vec{p}_i(t)$.
   - Update global best: $\vec{g}(t+1) = \arg\min_i f(\vec{p}_i(t+1))$.

3. **Termination**: Stop when $t \ge T_{max}$, or when $|f(\vec{g}(t)) - f_{true}| \le \epsilon$, or when no improvement over a fixed number of iterations (stagnation check).

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint Handling:** Velocity clamping with $V_{max}$ is *crucial*. Without it, particles gain infinite kinetic energy and diverge (called "swarm explosion"). A common KTU formula is $V_{max} = k \cdot (x_{max} - x_{min})$ with $k \in [0.1, 1.0]$.

## 2.4 Neighborhood Topologies (KTU-Favorite Discussion)

PSO supports multiple social structures. The choice of topology fundamentally alters convergence behavior:

| Topology | Information Flow | Convergence Speed | Diversity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Global (gbest)** | All-to-all (star) | Fast | Low — prone to premature convergence |
| **Local (lbest)** | Ring / Von Neumann | Slow | High — better exploration |
| **Wheel** | Central hub broadcasts | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Pyramid** | Hierarchical tree | Moderate | Moderate |

In **gbest PSO**, every particle is informed by the single best-performing particle. In **lbest PSO**, each particle is influenced only by a small spatial or index-based neighborhood of size $K$ — a feature useful for **dynamic optimization**, where multiple optima may be tracked simultaneously.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

PSO is not merely an academic curiosity; it is deployed in:

- **Power systems:** Optimal power flow, economic dispatch, unit commitment.
- **Antenna design:** Beamforming, array synthesis, RFID tag placement.
- **Robotics:** Path planning, swarm-robot coordination, SLAM.
- **Machine learning:** Hyper-parameter tuning, neural network weight optimization, feature selection.
- **Biomedical engineering:** Tumor classification, ECG signal denoising, drug dosage optimization.
- **Civil engineering:** Structural design optimization, traffic signal timing.
- **Dynamic systems:** Real-time tracking of moving targets in radar, financial time-series forecasting, adaptive control of nonlinear plants.

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Canonical PSO Update from a Simplified Model

We will derive the velocity update equation rigorously. This derivation frequently appears in KTU board exams as a "show that" question worth 7-10 marks.

### Step 1: Start from the Simplest Local Search Model

Consider a single particle $i$ in one dimension. The most basic local search update is:

$$
x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + v_i(t+1)
$$

**Reasoning:** Position changes by a discrete "step" called the velocity. This is mathematically equivalent to a forward Euler discretization of a continuous-time trajectory.

### Step 2: Define the Three Drivers of Velocity

The velocity must be influenced by:
- **Inertia:** A fraction of the previous velocity, $w \cdot v_i(t)$.
- **Cognitive pull:** A step toward the particle's own best, scaled by $c_1 r_1$.
- **Social pull:** A step toward the swarm's best, scaled by $c_2 r_2$.

### Step 3: Express Each Driver Mathematically

The cognitive pull is the vector difference between personal best and current position, scaled stochastically:

$$
\Delta v_{cog} = c_1 r_1 \left( p_i(t) - x_i(t) \right)
$$

The social pull is the vector difference between global best and current position, scaled stochastically:

$$
\Delta v_{soc} = c_2 r_2 \left( g(t) - x_i(t) \right)
$$

The inertia term retains prior momentum:

$$
\Delta v_{inertia} = w \cdot v_i(t)
$$

### Step 4: Sum the Three Drivers (Superposition Principle)

The total velocity update is the **linear superposition** of all three influences, because we assume the social-cognitive forces act additively and independently in the stochastic regime:

$$
v_i(t+1) = w \cdot v_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \left( p_i(t) - x_i(t) \right) + c_2 r_2 \left( g(t) - x_i(t) \right)
$$

### Step 5: Position Update by Forward Discretization

$$
x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + v_i(t+1)
$$

### Step 6: Vectorization to d Dimensions

Extending to $d$ dimensions with element-wise multiplication $\odot$:

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w \vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 \vec{r}_1 \odot (\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i)
$$

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i + \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

**Final form proven.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Problem)

> **Problem:** A PSO swarm has 3 particles in 2-D space. At iteration $t$, the state is:
>
> - Particle 1: $\vec{x}_1 = (2, 3)$, $\vec{v}_1 = (1, 0.5)$, $\vec{p}_1 = (1.5, 2.5)$.
> - Particle 2: $\vec{x}_2 = (4, 1)$, $\vec{v}_2 = (-0.5, 1)$, $\vec{p}_2 = (3, 1.5)$.
> - Particle 3: $\vec{x}_3 = (1, 4)$, $\vec{v}_3 = (0.2, -0.8)$, $\vec{p}_3 = (1, 4)$.
> - Global best: $\vec{g} = (1.5, 2.5)$ (from Particle 1).
> - Parameters: $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = 1.5$, $c_2 = 1.5$.
> - Random numbers sampled for Particle 1: $r_1 = (0.4, 0.7)$, $r_2 = (0.6, 0.2)$.
>
> **Compute $\vec{x}_1(t+1)$.**

### Step 1: Compute the Cognitive Term

$$
c_1 \vec{r}_1 \odot (\vec{p}_1 - \vec{x}_1) = 1.5 \cdot (0.4, 0.7) \odot ((1.5, 2.5) - (2, 3))
$$

Computing the difference:

$$
\vec{p}_1 - \vec{x}_1 = (1.5 - 2, \ 2.5 - 3) = (-0.5, -0.5)
$$

Element-wise scaling by $\vec{r}_1$:

$$
\vec{r}_1 \odot (\vec{p}_1 - \vec{x}_1) = (0.4 \times -0.5, \ 0.7 \times -0.5) = (-0.2, -0.35)
$$

Multiply by $c_1 = 1.5$:

$$
c_1 \vec{r}_1 \odot (\vec{p}_1 - \vec{x}_1) = (-0.3, -0.525)
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Social Term

$$
c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_1) = 1.5 \cdot (0.6, 0.2) \odot ((1.5, 2.5) - (2, 3))
$$

Difference:

$$
\vec{g} - \vec{x}_1 = (-0.5, -0.5)
$$

Element-wise:

$$
\vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_1) = (0.6 \times -0.5, \ 0.2 \times -0.5) = (-0.3, -0.1)
$$

Multiply by $c_2 = 1.5$:

$$
c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_1) = (-0.45, -0.15)
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Inertia Term

$$
w \vec{v}_1 = 0.7 \times (1, 0.5) = (0.7, 0.35)
$$

### Step 4: Sum All Three Terms to Get New Velocity

$$
\vec{v}_1(t+1) = (0.7, 0.35) + (-0.3, -0.525) + (-0.45, -0.15)
$$

Component-wise addition:

$$
\vec{v}_1(t+1) = (0.7 - 0.3 - 0.45, \ 0.35 - 0.525 - 0.15) = (-0.05, -0.325)
$$

### Step 5: Update Position

$$
\vec{x}_1(t+1) = \vec{x}_1 + \vec{v}_1(t+1) = (2, 3) + (-0.05, -0.325) = (1.95, 2.675)
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\vec{v}_1(t+1) = (-0.05, -0.325), \quad \vec{x}_1(t+1) = (1.95, 2.675)
$$

> **Interpretation:** Particle 1 moved slightly to the left and downward — consistent with both $\vec{p}_1$ and $\vec{g}$ being at $(1.5, 2.5)$, which is up-and-left of its current position. ✅

## 3.3 Dynamic Optimization Problems — The Extended Analysis

A **dynamic optimization problem (DOP)** is an optimization problem whose objective function, constraints, or both change over time. Formally:

$$
F(\vec{x}, t) = \min_{\vec{x} \in \Omega(t)} f(\vec{x}, t)
$$

where $\Omega(t)$ is the feasible region at time $t$ and the optimum $\vec{x}^*(t)$ may drift continuously or in discrete steps.

### Why Standard PSO Fails in Dynamic Environments

| Problem | Mechanism in Standard PSO |
|---|---|
| **Loss of diversity** | The swarm converges to a single point; cannot detect a moved optimum. |
| **Outdated memory** | $\vec{p}_i$ and $\vec{g}$ retain old values; the swarm chases a phantom optimum. |
| **Premature convergence** | Once converged, velocity $\to 0$; particles cannot re-explore. |

### Countermeasure 1: Charged PSO (CPSO)

**Idea:** Add electrostatic repulsion between particles so that even at convergence, particles maintain a minimum distance — a phenomenon called *quantum-like* or *charged* behavior.

$$
\vec{F}_{ij} = k \cdot \frac{Q_i Q_j}{\Vert \vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j \Vert^2} \cdot \frac{\vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j}{\Vert \vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j \Vert}
$$

A "charged" particle experiences a repulsion force that prevents total collapse.

### Countermeasure 2: Quantum Swarm Optimization (QSO)

**Idea:** Replace the classical trajectory of particles with a quantum-delta-potential-well model. Each particle has no defined velocity — instead, its position is sampled from a probability distribution centered on the swarm's best.

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{g}(t) \pm \beta \cdot \vert \vec{x}_i(t) - \vec{g}(t) \vert \cdot \ln(1/u)
$$

where $u \sim U(0,1)$ and $\beta$ is a creativity coefficient. This naturally maintains diversity.

### Countermeasure 3: Multi-Swarm Approaches

The swarm is split into $K$ subswarms, each tracking a separate peak. Subswarms can:
- **Compete:** Exclude the worst subswarm periodically.
- **Cooperate:** Share information through a global best.
- **Anti-converge:** Repel each other to cover different regions.

### Countermeasure 4: Memory-Based Approaches

Maintain an **archive of past optima** (an external memory). When a change is detected, the swarm is **re-seeded** from this archive to re-explore previously known promising regions. The detection uses **sentry particles** whose fitness is monitored for change.

### Countermeasure 5: Self-Adaptive PSO

Parameters $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$ are encoded as part of the particle and evolve alongside the position. Each particle tunes its own exploration vs. exploitation balance — a highly KTU-recommended advanced variant.

## 3.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity update | $\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i + c_1\vec{r}_1\odot(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2\vec{r}_2\odot(\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i)$ | Canonical form |
| 2 | Position update | $\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)$ | Forward Euler discretization |
| 3 | Linearly decreasing inertia | $w(t) = w_{max} - (w_{max} - w_{min})t / T_{max}$ | $w_{max}=0.9, w_{min}=0.4$ |
| 4 | Constriction coefficient | $\chi = 2k / \vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi}\vert$ with $\phi = c_1 + c_2$ | Clerc–Kennedy stability |
| 5 | Velocity clamping | $v_{ij} \in [-V_{max}, V_{max}]$ | Prevents swarm explosion |
| 6 | QSO position | $\vec{x}_i = \vec{g} \pm \beta \vert \vec{m} - \vec{x}_i\vert \ln(1/u)$ | Quantum-delta-well |
| 7 | Charged force | $\vec{F}_{ij} = kQ_iQ_j(\vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j) / \Vert \vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j\Vert^3$ | Coulomb-like repulsion |
| 8 | PSO convergence | $E[\vec{x}_i(\infty)] = (c_1\vec{p}_i + c_2\vec{g}) / (c_1 + c_2)$ | Stochastic expectation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (8) reveals that a particle's expected attractor lies on a *stochastic weighted segment* between its personal best and the global best — a foundational result used in PSO convergence proofs and stability analysis (Van den Bergh, 2002; Clerc & Kennedy, 2002).

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PSO Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following diagram shows the canonical PSO execution flow. Note the strict use of alphanumeric node identifiers and uppercase text labels (no markdown inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START PSO ALGORITHM])
    initA[Initialize swarm: random positions and velocities]
    setBestA[Set pbest for each particle, find gbest]
    loopA{Loop until max iterations or convergence}
    randGenA[Generate random vectors r1 and r2 in 0 1 to the d]
    velUpA[Update velocity vi using canonical equation]
    velClampA[Clamp velocity to minus Vmax to Vmax]
    posUpA[Update position xi plus vi]
    posClampA[Clamp position to xmin to xmax]
    fitEvalA[Evaluate fitness f of xi]
    pbestChkA{Is f of xi better than f of pbest}
    pbestUpA[Update pbest to xi]
    pbestKeepA[Keep old pbest]
    gbestChkA{Is f of pbest better than f of gbest}
    gbestUpA[Update gbest to new pbest]
    gbestKeepA[Keep old gbest]
    incrA[Increment iteration counter t]
    outA([OUTPUT gbest as final solution])

    startA --> initA --> setBestA --> loopA
    loopA -- NO not done --> randGenA --> velUpA --> velClampA --> posUpA --> posClampA --> fitEvalA --> pbestChkA
    pbestChkA -- YES --> pbestUpA --> gbestChkA
    pbestChkA -- NO --> pbestKeepA --> gbestChkA
    gbestChkA -- YES --> gbestUpA --> incrA
    gbestChkA -- NO --> gbestKeepA --> incrA
    incrA --> loopA
    loopA -- YES done --> outA
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Social Adaptation

This diagram decomposes the three forces that drive a single particle's velocity update — the *core* of social adaptation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inertiaA[INERTIA BLOCK w times vi of t]
    cogA[COGNITIVE BLOCK c1 times r1 times pbest minus xi]
    socA[SOCIAL BLOCK c2 times r2 times gbest minus xi]
    velA[VELOCITY SUMMER vi of t plus 1]
    posA[POSITION UPDATER xi of t plus 1 equals xi plus vi]
    outA[FITNESS EVALUATOR f of xi]

    inertiaA --> velA
    cogA --> velA
    socA --> velA
    velA --> posA
    posA --> outA
    outA -- pbest check --> cogA
    outA -- gbest check --> socA
```

## 4.3 Dynamic PSO Architecture (Block Diagram)

For dynamic optimization, the standard PSO is augmented with a change-detection and diversity-restoration layer. The figure below captures the functional interaction between these components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dynA[DYNAMIC ENVIRONMENT f of x t]
    envMonA[ENVIRONMENT MONITOR / SENTRY PARTICLES]
    chgA{CHANGE DETECTED IN FITNESS LANDSCAPE}
    dynA --> envMonA --> chgA
    chgA -- NO change --> stdA[STANDARD PSO UPDATE LOOP]
    chgA -- YES change detected --> divA[DIVERSITY RESTORATION MECHANISM]
    divA --> reseedA[RE-SEED FROM MEMORY ARCHIVE]
    divA --> chgA2[APPLY CHARGED OR QUANTUM VARIANT]
    reseedA --> stdA
    chgA2 --> stdA
    stdA --> archiveA[UPDATE MEMORY ARCHIVE WITH NEW PBEST AND GBEST]
    archiveA --> dynA
```

## 4.4 Particle Update Conceptual Diagram (Symbolic)

The following diagram describes the conceptual geometry of a particle's trajectory in 2-D space, showing the three force vectors and the resulting movement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1A[Current position xi of t]
    p2A[Personal best pbest]
    p3A[Global best gbest]
    p4A[New position xi of t plus 1]
    noteA[Inertia pulls along previous velocity]
    noteB[Cognitive pull directed from xi to pbest]
    noteC[Social pull directed from xi to gbest]
    noteD[Resultant move is the vector sum of all three forces]

    p1A -- Velocity w times vi --> noteA
    p1A -- c1 r1 times pbest minus xi --> noteB
    p1A -- c2 r2 times gbest minus xi --> noteC
    noteA --> p4A
    noteB --> p4A
    noteC --> p4A
    p4A --> noteD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading aid for KTU exams:** When a board question asks you to *"sketch the velocity update geometry"*, draw:
> 1. The particle at its current position.
> 2. An arrow toward $\vec{p}_i$ (cognitive) and another toward $\vec{g}$ (social).
> 3. An arrow along the previous velocity (inertia).
> 4. The resultant as the vector sum, ending at the new position.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: Define Particle Swarm Optimization. List the three components of the PSO velocity update equation and explain the role of inertia weight $w$.**

**Model Answer:**

Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is a population-based meta-heuristic optimization technique inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling, developed by Kennedy and Eberhart in 1995. In PSO, a population of candidate solutions called particles moves through the search space, with each particle's movement influenced by its own best-known position and the best-known position of the swarm.

The three components of the PSO velocity update equation are:
1. **Inertia component** ($w \cdot \vec{v}_i$): Controls the influence of the particle's previous velocity.
2. **Cognitive component** ($c_1 \vec{r}_1 \odot (\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i)$): Pulls the particle toward its personal best position.
3. **Social component** ($c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i)$): Pulls the particle toward the global best position found by the swarm.

The **inertia weight $w$** balances exploration and exploitation. A large $w$ (e.g., 0.9) promotes global search by allowing particles to maintain momentum and explore new regions. A small $w$ (e.g., 0.4) promotes local search and fine-tuning near promising regions. A linearly decreasing schedule is commonly used. ✅

---

### **Question 2** *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q: What is a dynamic optimization problem? Why does standard PSO struggle in such environments, and what is one solution?**

**Model Answer:**

A **dynamic optimization problem (DOP)** is an optimization problem whose objective function, constraints, or both change over time. Formally, we seek $\vec{x}^*(t) = \arg\min_{\vec{x}} f(\vec{x}, t)$, where the optimum may drift continuously or in discrete jumps.

Standard PSO struggles in dynamic environments because:
- The swarm converges to a point, losing the **diversity** needed to detect a moved optimum.
- The **personal best ($\vec{p}_i$) and global best ($\vec{g}$) become outdated** — particles chase a phantom optimum that no longer exists.
- Once particles converge, their velocities approach zero, so they cannot **re-explore** the new landscape.

**One solution:** Use **Charged PSO (CPSO)**, where a fraction of the swarm carries an electrostatic charge and experiences mutual repulsion. This **maintains diversity** even at convergence, enabling the swarm to detect and track the moving optimum. ✅

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Pattern:** You are given a 14-mark question and must answer **either** the OR option. Each question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A** *(14 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`

#### **Q: (a)** *(7 Marks)*
**Explain the Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm in detail. State the velocity and position update equations and describe the role of each parameter ($w$, $c_1$, $c_2$, $\vec{r}_1$, $\vec{r}_2$).**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
PSO is a population-based stochastic optimization technique inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking, proposed by Kennedy and Eberhart (1995). Each particle is a candidate solution moving in a $d$-dimensional search space.

**Update Equations:**

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w \vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 \vec{r}_1 \odot (\vec{p}_i(t) - \vec{x}_i(t)) + c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g}(t) - \vec{x}_i(t))
$$

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)
$$

**Role of Each Parameter:**

| Parameter | Role |
|---|---|
| $w$ (inertia weight) | Controls the contribution of the previous velocity. Higher $w$ → more exploration; lower $w$ → more exploitation. |
| $c_1$ (cognitive coefficient) | Scales the pull toward $\vec{p}_i$. High $c_1$ → particle trusts own experience. |
| $c_2$ (social coefficient) | Scales the pull toward $\vec{g}$. High $c_2$ → particle trusts the swarm. |
| $\vec{r}_1$, $\vec{r}_2$ | Random vectors in $[0,1]^d$ that introduce stochasticity. |

**Algorithm Steps (in order):**
1. Initialize positions and velocities randomly.
2. Evaluate fitness of each particle.
3. Set $\vec{p}_i = \vec{x}_i$ for each particle.
4. Set $\vec{g}$ to the position of the best particle.
5. Repeat until convergence: update velocity, update position, evaluate fitness, update $\vec{p}_i$, update $\vec{g}$. ✅

#### **Q: (b)** *(7 Marks)*
**A 2-D PSO problem has the following state at iteration $t=5$ for particle 1:**
- $\vec{x}_1 = (3, 4)$, $\vec{v}_1 = (0.5, -1)$, $\vec{p}_1 = (2, 3)$.
- Global best $\vec{g} = (1, 2)$.
- Parameters: $w = 0.6$, $c_1 = 1.5$, $c_2 = 2.0$.
- Random vectors: $\vec{r}_1 = (0.3, 0.8)$, $\vec{r}_2 = (0.9, 0.4)$.

**Compute $\vec{v}_1(6)$ and $\vec{x}_1(6)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cognitive term**

$$
c_1 \vec{r}_1 \odot (\vec{p}_1 - \vec{x}_1) = 1.5 \cdot (0.3, 0.8) \odot ((2,3) - (3,4))
$$

$$
\vec{p}_1 - \vec{x}_1 = (-1, -1)
$$

$$
\vec{r}_1 \odot (-1, -1) = (-0.3, -0.8)
$$

$$
c_1 \vec{r}_1 \odot (\vec{p}_1 - \vec{x}_1) = (-0.45, -1.2)
$$

**Step 2: Social term**

$$
c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_1) = 2.0 \cdot (0.9, 0.4) \odot ((1,2) - (3,4))
$$

$$
\vec{g} - \vec{x}_1 = (-2, -2)
$$

$$
\vec{r}_2 \odot (-2, -2) = (-1.8, -0.8)
$$

$$
c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_1) = (-3.6, -1.6)
$$

**Step 3: Inertia term**

$$
w \vec{v}_1 = 0.6 \cdot (0.5, -1) = (0.3, -0.6)
$$

**Step 4: Sum**

$$
\vec{v}_1(6) = (0.3, -0.6) + (-0.45, -1.2) + (-3.6, -1.6)
$$

$$
\vec{v}_1(6) = (0.3 - 0.45 - 3.6, \ -0.6 - 1.2 - 1.6) = (-3.75, -3.4)
$$

**Step 5: Position update**

$$
\vec{x}_1(6) = \vec{x}_1(5) + \vec{v}_1(6) = (3, 4) + (-3.75, -3.4) = (-0.75, 0.6)
$$

**Final Answer:** $\vec{v}_1(6) = (-3.75, -3.4)$, $\vec{x}_1(6) = (-0.75, 0.6)$. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> - **Forgetting element-wise multiplication $\odot$** — many students write $c_1 r_1 (p_i - x_i)$ and treat $\vec{r}_1$ as a scalar. This loses up to 2 marks.
> - **Mixing up the order** of position update (must be $x_i + v_{new}$, not $x_i + v_{old}$).
> - **Omitting clamping** of velocity/position when describing the algorithm — not always needed in numerical problems, but examiners expect a mention for the full algorithm question.

---

### **QUESTION B** *(14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`

#### **Q: (a)** *(7 Marks)*
**Discuss the various techniques used to adapt PSO for dynamic optimization problems. Compare at least two techniques in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

Dynamic optimization problems (DOPs) have time-varying objective functions. Standard PSO loses diversity and tracks outdated optima. The following techniques address this:

**1. Charged PSO (CPSO):**
A fraction of particles is assigned a positive charge. The velocity update is augmented with a Coulomb-like repulsion term:

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i + c_1\vec{r}_1\odot(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2\vec{r}_2\odot(\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i) + \vec{F}_{ij}/m
$$

The repulsion maintains a minimum inter-particle distance, ensuring that when the optimum moves, the swarm can detect the change.

**2. Quantum Swarm Optimization (QSO):**
Particles are modeled as quantum particles in a delta potential well centered at $\vec{g}$:

$$
\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{g}(t) \pm \beta \vert \vec{x}_i(t) - \vec{g}(t) \vert \cdot \ln(1/u)
$$

Velocity is undefined; positions are sampled stochastically around $\vec{g}$. This naturally preserves diversity.

**3. Multi-Swarm PSO:**
The population is partitioned into $K$ subswarms, each tracking a separate peak. Anti-convergence repulsion keeps subswarms spatially separated. Best-performing subswarms are retained, worst are reinitialized.

**4. Memory-Based PSO with Sentry Particles:**
A fraction of particles is designated as sentries, monitoring the environment. When fitness changes, a **change detection signal** triggers re-seeding of the swarm from an archive of past optima.

**Comparison (CPSO vs. QSO):**

| Aspect | Charged PSO | Quantum PSO |
|---|---|---|
| Diversity mechanism | Coulomb repulsion | Quantum probability distribution |
| Velocity preserved? | Yes | No — only stochastic positions |
| Computational cost | Moderate (extra force term) | Low (no velocity update) |
| Best for | Continuous, slow drifts | Fast, large-scale changes |
| Tuning parameter | Charge $Q$, constant $k$ | Creativity $\beta$ |

**5. Self-Adaptive PSO:**
Parameters $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$ are encoded as additional particle components and evolve over time. Each particle tunes itself.

**Conclusion:** No single technique dominates across all DOPs. CPSO is robust for gradual drift, while QSO is computationally efficient for abrupt changes. ✅

#### **Q: (b)** *(7 Marks)*
**Explain the role of neighborhood topology in PSO. Compare global-best (gbest) and local-best (lbest) topologies in terms of convergence speed, diversity, and suitability for dynamic problems.**

**Model Solution:**

**Neighborhood topology** defines the social communication structure — i.e., which particles a given particle considers when computing its social component. The topology is captured by a graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of particles and $E$ defines communication links.

**Global-Best (gbest) PSO — Star Topology:**
Every particle is connected to every other particle. The social component uses the single best particle in the *entire* swarm:

$$
\vec{v}_i^{soc} = c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g}_{global} - \vec{x}_i)
$$

- **Convergence speed:** Very fast — information propagates in one step.
- **Diversity:** Low — all particles pulled toward the same attractor.
- **Dynamic suitability:** Poor — once converged, the swarm cannot escape to track a moved optimum.

**Local-Best (lbest) PSO — Ring or Von Neumann Topology:**
Each particle communicates only with $K$ immediate neighbors (ring) or with particles in a 2-D grid (Von Neumann). The social component uses the best in the local neighborhood:

$$
\vec{v}_i^{soc} = c_2 \vec{r}_2 \odot (\vec{g}_{neighborhood(i)} - \vec{x}_i)
$$

- **Convergence speed:** Slower — information must diffuse through the graph.
- **Diversity:** High — different neighborhoods may track different optima simultaneously.
- **Dynamic suitability:** Excellent — multiple subswarms can track multiple moving peaks.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Aspect | gbest (Star) | lbest (Ring/Von Neumann) |
|---|---|---|
| Convergence speed | Fast | Slow |
| Diversity | Low | High |
| Risk of premature convergence | High | Low |
| Suitability for static problems | High | Moderate |
| Suitability for dynamic problems | Low | High |
| Information flow | O(1) hop | O(N) hops |

**Conclusion:** The choice of topology is a fundamental design decision. gbest is preferred for fast convergence on static, unimodal problems. lbest is preferred for multimodal or dynamic landscapes where diversity is critical. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Defining only one technique** for part (a) is a common reason for losing 2-3 marks. Examiners expect **at least two** techniques with a clear comparison.
> - **Drawing a topology diagram** is worth 1-2 marks in part (b). Students often describe in words but fail to sketch the star vs. ring graphs.
> - **Not linking topology to dynamic optimization** — explicitly stating *why* lbest helps in dynamic environments earns full credit.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam — PSO & Dynamic Optimization**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [x] PSO was developed by **Kennedy & Eberhart (1995)**.
- [x] Inspired by **bird flocking, fish schooling**, and **social cognition theory**.
- [x] Each particle has a **position $\vec{x}_i$**, **velocity $\vec{v}_i$**, **personal best $\vec{p}_i$**, and shares knowledge of the **global best $\vec{g}$**.
- [x] Social adaptation = **Inertia + Cognition + Social pull**.

### ✅ Must-Memorize Formulas
- [x] **Velocity update:** $\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i + c_1\vec{r}_1\odot(\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i) + c_2\vec{r}_2\odot(\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i)$.
- [x] **Position update:** $\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)$.
- [x] **Linearly decreasing inertia:** $w(t) = w_{max} - (w_{max} - w_{min}) \cdot t / T_{max}$.
- [x] **Constriction coefficient:** $\chi = 2k / \vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert$ where $\phi = c_1 + c_2$.
- [x] **Quantum position sampling:** $\vec{x}_i = \vec{g} \pm \beta \vert \vec{m} - \vec{x}_i \vert \ln(1/u)$.

### ✅ Parameter Recommendations (Standard KTU Values)
- [x] Swarm size $N = 20$ to $50$ (for most problems).
- [x] $c_1 = c_2 = 2.0$ is a common default.
- [x] $c_1 + c_2 \le 4.0$ for stability (Clerc–Kennedy).
- [x] $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ with linearly decreasing schedule.
- [x] $V_{max} = k \cdot (x_{max} - x_{min})$ where $k \in [0.1, 1.0]$.

### ✅ Dynamic PSO Variants to Remember
- [x] **Charged PSO (CPSO)** — repulsion for diversity.
- [x] **Quantum PSO (QSO)** — probabilistic position sampling.
- [x] **Multi-Swarm PSO** — multiple subswarms for multiple peaks.
- [x] **Memory-Based PSO** — archive + sentry particles.
- [x] **Self-Adaptive PSO** — parameters evolve with the particle.

### ✅ Common KTU Exam Traps
- [x] Forgetting $\odot$ (element-wise) and treating $\vec{r}$ as a scalar.
- [x] Confusing **gbest** (global) with **lbest** (local neighborhood best).
- [x] Forgetting to **clamp** velocity — never mention swarm explosion in essays.
- [x] Writing position update with **old** velocity instead of new.
- [x] Not mentioning **change detection** when discussing dynamic PSO.

### ✅ One-Line High-Yield Definitions
- [x] **PSO:** Population-based stochastic optimization inspired by social behavior of bird flocking.
- [x] **pbest:** Best position a particle has ever occupied.
- [x] **gbest:** Best position found by the entire swarm.
- [x] **Inertia weight:** Balances global vs. local search.
- [x] **Dynamic optimization:** Optimization in time-varying landscapes.
- [x] **Charged PSO:** PSO variant with electrostatic repulsion to preserve diversity.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"Explain PSO for dynamic optimization"*, always structure your answer as: **(1) Why standard PSO fails → (2) List at least 3 variants → (3) Compare two with a table → (4) Conclude with a real-world example.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) Optimization, Biologically Inspired Algorithms in Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT414 - NATURE INSPIRED COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) Optimization, Biologically Inspired Algorithms in Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biologically Inspired Computing — The Big Picture

**Biologically Inspired Computing (BIC)** refers to a family of computational paradigms, metaheuristics, and optimization algorithms whose structural logic, search operators, and adaptive mechanisms are abstracted directly from the behavior, evolution, physiology, and swarm intelligence of biological systems. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic sits at the intersection of computational intelligence, soft computing, and operations research.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECAT414 / Module 3):**
> *Biologically inspired algorithms are stochastic population-based optimization techniques that mimic natural evolutionary, swarm, ecological, or neural processes to find near-optimal solutions for complex, non-differentiable, multimodal, and NP-hard engineering problems where classical deterministic methods fail.*

The umbrella of Biologically Inspired Algorithms (BIA) broadly covers:

- **Evolutionary Algorithms (EA)** — Genetic Algorithm (GA), Differential Evolution (DE), Genetic Programming (GP), Evolution Strategies (ES).
- **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** — Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), Artificial Bee Colony (ABC), Firefly Algorithm (FA), Cuckoo Search (CS), Bat Algorithm (BA), Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO), Whale Optimization Algorithm (WOA), Krill Herd (KH), Salp Swarm Algorithm (SSA).
- **Ecology-Inspired Algorithms** — Invasive Weed Optimization (IWO), Biogeography-Based Optimization (BBO), Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO).
- **Immune-System-Inspired** — Clonal Selection Algorithm (CSA), Artificial Immune Systems (AIS).
- **Neural-Inspired** — Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) as function approximators for fitness landscapes.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Beehive, the Mountain, and the Treasure

Imagine a **dark, fog-covered mountain range** in which **treasure chests are scattered** at unknown altitudes. You cannot see them; you can only probe an altitude by "tasting the air" at any coordinate. A **swarm of blind explorers** wants to find the highest peak.

- Each **bee** = one explorer in the swarm.
- Each **food source location** = one probed coordinate (candidate solution).
- The **nectar amount** = the quality (fitness) of that coordinate.
- The **waggle dance** = the communication channel that recruits better-informed peers to richer food patches.
- The **abandonment of exhausted patches** = the ability to forget bad memory and search elsewhere.

This is exactly how the **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC)** algorithm works on a mathematical fitness landscape.

## 1.2 Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) — Formal Definition

**Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) Optimization** is a **population-based, swarm-intelligence metaheuristic** introduced by **Derviş Karaboga in 2005** for solving **continuous and combinatorial optimization problems**. It mathematically models the **foraging behavior of a honey bee colony**, where bees collectively locate, evaluate, share, and exploit rich food sources (candidate solutions) while probabilistically abandoning depleted ones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Entities in ABC:**
> - **Food Source** → Candidate solution vector $x_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \dots, x_{iD})$ in a $D$-dimensional search space.
> - **Nectar Amount** → Fitness value of the food source.
> - **Employed Bee** → A bee currently exploiting a known food source and sharing information.
> - **Onlooker Bee** → A bee waiting in the hive that probabilistically selects a food source based on the dances.
> - **Scout Bee** → A bee that abandons an exhausted food source and explores a new random location.

### Why ABC? — The "Why" Behind the Algorithm

| Property | Classical Methods (Gradient-Based) | ABC Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Derivative requirement | **Required** | **Not required** |
| Handling of discontinuities | **Poor** | **Excellent** |
| Multimodal landscapes | **Traps in local optima** | **Robust global search** |
| Constraint handling | **Complex** | **Flexible penalty/barrier methods** |
| Parallelism | **Limited** | **Inherently parallel (population-based)** |
| Convergence guarantee | **Mathematical** | **Probabilistic, near-optimal** |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **"distinguish between evolutionary, swarm-based, and ecology-based algorithms"** question. Remember: ABC belongs to **Swarm Intelligence (SI)**, not pure evolutionary computation, because its operators are **social-cooperation-based** (waggle dance, recruitment), not selection-crossover-mutation-based.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters (KTU Highlights)

The following **bold metrics** are mandatory for any KTU numerical or descriptive answer:

- **SN (Number of Food Sources)** = Number of employed bees = Number of onlooker bees. Total colony size = **2 × SN**.
- **D (Dimension)** = Number of decision variables in the optimization problem.
- **MCN (Maximum Cycle Number)** = Total iterations / generations of the algorithm.
- **Limit (Abandonment Threshold)** = Number of trials after which an exhausted food source is converted into a scout. Standard value: **Limit = SN × D** (or **0.5 × SN × D** in some variants).
- **φ_{ij} (Random Perturbation Coefficient)** ∈ [−1, 1] — a uniform random number controlling exploration intensity.
- **f_i (Objective Function Value)** — to be **minimized** (or maximized with sign inversion).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of the ABC Algorithm (Operative Logic)

The ABC algorithm progresses through **three distinct bee phases per cycle (iteration)**, plus an **initialization phase**. Below is the deep, step-by-step logic.

### Phase 0 — Initialization Phase

1. The algorithm generates **SN** initial food source positions randomly within the bounds $[L_j, U_j]$ for each dimension $j = 1, 2, \dots, D$.
2. Each food source is evaluated against the objective function $f(x_i)$ to compute the **nectar amount (fitness)**.
3. The **trial counter** for each food source is set to zero: $\text{trial}_i = 0$.

### Phase 1 — Employed Bee Phase (Local Search / Exploitation)

1. Each **employed bee** $i$ generates a **new candidate food source** $v_i$ in the neighborhood of its currently assigned source $x_i$.
2. The new position is computed by mutating **exactly one dimension** $j$ chosen uniformly at random.
3. A **greedy selection** is performed: if $f(v_i) \le f(x_i)$, the bee adopts $v_i$ and resets $\text{trial}_i = 0$; otherwise, it keeps $x_i$ and **increments** $\text{trial}_i = \text{trial}_i + 1$.
4. This step drives **exploitation** — fine-tuning known good regions.

### Phase 2 — Onlooker Bee Phase (Probabilistic Selection / Information Sharing)

1. Onlooker bees watch the **waggle dance** performed by employed bees in the hive.
2. They compute a **selection probability** $P_i$ for each food source proportional to its fitness.
3. Using **roulette-wheel selection**, an onlooker bee picks a food source and performs the same neighbor-generation and greedy-selection steps as an employed bee.
4. This step drives **exploitation guided by social information**.

### Phase 3 — Scout Bee Phase (Abandonment / Exploration)

1. If $\text{trial}_i > \text{Limit}$ for any food source, it is **abandoned**.
2. The employed bee of that source becomes a **scout bee**.
3. The scout generates a **completely new random food source** in the search space.
4. This step drives **exploration** — preventing premature convergence to local optima.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why mutate only one dimension $j$ per candidate?** → This is the canonical Karaboga ABC design. Single-dimension perturbation preserves information in untouched dimensions and produces small, controlled moves that resemble bee local foraging.
- **Why use a greedy selection?** → It ensures **monotonic non-degradation** of the best-known solution across cycles.
- **Why roulette-wheel selection for onlookers?** → Better food sources (higher fitness) get more onlookers, mimicking the natural **"rich get richer"** recruitment mechanism.
- **Why scouts?** → Without scouts, the algorithm could get stuck forever on mediocre solutions — scouts inject **diversification pressure**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation** required for KTU 2024 ESE questions on ABC. Memorize the form, the conditions, and the units / ranges.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Conditions / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_{ij} = L_j + \text{rand}(0,1) \cdot (U_j - L_j)$ | Initial random food source generation | $i = 1..SN$, $j = 1..D$ |
| 2 | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ | Candidate neighbor (mutation) | $k \neq i$, $j$ random, $\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 3 | $\text{fit}_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}$ if $f_i \ge 0$ | Fitness conversion (minimization) | $f_i \ge 0$ |
| 4 | $\text{fit}_i = 1 + \vert f_i \vert$ if $f_i < 0$ | Fitness conversion (minimization) | $f_i < 0$ |
| 5 | $P_i = \dfrac{\text{fit}_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} \text{fit}_n}$ | Onlooker selection probability | $0 \le P_i \le 1$, $\sum P_i = 1$ |
| 6 | $\text{Limit} = SN \cdot D$ | Standard abandonment threshold | Alternative: $0.5 \cdot SN \cdot D$ |
| 7 | $\text{Colony Size} = 2 \cdot SN$ | Total bees in colony | $SN$ employed + $SN$ onlookers |
| 8 | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ | Eq. 2 restated (used in scout variants) | $\phi_{ij} \sim U(-1, 1)$ |
| 9 | $\text{Mean}_j = \dfrac{1}{SN} \sum_{i=1}^{SN} x_{ij}$ | Mean position of food sources | $j = 1..D$ |
| 10 | $\sigma_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{SN} \sum_{i=1}^{SN} (x_{ij} - \text{Mean}_j)^2}$ | Standard deviation of population | Diversity metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $\text{fit}_i = \dfrac{1}{f_i}$. This is **wrong** — it is undefined for $f_i = 0$ and produces infinite fitness. The correct form is $\text{fit}_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of ABC

| Engineering Domain | Application of ABC |
|---|---|
| **Power Systems** | Optimal reactive power dispatch, economic load dispatch, unit commitment |
| **Structural Engineering** | Truss sizing, shape optimization, topology optimization |
| **Manufacturing** | Job-shop scheduling, parameter tuning of CNC, robotic path planning |
| **Image Processing** | Image segmentation (multilevel thresholding), feature selection |
| **Telecommunications** | Antenna array design, channel allocation, routing in MANETs |
| **Biomedical** | Cancer classification, ECG/EEG signal denoising, drug design |
| **Control Systems** | PID tuning, model predictive control, fuzzy system design |
| **ML / Data Mining** | Neural network weight optimization, feature subset selection, SVM parameter tuning |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Complete ABC Cycle (Hand-Calculable)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks for a **single-iteration numerical trace** of ABC. We will solve a **2-dimensional minimization problem** by hand, exactly as the examiner expects.

### Problem Setup

- **Objective function** (Sphere function): $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$
- **Search bounds**: $L_j = -5$, $U_j = 5$ for both dimensions.
- **Number of food sources**: $SN = 3$
- **Dimension**: $D = 2$
- **Limit** = $SN \times D = 3 \times 2 = 6$

### Step A — Initialization Phase

Generate SN = 3 food sources. Using the formula from §2.3 (Eq. 1):

$$x_{ij} = L_j + \text{rand}(0,1) \cdot (U_j - L_j) = -5 + r \cdot 10$$

Suppose the random draws give (rounded to 3 decimals):

- **Food source 1**: $x_1 = (1.000,\ 2.000)$ → $f(x_1) = 1^2 + 2^2 = 5.000$
- **Food source 2**: $x_2 = (-1.500,\ 0.500)$ → $f(x_2) = (-1.5)^2 + 0.5^2 = 2.500$
- **Food source 3**: $x_3 = (0.000,\ -1.000)$ → $f(x_3) = 0^2 + (-1)^2 = 1.000$

All trial counters start at 0: $\text{trial}_1 = \text{trial}_2 = \text{trial}_3 = 0$.

### Step B — Fitness Conversion (Using Eq. 3)

Since all $f_i \ge 0$, we use $\text{fit}_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}$:

$$\text{fit}_1 = \frac{1}{1 + 5.000} = \frac{1}{6.000} = 0.1667$$

$$\text{fit}_2 = \frac{1}{1 + 2.500} = \frac{1}{3.500} = 0.2857$$

$$\text{fit}_3 = \frac{1}{1 + 1.000} = \frac{1}{2.000} = 0.5000$$

### Step C — Selection Probabilities (Using Eq. 5)

$$\sum \text{fit} = 0.1667 + 0.2857 + 0.5000 = 0.9524$$

$$P_1 = \frac{0.1667}{0.9524} = 0.1750$$

$$P_2 = \frac{0.2857}{0.9524} = 0.3000$$

$$P_3 = \frac{0.5000}{0.9524} = 0.5250$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check**: $P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 1.0000$ ✓ — The lowest $f_i$ (food source 3) gets the highest selection probability — exactly as biologically expected.

### Step D — Employed Bee Phase (Mutate using Eq. 2)

For **employed bee 1**, suppose random partner $k = 3$ and random dimension $j = 1$, and $\phi_{11} = 0.7$:

$$v_{11} = x_{11} + \phi_{11}(x_{11} - x_{31}) = 1.000 + 0.7 \cdot (1.000 - 0.000) = 1.000 + 0.700 = 1.700$$

Dimension 2 stays unchanged: $v_{12} = x_{12} = 2.000$. So $v_1 = (1.700,\ 2.000)$.

$$f(v_1) = (1.7)^2 + (2.0)^2 = 2.89 + 4.00 = 6.890$$

Greedy selection: $f(v_1) = 6.890 > f(x_1) = 5.000$ → **Reject $v_1$**, retain $x_1$, increment $\text{trial}_1 = 1$.

For **employed bee 2**, suppose $k = 1$, $j = 2$, $\phi_{22} = -0.4$:

$$v_{22} = x_{22} + \phi_{22}(x_{22} - x_{12}) = 0.500 + (-0.4) \cdot (0.500 - 2.000)$$

$$v_{22} = 0.500 + (-0.4) \cdot (-1.500) = 0.500 + 0.600 = 1.100$$

Dimension 1 unchanged: $v_{21} = x_{21} = -1.500$. So $v_2 = (-1.500,\ 1.100)$.

$$f(v_2) = (-1.5)^2 + (1.1)^2 = 2.25 + 1.21 = 3.460$$

Greedy selection: $f(v_2) = 3.460 > f(x_2) = 2.500$ → **Reject $v_2$**, retain $x_2$, increment $\text{trial}_2 = 1$.

For **employed bee 3**, suppose $k = 2$, $j = 1$, $\phi_{31} = 0.9$:

$$v_{31} = x_{31} + \phi_{31}(x_{31} - x_{21}) = 0.000 + 0.9 \cdot (0.000 - (-1.500)) = 0.000 + 1.350 = 1.350$$

Dimension 2 unchanged: $v_{32} = x_{32} = -1.000$. So $v_3 = (1.350,\ -1.000)$.

$$f(v_3) = (1.35)^2 + (-1.0)^2 = 1.8225 + 1.0000 = 2.8225$$

Greedy selection: $f(v_3) = 2.8225 > f(x_3) = 1.000$ → **Reject $v_3$**, retain $x_3$, increment $\text{trial}_3 = 1$.

### Step E — Onlooker Bee Phase (Probabilistic Choice)

Using probabilities from Step C, suppose the onlooker bees draw random numbers that land on **food source 3** (twice) and **food source 2** (once).

**Onlooker A (chose food source 3)**: Suppose $k = 1$, $j = 2$, $\phi = -0.6$:

$$v_{3,2}^{\text{new}} = x_{32} + \phi(x_{32} - x_{12}) = -1.000 + (-0.6)(-1.000 - 2.000) = -1.000 + 1.800 = 0.800$$

So new candidate $= (0.000,\ 0.800)$:

$$f = 0^2 + 0.8^2 = 0.640$$

Greedy: $0.640 < 1.000$ ✓ → **Accept!** $x_3 \leftarrow (0.000,\ 0.800)$, reset $\text{trial}_3 = 0$.

**Onlooker B (chose food source 3 again)**: Suppose $k = 2$, $j = 1$, $\phi = 0.3$:

$$v_{3,1}^{\text{new}} = 0.000 + 0.3(0.000 - (-1.500)) = 0.450$$

Candidate $= (0.450,\ 0.800)$:

$$f = 0.45^2 + 0.8^2 = 0.2025 + 0.64 = 0.8425$$

Greedy: $0.8425 < 0.640$? **No**, $0.8425 > 0.640$ → **Reject**, $\text{trial}_3 = 1$.

**Onlooker C (chose food source 2)**: Suppose $k = 1$, $j = 1$, $\phi = -0.2$:

$$v_{2,1}^{\text{new}} = -1.500 + (-0.2)(-1.500 - 1.000) = -1.500 + 0.500 = -1.000$$

Candidate $= (-1.000,\ 0.500)$:

$$f = (-1)^2 + 0.5^2 = 1.25$$

Greedy: $1.25 < 2.500$ ✓ → **Accept!** $x_2 \leftarrow (-1.000,\ 0.500)$, reset $\text{trial}_2 = 0$.

### Step F — Scout Bee Phase

After this cycle: $\text{trial}_1 = 1$, $\text{trial}_2 = 0$, $\text{trial}_3 = 1$. Since all $\text{trial}_i \le \text{Limit} = 6$, **no abandonment** occurs. **Cycle 1 complete.**

### Final Cycle 1 Solution

$$\boxed{x_{\text{best}} = x_3 = (0.000,\ 0.800),\quad f_{\text{best}} = 0.640}$$

The global optimum is at $(0, 0)$ with $f = 0$. The algorithm has moved significantly closer in one cycle.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — ABC vs. Other Swarm Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparative matrix is a **high-yield KTU question** and appears frequently in ESE.

| Feature | **ABC** | **PSO** | **ACO** | **GA** | **DE** | **GWO** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inspiration** | Honey bee foraging | Bird/fish flocking | Ant pheromone trails | Darwinian evolution | Evolution (vectors) | Wolf pack hierarchy |
| **Population type** | Food sources | Particles | Ants | Chromosomes | Individuals | Wolves |
| **Search operator** | Random perturbation | Velocity update | Pheromone deposit | Crossover + mutation | Differential mutation | α/β/δ hierarchy |
| **Memory** | Trial counter | Personal + global best | Pheromone matrix | None explicit | None explicit | Three leaders |
| **Greedy selection** | Yes (food source) | Yes (pbest/gbest) | Probabilistic | Generational replacement | One-to-one greedy | One-to-one greedy |
| **Parameters** | SN, Limit, MCN | $w, c_1, c_2$, MCN | $\alpha, \beta, \rho$, MCN | $p_c, p_m$, pop size | $F, CR$, pop size | MCN, pop size |
| **Constraint** | Continuous (native) | Continuous | Combinatorial (native) | Both | Continuous | Continuous |
| **Convergence speed** | Slow but stable | Fast | Medium | Medium | Fast | Fast |
| **Local-optima escape** | Strong (scout) | Weak (inertia helps) | Medium (evaporation) | Strong (mutation) | Medium | Strong |
| **Hand-calculable** | **Yes (excellent)** | Yes | Difficult | Yes | Yes | Yes |

## 3.3 Pseudocode Reference (Theory-Only, KTU-Compatible)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU does not require executable code, but examiners expect **structured algorithmic understanding**. The following is presented in **plain pseudocode form**, not as a programming language.

```
ALGORITHM: Artificial Bee Colony (ABC)
INPUT : Objective f, bounds [L, U], SN, D, MCN, Limit
OUTPUT: Best solution x_best and f_best

1.  // INITIALIZATION
2.  for i = 1 to SN do
3.      for j = 1 to D do
4.          x[i][j] = L[j] + rand(0,1) * (U[j] - L[j])
5.      f[i] = f(x[i])
6.      fit[i] = 1 / (1 + f[i])          // for f[i] >= 0
7.      trial[i] = 0
8.  end for
9.  x_best = argmin f(x[i]); f_best = min f(x[i])
10. for cycle = 1 to MCN do
11.     // EMPLOYED BEE PHASE
12.     for i = 1 to SN do
13.         k = random index, k != i
14.         j = random dimension in [1, D]
15.         phi = 2*rand(0,1) - 1         // in [-1, 1]
16.         v[i][j] = x[i][j] + phi*(x[i][j] - x[k][j])
17.         f_v = f(v[i])
18.         if f_v <= f[i] then
19.             x[i] = v[i];  f[i] = f_v
20.             trial[i] = 0
21.         else
22.             trial[i] = trial[i] + 1
23.     end for
24.     // ONLOOKER BEE PHASE
25.     for each onlooker t = 1 to SN do
26.         select i via roulette-wheel on P[i] = fit[i] / sum(fit)
27.         perform same mutation/greedy as lines 13-23
28.     end for
29.     // SCOUT BEE PHASE
30.     if max(trial[i]) > Limit then
31.         i_max = argmax trial[i]
32.         reinitialize x[i_max] randomly   // becomes scout
33.     end if
34.     update x_best, f_best
35. end for
36. RETURN x_best, f_best
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ABC Algorithm — Complete Cycle Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block provides a **board-exam-quality flowchart** of one complete ABC cycle. All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes to comply with Mermaid parser safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start Cycle k = 1 to MCN] --> B1[Employed Bee Phase]
    B1 --> B2[For each food source i from 1 to SN]
    B2 --> B3[Pick random partner k and dimension j]
    B3 --> B4[Generate candidate v_ij using phi]
    B4 --> B5{Evaluate f_v vs f_i}
    B5 -->|f_v better| B6[Accept v_i and reset trial_i to 0]
    B5 -->|f_v worse| B7[Retain x_i and increment trial_i]
    B6 --> C1[Onlooker Bee Phase]
    B7 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Compute selection probability P_i]
    C2 --> C3[Onlooker chooses food source via roulette wheel]
    C3 --> C4[Apply mutation and greedy selection]
    C4 --> D1[Scout Bee Phase]
    D1 --> D2{Any trial_i greater than Limit}
    D2 -->|Yes| D3[Abandon food source and reinitialize randomly]
    D2 -->|No| E1[Update global best solution]
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2{Cycle k equals MCN}
    E2 -->|No| A1
    E2 -->|Yes| F1[Return best solution and exit]
```

## 4.2 Bee Classification and Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Hive] --> E1[Employed Bee]
    H1 --> O1[Onlooker Bee]
    H1 --> S1[Scout Bee]
    E1 --> FS1[Food Source Exploitation]
    FS1 --> W1[Waggle Dance in Hive]
    W1 --> O1
    O1 --> FS1
    S1 --> FS2[Random Exploration in Field]
    FS2 --> FS1
    E1 -->|Trial Limit Exceeded| S1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Biologically Inspired Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram maps the **macro-architecture** common to all biologically inspired algorithms (GA, PSO, ABC, GWO, etc.) so that students can identify the universal pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Problem Definition] --> P2[Encode Solution as Individual]
    P2 --> P3[Initialize Population]
    P3 --> P4[Evaluate Fitness Function]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Bioinspired Operator]
    P5 --> P6[Selection or Recruitment Step]
    P6 --> P7[Update Global Best Memory]
    P7 --> P8{Convergence Check}
    P8 -->|Not Met| P3
    P8 -->|Met| P9[Decode Best Individual]
    P9 --> P10[Report Optimal or Near Optimal Solution]
```

flowchart TD
    PH0[Phase 0 Initialization] --> PH1[Phase 1 Employed Bees]
    PH1 --> PH2[Phase 2 Onlooker Bees]
    PH2 --> PH3[Phase 3 Scout Bees]

    PH4 --> PH5{Iteration Limit Reached}

```

    N1[Foraging Bee in Field] --> A1[Local Search by Employed Bee]
    N2[Waggle Dance] --> A2[Probability Sharing to Onlooker]
    N3[Recruitment to Rich Source] --> A3[Roulette Wheel Selection]
    N4[Abandonment of Depleted Source] --> A4[Scout Phase with Limit Threshold]
    N5[Nectar Quality] --> A5[Fitness Evaluation]
    N6[Colony of Bees] --> A6[Population of Food Sources]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm. List and briefly explain the three types of bees used in the ABC algorithm.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm** is a population-based swarm intelligence metaheuristic proposed by **Derviş Karaboga in 2005** that mimics the foraging behavior of a honey bee colony to solve continuous and combinatorial optimization problems.

The **three types of bees** are:

1. **Employed Bees** — Each employed bee is associated with a specific food source. They perform **local search** around the source by generating a candidate neighbor using $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ and apply **greedy selection**.
2. **Onlooker Bees** — They remain in the hive and **probabilistically select** a food source using the **roulette-wheel selection** based on fitness-derived probability $P_i = \text{fit}_i / \sum \text{fit}_n$.
3. **Scout Bees** — When a food source is **abandoned** (after its trial counter exceeds the **Limit** threshold), the associated employed bee becomes a **scout** and performs **random exploration** of the search space.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]**
*State and explain the role of the 'Limit' parameter in the ABC algorithm. What is the standard formula used to set it?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Limit** parameter in ABC is the **maximum number of trials** a food source can undergo without improvement before it is **abandoned** and its bee is converted into a **scout bee**.

**Role:**
- It controls the **exploration vs. exploitation** balance by preventing the algorithm from getting stuck in local optima.
- It introduces a **forgetting mechanism** — abandoned food sources are replaced with fresh random ones, ensuring population diversity.

**Standard Formula:**

$$\text{Limit} = SN \times D$$

where $SN$ is the number of food sources and $D$ is the dimensionality of the problem. Some variants use $\text{Limit} = 0.5 \times SN \times D$ for a more aggressive abandonment rate.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **complete algorithmic flow** of the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm. Discuss the role of the **fitness function**, **selection probability**, and the **Limit** parameter in the algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following minimization problem: $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2$ with bounds $-3 \le x_1, x_2 \le 3$. Using **SN = 3** and the following randomly initialized food sources:
- $x_1 = (1.0, 2.0)$, $x_2 = (0.5, 1.0)$, $x_3 = (-0.5, 0.0)$

Compute (i) the **fitness values**, (ii) the **selection probabilities** for onlooker bees, and (iii) perform **one complete Employed Bee Phase** for all three food sources using the standard mutation formula with suitable random choices. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — [7 Marks]

The ABC algorithm proceeds in **four phases**:

**1. Initialization Phase (1 Mark):**
SN food sources are generated randomly within the search bounds: $x_{ij} = L_j + r \cdot (U_j - L_j)$. The objective function $f(x_i)$ is evaluated for each, fitness $\text{fit}_i = 1/(1+f_i)$ (for $f_i \ge 0$) is computed, and trial counters are set to 0.

**2. Employed Bee Phase (2 Marks):**
Each employed bee $i$ produces a candidate neighbor $v_i$ by mutating one dimension $j$: $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ where $\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$ and $k \neq i$. Greedy selection is applied — better $f(v_i)$ replaces $x_i$ and resets the trial counter; otherwise the trial counter is incremented.

**3. Onlooker Bee Phase (2 Marks):**
Onlookers compute $P_i = \text{fit}_i / \sum \text{fit}_n$ and use **roulette-wheel selection** to choose a food source. The same mutation/greedy procedure is then performed. This emulates the **waggle-dance recruitment** in real bees.

**4. Scout Bee Phase (1 Mark):**
If any $\text{trial}_i > \text{Limit} = SN \times D$, the food source is **abandoned** and a **scout bee** reinitializes it randomly. This injects fresh diversity to escape local optima.

**Role of key components (1 Mark):**
- **Fitness function** $\text{fit}_i$ converts raw objective values into a non-negative selection metric.
- **Selection probability** $P_i$ governs how many onlookers each food source attracts — fitter sources get more bees (rich-get-richer).
- **Limit** parameter prevents stagnation and balances exploration.

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute objective values (1 Mark)**

$$f(x_1) = 1.0^2 + (2.0 - 1)^2 = 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0$$

$$f(x_2) = 0.5^2 + (1.0 - 1)^2 = 0.25 + 0.0 = 0.25$$

$$f(x_3) = (-0.5)^2 + (0.0 - 1)^2 = 0.25 + 1.0 = 1.25$$

**Step 2: Compute fitness (1 Mark)** — Using $\text{fit}_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}$:

$$\text{fit}_1 = \frac{1}{1 + 2.0} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333$$

$$\text{fit}_2 = \frac{1}{1 + 0.25} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.8000$$

$$\text{fit}_3 = \frac{1}{1 + 1.25} = \frac{1}{2.25} = 0.4444$$

**Step 3: Compute selection probabilities (1.5 Marks)**

$$\sum \text{fit} = 0.3333 + 0.8000 + 0.4444 = 1.5777$$

$$P_1 = \frac{0.3333}{1.5777} = 0.2113$$

$$P_2 = \frac{0.8000}{1.5777} = 0.5071$$

$$P_3 = \frac{0.4444}{1.5777} = 0.2816$$

**Sanity check:** $0.2113 + 0.5071 + 0.2816 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 4: One Complete Employed Bee Phase (3.5 Marks)**

Assume the following random choices: $k$ is the partner, $j$ is the mutated dimension, $\phi$ is the perturbation coefficient.

**Employed bee 1** (chose $k=2$, $j=1$, $\phi=0.5$):

$$v_{11} = 1.0 + 0.5 \times (1.0 - 0.5) = 1.0 + 0.25 = 1.25$$

$$v_{12} = 2.0 \text{ (unchanged)}$$

$$f(v_1) = (1.25)^2 + (2.0 - 1)^2 = 1.5625 + 1.0 = 2.5625$$

Since $2.5625 > 2.0$ → **Reject**. $\text{trial}_1 = 1$.

**Employed bee 2** (chose $k=3$, $j=2$, $\phi=-0.8$):

$$v_{22} = 1.0 + (-0.8) \times (1.0 - 0.0) = 1.0 - 0.8 = 0.2$$

$$v_{21} = 0.5 \text{ (unchanged)}$$

$$f(v_2) = (0.5)^2 + (0.2 - 1)^2 = 0.25 + 0.64 = 0.89$$

Since $0.89 > 0.25$ → **Reject**. $\text{trial}_2 = 1$.

**Employed bee 3** (chose $k=1$, $j=1$, $\phi=-0.6$):

$$v_{31} = -0.5 + (-0.6) \times (-0.5 - 1.0) = -0.5 + 0.9 = 0.4$$

$$v_{32} = 0.0 \text{ (unchanged)}$$

$$f(v_3) = (0.4)^2 + (0.0 - 1)^2 = 0.16 + 1.0 = 1.16$$

Since $1.16 < 1.25$ → **Accept!** $x_3 \leftarrow (0.4,\ 0.0)$, reset $\text{trial}_3 = 0$.

**Summary after Employed Bee Phase:**

- $x_1 = (1.0,\ 2.0)$, $\text{trial}_1 = 1$
- $x_2 = (0.5,\ 1.0)$, $\text{trial}_2 = 1$
- $x_3 = (0.4,\ 0.0)$, $\text{trial}_3 = 0$ ← **updated**

Global best so far: $x_2 = (0.5,\ 1.0)$ with $f = 0.25$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Retest Paper]**

**(a)** Describe **any four biologically inspired optimization algorithms** other than ABC. For each, state its natural inspiration and one distinguishing algorithmic feature. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** and **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC)** across the following dimensions: (i) representation of individuals, (ii) search operators, (iii) selection mechanism, (iv) exploration vs. exploitation balance, and (v) typical convergence behavior. State one engineering application where ABC outperforms GA and justify why. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — [7 Marks]

**1. Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) — [1.75 Marks]**
- **Inspiration:** Social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling (Kennedy & Eberhart, 1995).
- **Distinguishing feature:** Uses both **personal best (pbest)** and **global best (gbest)** memory; updates velocity by $v_{i}^{t+1} = w v_i^t + c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i)$.

**2. Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) — [1.75 Marks]**
- **Inspiration:** Pheromone-trail laying and following behavior of ants (Dorigo, 1992).
- **Distinguishing feature:** **Indirect communication via pheromone matrix** $\tau_{ij}$; uses probabilistic transition rules where higher pheromone concentration attracts more ants. Excellent for **combinatorial problems** like TSP.

**3. Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) — [1.75 Marks]**
- **Inspiration:** Hierarchical leadership and hunting behavior of grey wolf packs (Mirjalili, 2014).
- **Distinguishing feature:** Uses three leaders $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\delta$ to guide the search; encircling prey model $D = \vert C \cdot X_p - X(t) \vert$ with coefficient vectors $A$ and $C$ linearly decreasing for exploration-to-exploitation transition.

**4. Cuckoo Search (CS) — [1.75 Marks]**
- **Inspiration:** Brood-parasitic behavior of cuckoo species combined with Lévy flights (Yang & Deb, 2009).
- **Distinguishing feature:** Uses **Lévy flights** for global search (heavy-tailed step distribution) and a **host-nest discovery probability** $p_a$ for replacement. Converges faster than GA on many benchmarks.

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — [7 Marks]

| Dimension | **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** | **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC)** | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Representation | Binary/real-coded **chromosomes** | Real-valued **food source vectors** | 1.0 |
| (ii) Search operators | **Crossover** + **Mutation** | Single-dimension **random perturbation** $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ | 1.5 |
| (iii) Selection mechanism | **Roulette / tournament / rank-based** | **Roulette-wheel for onlookers**, greedy for employed | 1.0 |
| (iv) Exploration vs. exploitation | Crossover = exploitation, mutation = exploration; can be imbalanced | Scout = exploration, onlooker + employed = exploitation; balanced by **Limit** | 1.5 |
| (v) Convergence behavior | **Fast** initial, can **prematurely converge** | **Slower** but **more stable**, less prone to local optima | 1.0 |
| (vi) Number of parameters | Population size, $p_c$, $p_m$ | $SN$, $D$, $Limit$, $MCN$ | 1.0 |

**Engineering application where ABC outperforms GA (1 Mark):**

**Multilevel image thresholding** (e.g., Kapur's or Otsu's entropy maximization) — ABC's **probabilistic onlooker recruitment** better preserves population diversity over many cycles, while GA's crossover rapidly converges to suboptimal local thresholds due to **schema theorem bias** in high-dimensional search spaces (each threshold is a decision variable, so for $k$-level thresholding, $D = k$, often $k \ge 5$). The **scout mechanism** in ABC actively prevents this stagnation.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on ABC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using $\text{fit}_i = 1/f_i$ instead of $1/(1+f_i)$** — This is mathematically incorrect for $f_i = 0$ and loses 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting the partner index $k \neq i$ condition** in the mutation formula — examiner will deduct 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Not stating the bounds of $\phi_{ij}$** explicitly as $\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$ — a common transcription error.
> 4. **Confusing Limit with maximum iterations** — Limit controls *abandonment per source*, not the global loop. Clearly distinguish **MCN** (max cycles) from **Limit** (abandonment threshold).
> 5. **Skipping the greedy-selection reasoning** — always write *why* a candidate is accepted or rejected (cite $f(v) \le f(x)$ or $f(v) > f(x)$). Examiners look for this comparator explicitly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ABC** = **Swarm Intelligence** (not evolutionary). Proposed by **Karaboga (2005)**.
- [x] Three bee types: **Employed, Onlooker, Scout**. Colony size = **2 × SN**.
- [x] **Initialization**: $x_{ij} = L_j + r(U_j - L_j)$ where $r \in [0, 1]$.
- [x] **Mutation**: $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ with $k \neq i$, $j$ random, $\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$.
- [x] **Fitness** (for minimization, $f \ge 0$): $\text{fit}_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}$.
- [x] **Selection probability**: $P_i = \dfrac{\text{fit}_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} \text{fit}_n}$.
- [x] **Limit** = $SN \times D$ (standard) or $0.5 \times SN \times D$ (aggressive variant).
- [x] **Greedy selection** at every step preserves best-known solutions.
- [x] **Scout phase** is the key diversification operator — without it, the algorithm stagnates.
- [x] **Applications**: Power dispatch, structural optimization, image segmentation, PID tuning, neural network training, feature selection.
- [x] **ABC vs GA**: ABC is slower but more robust to local optima; GA is faster but prone to premature convergence.
- [x] **ABC vs PSO**: PSO uses velocity with inertia; ABC uses random neighbor perturbation — no velocity concept.
- [x] **ABC vs GWO**: GWO has explicit leaders ($\alpha, \beta, \delta$); ABC has implicit ranking via fitness.
- [x] **No derivative required** — ABC is a **derivative-free** metaheuristic.
- [x] **Probabilistic, not deterministic** — convergence is near-optimal, not guaranteed.
- [x] **Population-based** → inherently **parallelizable** for GPU/CPU cluster deployment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT414 - NATURE INSPIRED COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Other Swarm Intelligence Algorithms

Swarm Intelligence (SI) is a meta-heuristic computational paradigm inspired by the collective, decentralized, and self-organized behavior of biological populations in nature. Beyond the classical Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), the KTU 2024 scheme mandates the study of three additional bio-inspired metaheuristics: **Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA)**, **Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO)**, and **Intelligent Water Drop (IWD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swarm Intelligence Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A population-based, decentralized optimization paradigm where simple agents interact locally with each other and with their environment, producing emergent global intelligent behavior without any central controller.

---

## 1.2 Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA)

### Formal Definition
The **Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA)** is a swarm intelligence optimization technique proposed by Li Xiao-Lei et al. (2002) that simulates the foraging, swarming, and following behaviors of a school of fish in water. Each "fish" is an agent representing a candidate solution, and the swarm collectively searches for an optimum by emulating four fundamental behaviors.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine a group of fish in a murky lake searching for food. Each fish cannot see the entire lake but can see nearby fish. If one fish finds a tasty food patch (high fitness), the others swim toward that area (following behavior). If food is scarce, fish spread out to explore independently (searching behavior). When threatened or moving collectively, fish form groups (swarming behavior). The collective knowledge of the school locates the richest food source in the lake — this is the global optimum.

### Core Parameters of AFSA
- **Visual** — the perception radius of a fish
- **Step** — the maximum displacement per movement
- **δ (delta)** — crowding factor (controls neighborhood density)
- **Try_number** — maximum attempts in prey behavior

### Four Behavioral Operators
1. **Prey (Af_Prey):** Fish moves toward regions of higher fitness (gradient ascent).
2. **Swarm (Af_Swarm):** Fish moves toward the center of mass of its neighbors.
3. **Follow (Af_Follow):** Fish moves toward a neighbor that has found better food.
4. **Move (Af_Move):** Random exploration when no improvement is found.

---

## 1.3 Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO) Algorithm

### Formal Definition
The **Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO)** algorithm, proposed by **Kevin M. Passino (2002)**, is a bio-inspired global optimization technique that mimics the chemotactic (food-seeking) behavior of the *Escherichia coli (E. coli)* bacterium. Each bacterium represents a candidate solution, and its movement through the search space is governed by four principal mechanisms.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine millions of microscopic bacteria swimming in your stomach. They cannot "see" food but can sense nutrient concentration through chemical gradients. They "tumble" (rotate randomly) to find a direction of higher nutrient density, then "swim" (run) forward in that direction. After several generations, bacteria aggregate where food is richest. Some unlucky bacteria are carried away by environmental disturbances (elimination/dispersal), which introduces diversity, while successful bacteria reproduce to maintain the best traits.

### Core Parameters of BFO
- **N_c** — number of chemotactic steps
- **N_s** — maximum swim length after a tumble
- **N_re** — number of reproductive steps
- **N_ed** — number of elimination-dispersal events
- **P_ed** — probability of elimination/dispersal
- **C(i)** — step size per swim (run-length unit)
- **d_attract, w_attract, h_repel, w_repel** — cell-to-cell signaling coefficients

### Three Core Mechanisms
1. **Chemotaxis:** Tumbling + Swimming (gradient-based ascent)
2. **Reproduction:** Elimination of weak half, duplication of strong half (Darwinian selection)
3. **Elimination & Dispersal:** Stochastic relocation to escape local optima

---

## 1.4 Intelligent Water Drop (IWD) Algorithm

### Formal Definition
The **Intelligent Water Drop (IWD)** algorithm, proposed by **Hamed Shah-Hosseini (2007)**, is a nature-inspired optimization method based on the observation that natural water drops (raindrops) flowing in a riverbed find an optimal path between two points by modifying their environment. Each drop removes soil from one path and deposits it along another, with the amount of soil carried proportional to the velocity of the drop.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Picture raindrops running down a mountainside toward the ocean. Each drop carves tiny grooves in the soil. Droplets that travel faster carry more soil (kinetic energy model). Fast drops carve deeper paths; subsequent drops preferentially follow these deeper paths because they offer less resistance. Over time, what was a chaotic set of rivulets becomes a few deep, well-defined channels — and the system has "found" the optimal path from mountain to sea.

### Core Parameters of IWD
- **soil(IWD)** — soil amount currently held by an IWD
- **velocity(IWD)** — current speed of an IWD
- **Δsoil** — soil removed from a path during traversal
- **Δtime** — time taken to traverse an edge
- **a_v, b_v, c_v, a_s, b_s, c_s** — static parameters of the IWD model

### Key Properties
1. **Environment-Adaptive:** The graph (riverbed) is dynamically modified.
2. **Stigmergic Communication:** Indirect coordination through soil trace.
3. **Graph-Based:** Naturally suited for routing and combinatorial problems (e.g., TSP).

---

## 1.5 Applications of Biologically Inspired Algorithms in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three algorithms, together with ACO, PSO, GA, and others, form the **bio-inspired optimization toolkit** widely deployed across engineering disciplines for solving NP-hard, multi-modal, and non-differentiable problems where classical mathematical programming fails.

**Key Engineering Applications:**
- Structural optimization (truss design, topology optimization)
- Job-shop scheduling and manufacturing
- Vehicle routing and logistics
- Image segmentation and feature selection
- Wireless sensor network routing
- Power system load dispatch (economic dispatch)
- Robotic path planning
- Protein structure prediction in bioinformatics
