# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm — Operational Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Behavioral Definitions (Rigorous)

Let $X_i$ denote the current state (position) of fish *i* in an $n$-dimensional search space, and $f(X_i)$ be the fitness (food concentration).

**Prey Behavior (Af_Prey):**

The fish selects a random position $X_j$ within its visual range:

$$X_j = X_i + \text{Visual} \cdot \text{rand}()$$

If $f(X_j) > f(X_i)$, it moves in that direction:

$$X_{i}^{t+1} = X_i^t + \frac{X_j - X_i^t}{\Vert X_j - X_i^t \Vert} \cdot \text{Step} \cdot \text{rand}()$$

Otherwise, it repeats the random sampling for Try_number attempts. If no improvement is found, it executes Move.

**Swarm Behavior (Af_Swarm):**

Let $n_f$ = number of fish in the visual range and $X_c$ = center of mass:

$$X_c = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n_f} X_j}{n_f}$$

If $\frac{f(X_c)}{n_f} > \delta \cdot f(X_i)$, the fish moves toward $X_c$:

$$X_{i}^{t+1} = X_i^t + \frac{X_c - X_i^t}{\Vert X_c - X_i^t \Vert} \cdot \text{Step} \cdot \text{rand}()$$

**Follow Behavior (Af_Follow):**

Let $X_{\max}$ be the neighbor with the highest fitness:

$$X_{\max} = \arg \max_{X_j \in \text{visual}} f(X_j)$$

If $\frac{f(X_{\max})}{n_f} > \delta \cdot f(X_i)$, the fish moves toward $X_{\max}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these behaviors matter:**
> Prey provides **exploitation** (local search), Swarm provides **cohesion** (avoid premature convergence), Follow provides **elite guidance** (convergence speed), and Move ensures **exploration** (global coverage).

---

## 2.2 Bacterial Foraging Optimization — Operational Mechanics

### 2.2.1 The E. coli Chemotaxis Model

An E. coli bacterium performs two motion primitives:

**Tumble:** Flagella rotate clockwise; the bacterium reorients randomly:
$$\theta(i) = \frac{\text{unit}(d-1) \cdot \pi}{\sqrt{d \cdot d-1}}$$

**Swim (Run):** Flagella rotate counter-clockwise; the bacterium moves along its current heading for $N_s$ steps:
$$\theta^i(j+1, k, l) = \theta^i(j, k, l) + C(i) \cdot \frac{\Delta(i)}{\sqrt{\Delta^T(i) \Delta(i)}}$$

where $\Delta(i) \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is a randomly generated direction vector, $C(i)$ is the step-size, and $\theta^i(j,k,l)$ denotes bacterium *i* at chemotactic step *j*, reproduction step *k*, elimination-dispersal step *l*.

### 2.2.2 Cell-to-Cell Attractant-Repellent Function

$$J_{cc}(\theta, P) = \sum_{i=1}^{S} J_{cc}^i(\theta, \theta^i)$$

$$J_{cc}^i(\theta, \theta^i) = -d_{\text{attract}} \exp\!\left(-w_{\text{attract}} \sum_{m=1}^{p}(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2\right) + h_{\text{repel}} \exp\!\left(-w_{\text{repel}} \sum_{m=1}^{p}(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2\right)$$

where:
- $d_{\text{attract}}$ = depth of attractant released
- $w_{\text{attract}}$ = width of attractant signal
- $h_{\text{repel}}$ = height of repellent effect
- $w_{\text{repel}}$ = width of repellent signal

The first term models **attraction** (aggregation), the second models **repulsion** (collision avoidance).

### 2.2.3 Reproduction and Elimination-Dispersal

**Reproduction (after $N_c$ chemotactic steps):**
Bacteria are sorted by accumulated health $J_{\text{health}}^i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_c+1} J(i,j,k,l)$. The lower $S_r = S/2$ bacteria die; the upper half split to form identical offspring.

**Elimination-Dispersal (after $N_{re}$ reproduction steps):**
Each bacterium is eliminated with probability $P_{ed}$ and reinitialized at a random location in the search space.

---

## 2.3 Intelligent Water Drop Algorithm — Operational Mechanics

### 2.3.1 The IWD Update Equations

Let an IWD currently carry soil = soil(IWD) and have velocity = vel(IWD). For traversal of edge *e* between nodes *i* and *j*:

**Velocity Update:**
$$\text{vel}^{\text{updated}}(IWD) = \text{vel}(IWD) + \frac{a_v}{b_v + c_v \cdot \text{soil}^{\alpha}(e)}$$

**Soil Update on Path:**
$$\Delta\text{soil}(e) = \frac{a_s}{b_s + c_s \cdot \text{time}^{\beta}(e; \text{vel}^{\text{updated}})}$$

where
$$\text{time}(e; \text{vel}) = \frac{\text{HU}(e)}{\text{vel}}$$

and **HU(e)** is the heuristic undesirability (edge length) between nodes.

**Soil Update of IWD:**
$$\text{soil}^{\text{updated}}(IWD) = \text{soil}(IWD) + \Delta\text{soil}(e)$$

### 2.3.2 Path Selection Probability

The IWD chooses the next node *j* from current *i* via:

$$p_e^{IWD}(i,j) = \frac{f(\text{soil}(i,j))}{\sum_{k \notin \text{vc}(IWD)} f(\text{soil}(i,k))}$$

where
$$f(\text{soil}(i,j)) = \frac{1}{\epsilon + g(\text{soil}(i,j))}, \quad g(\text{soil}(i,j)) = \begin{cases} \text{soil}(i,j) & \text{if } \min \\ -\text{soil}(i,j) & \text{if } \max \end{cases}$$

and **vc(IWD)** is the visited-construct tabu list.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> Lower-soil edges are chosen with higher probability — analogous to deeper river channels attracting more water.

### 2.3.3 Global Soil Update

After a complete IWD tour, the best-so-far path is rewarded by global soil reduction:

$$\text{soil}(e)^{\text{global}} = (1 - \rho) \cdot \text{soil}(e) - \rho \cdot \frac{1}{|T^{ib}|} \cdot \text{soil}^{ib}(e)$$

where $\rho$ is a learning factor, $T^{ib}$ is the iteration-best tour, and $\text{soil}^{ib}(e)$ is the soil of that IWD.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Algorithm | Equation | Purpose / Use |
|---|-----------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | AFSA | $X_{i}^{t+1} = X_i^t + \frac{X_j - X_i^t}{\Vert X_j - X_i^t \Vert} \cdot \text{Step} \cdot \text{rand}()$ | Prey movement toward food |
| 2 | AFSA | $X_c = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n_f} X_j}{n_f}$ | Center of swarm neighborhood |
| 3 | AFSA | Condition: $\frac{f(X_c)}{n_f} > \delta \cdot f(X_i)$ | Trigger swarm/follow behavior |
| 4 | BFO | $\theta^i(j+1,k,l) = \theta^i(j,k,l) + C(i)\frac{\Delta(i)}{\sqrt{\Delta^T(i)\Delta(i)}}$ | Chemotactic swim step |
| 5 | BFO | $J_{cc}^i = -d_{\text{attract}}e^{-w_{\text{attract}}\sum(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2} + h_{\text{repel}}e^{-w_{\text{repel}}\sum(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2}$ | Cell-to-cell attraction/repulsion |
| 6 | BFO | $J_{\text{health}}^i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_c+1} J(i,j,k,l)$ | Bacteria health for reproduction |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **AFSA** dominates continuous, multi-modal optimization (PID tuning, NN training).
> - **BFO** excels in dynamic, noisy environments (power dispatch, controller tuning).
> - **IWD** is exceptional for combinatorial problems (TSP, VRP, network routing).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Workflow Matrices

### 3.1.1 AFSA Algorithm — Step-by-Step Flow

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
Initialize $N$ fish with random positions $X_i$ in $[X_{\min}, X_{\max}]^d$. Set Visual, Step, $\delta$, Try_number.

**Step 2 — Behavior Evaluation:**
For each fish $X_i$, evaluate Prey, Swarm, and Follow behaviors. Select the action that yields the highest fitness improvement:

$$X_{i}^{\text{next}} = \arg\max\{f(\text{Af\_Prey}), f(\text{Af\_Swarm}), f(\text{Af\_Follow})\}$$

**Step 3 — Position Update:**
$$X_i^{t+1} = X_i^t + \frac{X_{i}^{\text{next}} - X_i^t}{\Vert X_{i}^{\text{next}} - X_i^t \Vert} \cdot \text{Step} \cdot \text{rand}()$$

**Step 4 — Bulletin Board Update:**
Record the global best $X^*$ and $f(X^*)$.

**Step 5 — Convergence Check:**
If $t < T_{\max}$ or fitness not converged, return to Step 2; else terminate.

---

### 3.1.2 BFO Algorithm — Step-by-Step Flow

The algorithm proceeds in nested loops: **Elimination-Dispersal → Reproduction → Chemotaxis**.

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
Initialize $S$ bacteria at random positions $\theta^i \in \mathbb{R}^p$, $i=1,\ldots,S$. Set $N_c$, $N_s$, $N_{re}$, $N_{ed}$, $P_{ed}$, $C(i)$.

**Step 2 — Chemotaxis Loop (for $j = 1$ to $N_c$):**

(a) Compute fitness including cell-to-cell attraction-repulsion:
$$J(i,j,k,l) = J(\theta^i(j,k,l)) + J_{cc}(\theta^i(j,k,l), P(j,k,l))$$

(b) **Tumble:** Generate random direction $\Delta(i)$:
$$\Delta_m(i) \in [-1, 1], \quad m = 1,\ldots,p$$

(c) **Move:**
$$\theta^i(j+1,k,l) = \theta^i(j,k,l) + C(i) \cdot \frac{\Delta(i)}{\sqrt{\Delta^T(i) \Delta(i)}}$$

(d) **Swim Loop:** While $J < J_{\text{last}}$ and swim length $< N_s$:
$$\theta^i(j+1,k,l) = \theta^i(j,k,l) + C(i) \cdot \frac{\Delta(i)}{\sqrt{\Delta^T(i) \Delta(i)}}$$

**Step 3 — Reproduction (after every $N_c$ chemotactic steps):**
Sort by $J_{\text{health}}^i$; lower half dies; upper half splits into two each.

**Step 4 — Elimination-Dispersal (after every $N_{re}$ reproductive steps):**
For each bacterium, with probability $P_{ed}$:
$$\theta^i = \text{rand position in feasible region}$$

---

### 3.1.3 IWD Algorithm — Step-by-Step Flow

**Step 1 — Problem Mapping:**
Map the problem to a graph $G = (V, E)$. Initialize all edge soils $\text{soil}(e) = \text{InitSoil}$.

**Step 2 — IWD Population Initialization:**
Create $N_{IWD}$ drops, each with initial $\text{soil}(IWD) = \text{InitSoil}$, $\text{velocity}(IWD) = \text{InitVel}$.

**Step 3 — Tour Construction (for each IWD):**

(a) At node *i*, select next node *j* via probability:
$$p_e^{IWD}(i,j) = \frac{f(\text{soil}(i,j))}{\sum_{k \notin \text{vc}} f(\text{soil}(i,k))}$$

(b) Update velocity:
$$\text{vel}^{\text{updated}} = \text{vel} + \frac{a_v}{b_v + c_v \cdot \text{soil}^{\alpha}(i,j)}$$

(c) Compute traversal time:
$$\text{time}(i,j;\text{vel}^{\text{updated}}) = \frac{\text{HU}(i,j)}{\text{vel}^{\text{updated}}}$$

(d) Compute soil removed from edge:
$$\Delta\text{soil}(i,j) = \frac{a_s}{b_s + c_s \cdot [\text{time}(i,j;\text{vel}^{\text{updated}})]^{\beta}}$$

(e) Update IWD's soil:
$$\text{soil}(IWD) \leftarrow \text{soil}(IWD) + \Delta\text{soil}(i,j)$$

(f) Update edge soil:
$$\text{soil}(i,j) \leftarrow \text{soil}(i,j) - \Delta\text{soil}(i,j)$$

**Step 4 — Global Update (after all IWDs complete):**
For edges in iteration-best tour:
$$\text{soil}(e) \leftarrow (1-\rho)\text{soil}(e) - \rho \cdot \frac{1}{\vert T^{ib}\vert} \cdot \text{soil}^{ib}(e)$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — IWD on a 4-Node TSP

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:**
> Apply IWD on a 4-city TSP: cities A, B, C, D with heuristic undesirabilities (distances) shown.

### Given Data
$$\text{HU}(A,B) = 4, \quad \text{HU}(A,C) = 3, \quad \text{HU}(A,D) = 5$$
$$\text{HU}(B,C) = 2, \quad \text{HU}(B,D) = 6, \quad \text{HU}(C,D) = 1$$

Parameters: $a_v = 1, b_v = 0.01, c_v = 1, a_s = 1, b_s = 0.01, c_s = 1, \alpha = 1, \beta = 1, \rho = 0.9, \text{InitSoil} = 100, \text{InitVel} = 1$.

### Iterative Computation (1st IWD, starting at A)

**Edge A→C selected (heuristically cheapest, lower soil-probability equivalent):**

Step (b) Velocity update:
$$\text{vel}^{\text{updated}} = 1 + \frac{1}{0.01 + 1 \cdot 100^1} = 1 + \frac{1}{100.01} = 1.0099$$

Step (c) Time:
$$\text{time}(A,C) = \frac{\text{HU}(A,C)}{\text{vel}^{\text{updated}}} = \frac{3}{1.0099} = 2.9704$$

Step (d) Soil removed from edge A→C:
$$\Delta\text{soil}(A,C) = \frac{1}{0.01 + 1 \cdot 2.9704^1} = \frac{1}{2.9804} = 0.3355$$

Step (e) IWD soil:
$$\text{soil}(IWD) = 100 + 0.3355 = 100.3355$$

Step (f) Edge soil:
$$\text{soil}(A,C) = 100 - 0.3355 = 99.6645$$

> [!TIP]
> This cycle repeats for every edge of the tour, after which the global update reinforces the best-so-far solution.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Performance Matrix

| Property | AFSA | BFO | IWD |
|----------|------|-----|-----|
| **Inspired by** | Fish schooling | E. coli chemotaxis | River formation |
| **Problem type** | Continuous optimization | Continuous optimization | Combinatorial optimization |
| **Search space** | $\mathbb{R}^n$ | $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Graph $(V,E)$ |
| **Communication** | Direct neighbor | Cell-to-cell signaling | Stigmergic (soil trail) |
| **Key parameter** | Visual, Step | $C(i), N_c, P_{ed}$ | $\rho, a_v, a_s, \beta$ |
| **Convergence** | Fast for unimodal | Slow but robust | Fast for TSP/VRP |
| **Premature convergence risk** | Moderate | Low | Low (due to global update) |
| **Exploration mechanism** | Move behavior | Elimination/dispersal | Velocity-based soil dynamics |
| **Strength** | Simple, robust | Handles noisy functions | Excellent for routing |
| **Weakness** | Slow on multimodal | Many parameters | Limited to graph problems |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall (KTU Valuation):**
> Many students confuse **chemotaxis** with "swim" alone. Chemotaxis = **Tumble + Swim** combined. Similarly, in IWD, the global update applies ONLY to the **iteration-best** IWD, not to every IWD.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Short Answer
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the four behaviors of an Artificial Fish in the AFSA algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

The Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA) implements four behaviors that drive the search:

1. **Prey Behavior (Af_Prey):** Mimics a fish moving toward regions of higher food concentration. The fish samples a random point within its visual range; if fitness improves, it advances; otherwise it tries again up to Try_number times.
2. **Swarm Behavior (Af_Swarm):** The fish computes the center of mass $X_c$ of its visible neighbors and moves toward it provided $\frac{f(X_c)}{n_f} > \delta f(X_i)$, ensuring cohesion.
3. **Follow Behavior (Af_Follow):** The fish identifies the best neighbor $X_{\max}$ (highest fitness in visual range) and moves toward it, accelerating convergence toward elite solutions.
4. **Move Behavior (Af_Move):** When no improvement is achievable, the fish performs a random walk, providing global exploration to escape local optima.

---

### Question 2 — Short Answer
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the three principal mechanisms of the Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO) algorithm and briefly state the role of elimination-dispersal.**

**Model Answer:**

The three principal mechanisms of BFO are:

1. **Chemotaxis** — combines *tumbling* (random direction change) and *swimming* (forward motion along that direction) guided by nutrient gradients; this implements local search.
2. **Reproduction** — after a fixed number of chemotactic steps, the worst half of bacteria are eliminated, and the best half are duplicated, mimicking Darwinian survival of the fittest.
3. **Elimination and Dispersal** — bacteria are stochastically removed with probability $P_{ed}$ and reinitialized at random positions.

**Role of Elimination-Dispersal:** It introduces **population diversity**, preventing premature convergence to local optima by enabling long-distance relocation of stagnated individuals. This is the only global exploration operator in BFO.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, every Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice between **Or-A** and **Or-B**. Both options are provided below.

---

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With neat pseudocode and equations, describe the **Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA)** in detail. Explain the role of *Visual*, *Step*, *Crowding Factor (δ)*, and *Try_number*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous optimization problem in 2-D has the objective:
$$f(x,y) = 4x + 3y - x^2 - y^2 + 5$$
Three fish are currently at positions $A(0,0)$, $B(1,0)$, and $C(0,1)$ with Visual $= 1.5$, Step $= 0.5$, $\delta = 0.5$. Compute the **Prey behavior** move of fish A toward B and verify if the fitness improves. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**AFSA Pseudocode:**

```
Initialize N fish with random positions X_i in feasible region
Set Visual, Step, delta, Try_number
For t = 1 to T_max:
    For each fish X_i:
        Compute fitness f(X_i)
        X_prey_result   = Af_Prey(X_i)
        X_swarm_result  = Af_Swarm(X_i)
        X_follow_result = Af_Follow(X_i)
        X_next = best of the three based on f()
        If no improvement after Try_number: X_next = Af_Move(X_i)
        Update X_i = X_next
    Update Bulletin Board: X* = argmax f(X_i)
Return X*
```

**Parameter Roles:**

| Parameter | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **Visual** | Defines the perception radius; larger → more global exploration, smaller → more local exploitation. |
| **Step** | Maximum displacement per iteration; controls the speed of convergence. |
| **δ (delta)** | Crowding factor; restricts excessive aggregation and prevents premature convergence. |
| **Try_number** | Maximum random samples in Prey; balances exploration vs. exploitation cost. |

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Given:** Visual $= 1.5$, Step $= 0.5$, $\delta = 0.5$, three fish at $A(0,0)$, $B(1,0)$, $C(0,1)$.

**Step 1 — Compute current fitnesses:**

$$f(A) = f(0,0) = 0 + 0 - 0 - 0 + 5 = 5$$

$$f(B) = f(1,0) = 4 + 0 - 1 - 0 + 5 = 8$$

$$f(C) = f(0,1) = 0 + 3 - 0 - 1 + 5 = 7$$

**Step 2 — Apply Prey Behavior from A toward B:**

Compute distance between A and B:

$$\Vert X_B - X_A \Vert = \sqrt{(1-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 1.0$$

Since $\Vert X_B - X_A \Vert = 1.0 < 1.5 = \text{Visual}$, fish B lies within A's visual range.

Generate a random unit vector $r \in [0,1]$ (assume $r = 0.6$):

$$X_A^{\text{next}} = X_A + \frac{X_B - X_A}{\Vert X_B - X_A \Vert} \cdot \text{Step} \cdot r$$

$$X_A^{\text{next}} = (0,0) + \frac{(1,0)}{1.0} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.6 = (0.30, 0)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate new fitness:**

$$f(0.30, 0) = 4(0.30) + 3(0) - (0.30)^2 - 0 + 5 = 1.20 - 0.09 + 5 = 6.11$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$\Delta f = 6.11 - 5 = +1.11 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Since fitness improved, fish A moves to $(0.30, 0)$. **Prey behavior is successful.**

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO)** algorithm. Discuss its four steps: **chemotaxis, swarming, reproduction, elimination-dispersal** with mathematical formulations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Intelligent Water Drop (IWD)** algorithm with its velocity and soil update equations. Show how the path selection probability $p_e^{IWD}(i,j)$ is computed and discuss the role of the global soil update. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**BFO Algorithm — Developed by Kevin Passino (2002).**

BFO mimics the foraging behavior of *E. coli* bacteria through four mechanisms:

**1. Chemotaxis (Tumble + Swim):**
The bacterium generates a random direction $\Delta(i)$ and moves with step size $C(i)$:

$$\theta^i(j+1,k,l) = \theta^i(j,k,l) + C(i) \cdot \frac{\Delta(i)}{\sqrt{\Delta^T(i) \Delta(i)}}$$

If fitness improves, it continues **swimming** in the same direction up to $N_s$ steps; otherwise, it **tumbles** again.

**2. Swarming (Cell-to-Cell Signaling):**
Bacteria release attractants and repellents that modulate movement:

$$J_{cc}(\theta, P) = \sum_{i=1}^{S} \left[-d_{\text{attract}} e^{-w_{\text{attract}}\sum_m(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2} + h_{\text{repel}} e^{-w_{\text{repel}}\sum_m(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2}\right]$$

The double exponential produces ring-shaped aggregation patterns mimicking bacterial swarms.

**3. Reproduction:**
After $N_c$ chemotactic steps, the **health** of each bacterium is computed:

$$J_{\text{health}}^i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_c+1} J(i,j,k,l)$$

Bacteria are sorted; the worst $S_r = S/2$ die, the best $S/2$ each split into two clones — preserving the best traits.

**4. Elimination-Dispersal:**
With probability $P_{ed}$, each bacterium is eliminated and randomly reinitialized:

$$\theta^i = \text{rand}(X_{\min}, X_{\max})$$

This mechanism prevents premature convergence and restores diversity.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Intelligent Water Drop (IWD) Algorithm — Hamed Shah-Hosseini (2007).**

The IWD algorithm models the formation of an optimal river path as water drops flow between two points, modifying the terrain (soil) as they travel.

**Velocity Update on traversing edge (i,j):**

$$\text{vel}^{\text{updated}}(IWD) = \text{vel}(IWD) + \frac{a_v}{b_v + c_v \cdot [\text{soil}(i,j)]^{\alpha}}$$

**Edge Traversal Time:**

$$\text{time}(i,j;\text{vel}^{\text{updated}}) = \frac{\text{HU}(i,j)}{\text{vel}^{\text{updated}}}$$

**Soil Removed from Edge:**

$$\Delta\text{soil}(i,j) = \frac{a_s}{b_s + c_s \cdot [\text{time}(i,j;\text{vel}^{\text{updated}})]^{\beta}}$$

**IWD Soil Update:**

$$\text{soil}(IWD) \leftarrow \text{soil}(IWD) + \Delta\text{soil}(i,j)$$

**Edge Soil Update:**

$$\text{soil}(i,j) \leftarrow \text{soil}(i,j) - \Delta\text{soil}(i,j)$$

**Path Selection Probability:**
At current node *i*, the next node *j* is chosen probabilistically (favoring low-soil edges):

$$p_e^{IWD}(i,j) = \frac{f(\text{soil}(i,j))}{\sum_{k \notin \text{vc}(IWD)} f(\text{soil}(i,k))}, \quad f(\text{soil}) = \frac{1}{\epsilon + \text{soil}(i,j)}$$

**Global Soil Update (Iteration-Best Reinforcement):**
After all $N_{IWD}$ drops complete their tours, the edges of the iteration-best tour receive reinforcement:

$$\text{soil}^{\text{global}}(e) = (1-\rho)\text{soil}(e) - \rho \cdot \frac{1}{\vert T^{ib}\vert} \cdot \text{soil}^{ib}(e)$$

**Role of Global Soil Update:** It **reinforces the best-so-far path** by depositing additional soil on its edges, biasing future IWDs toward high-quality solutions. The parameter $\rho$ controls the learning rate: higher $\rho$ produces faster convergence but risks premature convergence.

**Summary of Roles:**

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| Velocity update | Drops accelerate more on low-soil (good) edges |
| Soil removal | Erodes easy paths, accumulating soil in IWD |
| Path selection | Stigmergic mechanism — low-soil edges attract more flow |
| Global update | Reinforces globally best path |
| Visit tabu list vc | Prevents revisiting nodes (TSP enforcement) |

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 4 Specific):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Chemotaxis with Swim only** — Chemotaxis = Tumble + Swim. Just stating "swim" loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing cell-to-cell attraction and repulsion signs** — Attraction has a **negative** exponent (pulls together), repulsion has a **positive** exponent (pushes apart). Sign errors lose full marks in numerical questions.
> 3. **Missing the global update in IWD** — Some students only describe local edge soil updates. The **iteration-best global update** is a key 14-mark question component. Forgetting it = 3–4 marks lost.
> 4. **Confusing "Move" with "Prey" in AFSA** — Prey is **directed** (toward better fitness); Move is **random** (exploration). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **Forgetting Try_number in AFSA Prey behavior** — Prey does not run infinitely; it retries only Try_number times before falling back to Move.
> 6. **In BFO, stating "bacteria reproduce randomly"** — Reproduction is **deterministic**: the top half survive, the bottom half die. Randomness is only in **Elimination-Dispersal**.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **AFSA** = 4 behaviors: **Prey, Swarm, Follow, Move** (Prey → Swarm → Follow → Move in fallback order).
- [ ] **AFSA Prey equation:** $X_{i}^{t+1} = X_i^t + \frac{X_j - X_i^t}{\Vert X_j - X_i^t \Vert} \cdot \text{Step} \cdot \text{rand}()$
- [ ] **AFSA Center of Mass:** $X_c = \frac{\sum X_j}{n_f}$; use only if $\frac{f(X_c)}{n_f} > \delta f(X_i)$.
- [ ] **BFO Origin:** Kevin Passino (2002), based on *E. coli*.
- [ ] **BFO Chemotaxis** = Tumble + Swim; direction normalized by $\sqrt{\Delta^T \Delta}$.
- [ ] **BFO Cell-to-Cell function:** TWO exponentials — **attractant** ($-d_{\text{attract}}$) and **repellent** ($+h_{\text{repel}}$).
- [ ] **BFO Reproduction** = Top $S/2$ survive (split into clones); Bottom $S/2$ die.
- [ ] **BFO Elimination-Dispersal** = Random removal with probability $P_{ed}$.
- [ ] **IWD Origin:** Shah-Hosseini (2007), based on river formation.
- [ ] **IWD velocity update:** $\text{vel}^{\text{new}} = \text{vel} + \frac{a_v}{b_v + c_v \text{soil}^\alpha}$.
- [ ] **IWD soil removed from edge:** $\Delta\text{soil} = \frac{a_s}{b_s + c_s \text{time}^\beta}$.
- [ ] **IWD path probability:** Inverse of soil — **lower soil = higher probability**.
- [ ] **IWD global update:** Applies only to **iteration-best** $T^{ib}$, with learning factor $\rho$.
- [ ] **Engineering Apps:** AFSA→PID/continuous, BFO→noisy/power, IWD→TSP/VRP/routing.
- [ ] **All algorithms** are **population-based, meta-heuristic, derivative-free**, and tolerate **non-differentiable, multi-modal** objective functions.
- [ ] **AFSA** uses **direct neighbor** communication; **BFO** uses **cell-to-cell signaling**; **IWD** uses **stigmergic (soil-based)** indirect communication.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **AFSA = "PF-SM"** = **P**rey → **F**ollow → **S**warm → **M**ove (execution priority).
> - **BFO = "CSR-ED"** = **C**hemotaxis → **S**warming → **R**eproduction → **E**limination/**D**ispersal.
> - **IWD = "VS-PG"** = **V**elocity → **S**oil local → **P**ath prob → **G**lobal update.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies: ACO and PSO for NP-hard Problems, Routing Problems, Assignment Problems, Scheduling Problems, Subset Problems, Machine Learning Problems, Travelling Salesman Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT414 - NATURE INSPIRED COMPUTING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: ACO and PSO for NP-hard Problems, Routing Problems, Assignment Problems, Scheduling Problems, Subset Problems, Machine Learning Problems, Travelling Salesman Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nature-Inspired Computing (NIC)** refers to the family of optimization algorithms that derive their operational mechanics from biological, physical, or chemical phenomena observed in nature. Within Module 4, the focus is on **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** and **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** as **metaheuristic solvers for NP-hard combinatorial and continuous optimization problems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NP-Hard Problems (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> A decision/optimization problem $\Pi$ is classified as **NP-hard** if every problem in the class **NP** can be reduced to $\Pi$ in polynomial time. For such problems, no known deterministic algorithm guarantees an optimal solution in **polynomial time**. The search space grows **combinatorially** (e.g., factorially or exponentially) with problem size, making brute-force enumeration computationally infeasible for $n \geq 30$.

**ACO (Ant Colony Optimization)** — proposed by **Marco Dorigo** (1992) in his PhD thesis — is a **probabilistic, population-based metaheuristic** that searches for optimal paths through graphs by simulating the **stigmergic communication** of real ants depositing and following **pheromone trails**.

**PSO (Particle Swarm Optimization)** — proposed by **James Kennedy and Russell Eberhart** (1995) — is a **population-based, stochastic optimization technique** inspired by the **collective foraging behavior of bird flocks and fish schools**, where candidate solutions ("particles") navigate a multidimensional search space guided by personal and social best experiences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Metaheuristic vs. Heuristic**
> A **heuristic** is a problem-specific rule that finds a "good-enough" solution quickly. A **metaheuristic** is a higher-level, **problem-independent framework** that orchestrates heuristics to escape local optima. Both ACO and PSO are **metaheuristics** applicable across multiple NP-hard domains without algorithmic redesign.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

### 🍃 Intuition for ACO — "The Invisible Trail of Ants on a Picnic Blanket"

Imagine a **picnic blanket** scattered with sugar pieces at different distances from an ant colony. Initially, ants wander randomly. Ants that happen to find **shorter routes** to sugar return **faster** to the nest, depositing **pheromone** along the way. Over time, the shorter paths accumulate **more pheromone per unit time**, attracting even more ants. Longer paths receive less reinforcement and eventually evaporate. The colony **collectively converges** on the shortest path — even though **no single ant has global knowledge**.

> **Key Insight:** This is the essence of **stigmergy** — indirect coordination through environmental modification. Translating to NP-hard problems, cities in a TSP become "sugar sources" and pheromone intensities become the **collective memory** of the swarm.

### 🐦 Intuition for PSO — "The Flock of Starlings at Dusk"

Picture a **flock of starlings** performing an aerial ballet against a predator. Each bird adjusts its flight direction based on:
1. **Its own best-known position** (cognitive component — $p_{best}$).
2. **The flock's best-known position** (social component — $g_{best}$).

No bird knows the global optimum, yet the flock **swerves, accelerates, and converges** as a coherent unit. PSO applies the same principle: each **particle** is a candidate solution flying through a hyperspace of decision variables, constantly pulled toward personal and collective excellence.

> **Key Insight:** PSO is conceptually simpler than ACO — it uses **two position memories** ($p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$) instead of trail-based pheromone structures. This makes it exceptionally suited for **continuous, high-dimensional search spaces** such as neural network weight tuning.

## 1.3 Why ACO and PSO for NP-Hard Problems? — The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Polynomial-Time Approximation** | Both methods produce near-optimal solutions in reasonable time, satisfying the KTU-defined requirement for NP-hard solvers. |
| **Scalability** | Algorithms scale gracefully from $n=10$ to $n=10{,}000$ variables without exponential blow-up. |
| **Derivative-Free Optimization** | Neither requires gradient information — vital for non-differentiable, discrete, or black-box NP-hard problems. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Dec 2024 Pattern)**
> Module 4 of PECAT414 explicitly tests the ability to **map a specific NP-hard problem** to either ACO or PSO, **justify the choice**, and **describe the encoding/decoding scheme**. Memorize the encoding for TSP, Job-Shop Scheduling, and Quadratic Assignment.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of NP-Hard Problems Covered in Module 4

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists **seven canonical NP-hard problem classes**. The table below summarizes each, the standard benchmark dataset, and the preferred metaheuristic.

| # | NP-Hard Problem Class | Example Application | Preferred Metaheuristic | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Routing Problems** | Vehicle Routing (VRP), Network Routing | **ACO** | Naturally graph-structured |
| 2 | **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** | Logistics, PCB drilling | **ACO** (Dorigo's original test) | Edge-based pheromone fits tour construction |
| 3 | **Assignment Problems** | QAP, task-worker assignment | **ACO** (rank-based) | Discrete assignment encoded as graph |
| 4 | **Scheduling Problems** | Job-shop, flow-shop, RCPSP | **PSO** (discrete variant) | Continuous time-position encoding |
| 5 | **Subset Problems** | Knapsack, Set Cover, Feature Selection | **PSO (binary)** | Binary particle position = inclusion/exclusion |
| 6 | **Machine Learning Problems** | ANN training, SVM, clustering | **PSO** | Weight space is continuous and high-dimensional |
| 7 | **General NP-Hard Optimization** | SAT, Graph Coloring | **ACO + PSO hybrids** | Combining trail memory with social learning |

## 1.5 The "Combinatorial Explosion" — A Numerical Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why exact methods fail — the factorial barrier**
> For a TSP with $n$ cities, the number of possible tours is $\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$. Compute:
> - $n=10$: $181{,}440$ tours (tractable)
> - $n=20$: $\approx 6 \times 10^{16}$ tours (infeasible)
> - $n=50$: $\approx 3 \times 10^{62}$ tours (more than atoms in the universe)

A metaheuristic that examines only $10{,}000$ tours for $n=50$ is examining a fraction $10^{-58}$ of the search space, yet routinely finds solutions within **1–5% of the optimum**. **This is the practical justification for ACO and PSO.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) — Operational Mechanics

ACO operates as an **iterative construction graph search**. The four canonical algorithms in the ACO family are:

1. **Ant System (AS)** — Dorigo's original (1991).
2. **Ant Colony System (ACS)** — Dorigo & Gambardella (1997).
3. **Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)** — Stützle & Hoos (2000).
4. **Rank-Based Ant System (AS $_{\text{rank}}$)** — Bullnheimer, Hartl & Strauss (1999).

### 2.1.1 The Construction Graph

A problem is encoded as a **construction graph** $G = (C, L)$ where:
- $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_N\}$ is the set of **components** (e.g., cities in TSP).
- $L$ is the set of **connections** (e.g., edges $l_{ij}$ between cities $c_i$ and $c_j$).

A **candidate solution** is a **walk** through $G$ respecting problem-specific constraints. The pheromone $\tau_{ij}$ encodes the **learned desirability** of including connection $l_{ij}$.

### 2.1.2 The Pheromone Update Rule (Core ACO Engine)

After all $m$ ants have constructed their solutions, the pheromone trails are updated in **two phases**:

#### Phase 1: Pheromone Evaporation (Forgetting)

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t), \quad 0 < \rho \leq 1
$$

where $\rho$ is the **evaporation rate**. Evaporation prevents **premature convergence** by gradually forgetting poor historical choices.

#### Phase 2: Pheromone Deposition (Reinforcement)

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) \leftarrow \tau_{ij}(t+1) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

where the per-ant pheromone deposit is:

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ uses edge } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

with $Q$ being a **pheromone constant** and $L_k$ being the **tour length** of ant $k$.

### 2.1.3 The Probabilistic Transition Rule (Ant's Decision)

At each construction step, ant $k$ at component $c_i$ selects the next component $c_j$ with probability:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{c_l \in N_i^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}} & \text{if } j \in N_i^{k} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where:
- $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ is the **heuristic information** (inverse of distance).
- $N_i^{k}$ is the **feasible neighborhood** of ant $k$ at $c_i$.
- $\alpha \geq 0$ controls the influence of **pheromone** (learned knowledge).
- $\beta \geq 0$ controls the influence of **heuristic** (greedy information).

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameter Interpretation — KTU Exam Favorite**
> - $\alpha$ **high** → exploit known good trails (risk: stagnation).
> - $\beta$ **high** → act greedy (risk: local-optimum trap).
> - Balance $\alpha \approx \beta \approx 1$ is the empirical default.

## 2.2 Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) — Operational Mechanics

PSO maintains a **swarm** of $N$ particles, each representing a candidate solution. In an $D$-dimensional search space:

### 2.2.1 Position and Velocity Update

Each particle $i$ has:
- **Position vector:** $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{iD})$.
- **Velocity vector:** $\mathbf{v}_i = (v_{i1}, v_{i2}, \ldots, v_{iD})$.

**Velocity Update Equation:**

$$
v_{id}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1 \left(p_{id}^{best} - x_{id}(t)\right) + c_2 r_2 \left(g_{d}^{best} - x_{id}(t)\right)
$$

**Position Update Equation:**

$$
x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)
$$

where:
- $w$ is the **inertia weight** (typically $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$, linearly decreasing).
- $c_1$ is the **cognitive coefficient** (pull toward $p_{best}$).
- $c_2$ is the **social coefficient** (pull toward $g_{best}$).
- $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ are **uniformly distributed random** numbers.
- $p_{id}^{best}$ is the personal best position of particle $i$ in dimension $d$.
- $g_{d}^{best}$ is the global best position found by the swarm in dimension $d$.

### 2.2.2 Binary PSO (for Subset/Feature Selection)

For discrete problems, Kennedy & Eberhart (1997) proposed **Binary PSO (BPSO)**, where positions are constrained to $\{0, 1\}^D$. The velocity is mapped to a probability via the **sigmoid transfer function**:

$$
S(v_{id}) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-v_{id}}}
$$

and the position update becomes:

$$
x_{id}(t+1) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } r < S(v_{id}(t+1)) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}, \quad r \sim U(0,1)
$$

## 2.3 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY: No vertical pipes inside table cells. Use \vert or \mid.**

| Symbol | Name | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m$ | Number of ants | User-defined | $m = n$ to $2n$ for TSP |
| $N$ | Number of particles | User-defined | $20 \leq N \leq 100$ |
| $\alpha$ | Pheromone exponent | $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}$ in transition | $\alpha \in [0, 5]$, default $1$ |
| $\beta$ | Heuristic exponent | $[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$ in transition | $\beta \in [0, 5]$, default $2$ to $5$ |
| $\rho$ | Evaporation rate | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}$ | $\rho \in [0.01, 0.2]$ |
| $Q$ | Pheromone constant | Numerator of deposit | $Q \in [1, 10{,}000]$ |
| $\eta_{ij}$ | Heuristic info | $\eta_{ij} = 1 \vert d_{ij}$ | Distance-dependent |
| $w$ | Inertia weight | $w \cdot v_{id}$ term | $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ |
| $c_1$ | Cognitive coeff. | $c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x)$ | $c_1 \in [0, 2]$, typical $1.5$ |
| $c_2$ | Social coeff. | $c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x)$ | $c_2 \in [0, 2]$, typical $1.5$ |
| $r_1, r_2$ | Random numbers | $U(0,1)$ uniform | Each iteration, each dimension |
| $S(v)$ | Sigmoid | $(1+e^{-v})^{-1}$ | Range $(0, 1)$ |
| $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$ | Pheromone deposit | $Q / L_k$ for ant $k$ | Non-negative |
| $p_{ij}^{k}$ | Transition prob. | Proportional rule | Sum over $N_i^{k}$ equals $1$ |
| $L_k$ | Tour length | Sum of edge costs | Minimization objective |

## 2.4 Problem-Specific Encoding Schemes (Critical for KTU Answers)

### 2.4.1 Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) — ACO Encoding

- **Component:** city.
- **Construction graph:** complete graph $K_n$.
- **Pheromone:** $\tau_{ij}$ on edge $(i,j)$.
- **Heuristic:** $\eta_{ij} = 1 / d_{ij}$.
- **Solution:** Hamiltonian cycle (each city visited once).
- **Cost function:** $L_k = \sum_{(i,j) \in T_k} d_{ij}$.

> **Why ACO wins here:** Pheromone reinforcement on edges naturally biases subsequent ants toward short cycles — exactly the combinatorial structure being optimized.

### 2.4.2 Job-Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP) — PSO Encoding

- **Component:** operation (job, machine, sequence).
- **Particle position:** priority-based permutation of operations.
- **Decoding:** convert priority list to feasible schedule using **Giffler-Thompson** algorithm.
- **Cost function:** makespan $C_{max} = \max_j \{C_{j,n_j}\}$.

> **Why PSO wins here:** Continuous priority values can be ranked to produce high-quality schedules, and PSO's social learning accelerates convergence in highly constrained disjunctive graphs.

### 2.4.3 Quadratic Assignment Problem (QAP) — ACO Encoding

- **Component:** facility $i$ assigned to location $j$.
- **Pheromone:** $\tau_{ij}$ represents learned preference of placing facility $i$ at location $j$.
- **Cost function:** $F(\pi) = \sum_{i,j} a_{ij} \cdot b_{\pi(i)\pi(j)}$ where $a$ is the flow matrix and $b$ is the distance matrix.

### 2.4.4 0/1 Knapsack Problem — Binary PSO Encoding

- **Component:** item.
- **Particle position:** binary vector $x_i \in \{0,1\}^n$ where $x_{id} = 1$ means item $d$ is selected.
- **Penalty function:** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{d} v_d x_{id} - \lambda \cdot \max(0, \sum_{d} w_d x_{id} - W)$ where $W$ is capacity and $\lambda$ is a penalty coefficient.

### 2.4.5 Neural Network Training — PSO as Weight Optimizer

- **Particle position:** vector of all network weights and biases.
- **Particle dimension:** $D = (n_{inputs} \times n_{hidden}) + n_{hidden} + (n_{hidden} \times n_{outputs}) + n_{outputs}$.
- **Cost function:** mean squared error $E = \frac{1}{P} \sum_{p=1}^{P} \sum_{k=1}^{K} (y_{k}^{(p)} - t_{k}^{(p)})^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage over Backpropagation:** PSO does not require differentiable activation functions and is **less prone to local minima** because of swarm-based exploration.

## 2.5 Convergence Properties — Theoretical Guarantees

| Algorithm | Convergence Property | Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ant System (AS)** | Converges to best solution with probability approaching 1 as $t \to \infty$ (Gutjahr, 2000 — Graph-based AS) | Stochastic convergence |
| **ACS / MMAS** | Provably converges; guarantees that best-so-far solution is monotonically improved | Stützle & Dorigo (2002) |
| **PSO (standard)** | Does not guarantee convergence to global optimum; converges to a stable point in continuous domains | Clerc & Kennedy (2002) — stagnation analysis |
| **Binary PSO** | Markov chain analysis shows asymptotic convergence under fixed probability bounds | Kennedy & Eberhart (1997) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ACO Pheromone Update Convergence

We derive the steady-state behavior of a single edge $(i,j)$ under repeated deposition by the best-found ant.

### Setup

Let $\tau_{ij}^{(t)}$ denote the pheromone level on edge $(i,j)$ after iteration $t$. The update rule is:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{(t+1)} = (1 - \rho) \tau_{ij}^{(t)} + \Delta \tau_{ij}^{best}
$$

Assume the best ant deposits $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{best} = \frac{Q}{L^*}$ (where $L^*$ is the best-known tour length) **only if** edge $(i,j)$ belongs to the best tour.

### Recursive Unrolling

Expanding the recursion:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{(1)} = (1-\rho) \tau_{ij}^{(0)} + \Delta \tau_{ij}^{best}
$$

$$
\tau_{ij}^{(2)} = (1-\rho)^2 \tau_{ij}^{(0)} + (1-\rho) \Delta \tau_{ij}^{best} + \Delta \tau_{ij}^{best}
$$

$$
\tau_{ij}^{(t)} = (1-\rho)^t \tau_{ij}^{(0)} + \Delta \tau_{ij}^{best} \sum_{s=0}^{t-1} (1-\rho)^s
$$

### Geometric Series Identification

Recognize the finite geometric series:

$$
\sum_{s=0}^{t-1} (1-\rho)^s = \frac{1 - (1-\rho)^t}{1 - (1-\rho)} = \frac{1 - (1-\rho)^t}{\rho}
$$

### Closed-Form Steady State

Substituting:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{(t)} = (1-\rho)^t \tau_{ij}^{(0)} + \frac{\Delta \tau_{ij}^{best}}{\rho} \left[1 - (1-\rho)^t\right]
$$

As $t \to \infty$, the term $(1-\rho)^t \to 0$ (since $0 < \rho < 1$), yielding:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{*} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \tau_{ij}^{(t)} = \frac{\Delta \tau_{ij}^{best}}{\rho} = \frac{Q}{\rho L^*}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The pheromone on a "best-tour" edge stabilizes at $\dfrac{Q}{\rho L^*}$. Smaller $L^*$ → larger steady-state pheromone → stronger reinforcement. This confirms the **self-reinforcing cycle** of good solutions accumulating more pheromone.

## 3.2 Derivation: PSO Velocity Update Stability Analysis (Clerc & Kennedy)

We derive the conditions under which a particle's trajectory remains bounded (does not diverge to infinity).

### One-Dimensional Simplified PSO

Ignoring randomness by setting $r_1 = r_2 = r$ and $\phi_1 = c_1 r$, $\phi_2 = c_2 r$:

$$
v(t+1) = w v(t) + \phi_1 (p_{best} - x(t)) + \phi_2 (g_{best} - x(t))
$$

$$
x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)
$$

### Homogeneous Recurrence

Define the focus point $y = \frac{\phi_1 p_{best} + \phi_2 g_{best}}{\phi_1 + \phi_2}$ and let $\phi = \phi_1 + \phi_2$. Then:

$$
x(t+1) = w x(t) - \phi x(t) + (\phi_1 + \phi_2) y + w v(t) - w x(t) + x(t)
$$

After simplification (substituting $v(t) = x(t) - x(t-1)$):

$$
x(t+1) = (1 + w - \phi) x(t) - w x(t-1) + \phi y
$$

### Characteristic Equation

The homogeneous part $x(t+1) = (1 + w - \phi) x(t) - w x(t-1)$ has characteristic polynomial:

$$
\lambda^2 - (1 + w - \phi) \lambda + w = 0
$$

Solving for eigenvalues:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{(1+w-\phi) \pm \sqrt{(1+w-\phi)^2 - 4w}}{2}
$$

### Stability Condition

For **bounded** (non-divergent) trajectories, the eigenvalues must lie **inside the unit disk** in the complex plane. The necessary and sufficient condition (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002) is:

$$
0 < w < 1, \quad 0 < \phi < 4(1 + w)
$$

The **constriction coefficient** $\chi$ method sets $\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$ to ensure stability without parameter tuning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The standard recommendation is $\chi = 0.7298$ with $c_1 = c_2 = 1.49618$, which gives $\phi = 2.99236 < 4$. This combination is the **de facto default** in PSO literature.

## 3.3 Derivation: ACO Convergence Probability (Gutjahr's Graph-Based Ant System)

Gutjahr (2000) proved convergence for a modified ACO variant where the pheromone update is restricted to the iteration-best ant. The proof structure (paraphrased):

1. **Model the search** as a Markov chain over the set of all feasible solutions.
2. **Define** $P_{opt}(t)$ = probability that the optimal solution has been found by time $t$.
3. **Show** that at every iteration, there is a **positive probability** $p_{min} > 0$ that some ant constructs the optimal solution.
4. **Apply** the Borel-Cantelli lemma: $P_{opt}(t) \to 1$ as $t \to \infty$.

The key inequality established is:

$$
1 - P_{opt}(T) \leq (1 - p_{min})^T
$$

Hence:

$$
P_{opt}(T) \geq 1 - (1 - p_{min})^T \to 1
$$

> **Interpretation:** With **enough iterations**, ACO is **almost sure** to find the optimum. The convergence rate depends on $p_{min}$, which is influenced by the pheromone bias and the heuristic structure.

## 3.4 Derivation: Binary PSO Probability of Selecting Feature $d$

In feature selection (subset problem), the probability that particle $i$ selects feature $d$ is:

$$
P(x_{id} = 1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v_{id}}}
$$

We can derive the **expected number of selected features** per particle:

$$
E[\text{features selected}] = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v_{id}}}
$$

As $v_{id} \to +\infty$, the sigmoid saturates to $1$ (feature included). As $v_{id} \to -\infty$, it saturates to $0$ (excluded). The **sparsity** of the solution is controlled by the **velocity magnitude distribution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **V-shape transfer function (modern alternative):** Rashedi et al. (2009) proposed $P(x_{id}=1) = \vert \text{erf}(\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} v_{id}) \vert$ for improved binary PSO performance.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Numerical Example: ACO on a 4-City TSP

Let the cities be $A, B, C, D$ with the **distance matrix**:

| From\To | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **$A$** | $0$ | $2$ | $9$ | $10$ |
| **$B$** | $2$ | $0$ | $7$ | $12$ |
| **$C$** | $9$ | $7$ | $0$ | $5$ |
| **$D$** | $10$ | $12$ | $5$ | $0$ |

### Step 1: Compute Heuristic Matrix $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$

| From\To | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **$A$** | $-$ | $0.500$ | $0.111$ | $0.100$ |
| **$B$** | $0.500$ | $-$ | $0.143$ | $0.083$ |
| **$C$** | $0.111$ | $0.143$ | $-$ | $0.200$ |
| **$D$** | $0.100$ | $0.083$ | $0.200$ | $-$ |

### Step 2: Initialize Pheromone

Set $\tau_{ij}(0) = 0.1$ for all edges.

### Step 3: Ant $k=1$ Constructs a Tour (start at $A$, $\alpha=1, \beta=2$)

At $A$, the unvisited cities are $\{B, C, D\}$. Compute attractiveness $\tau^{\alpha} \cdot \eta^{\beta}$:

- $A \to B$: $0.1^1 \cdot 0.5^2 = 0.025$
- $A \to C$: $0.1^1 \cdot 0.111^2 = 0.00123$
- $A \to D$: $0.1^1 \cdot 0.1^2 = 0.001$

Sum $= 0.02723$. Probabilities:

- $P(A \to B) = 0.025 / 0.02723 = 0.918$
- $P(A \to C) = 0.00123 / 0.02723 = 0.0452$
- $P(A \to D) = 0.001 / 0.02723 = 0.0367$

Ant chooses $B$ (high probability). Continue to $B$ with unvisited $\{C, D\}$:

- $B \to C$: $0.1 \cdot 0.143^2 = 0.00204$
- $B \to D$: $0.1 \cdot 0.083^2 = 0.000694$

Sum $= 0.00274$. $P(B \to C) = 0.745$. Ant chooses $C$. Then $C \to D$ (forced). Tour: $A \to B \to C \to D \to A$.

Tour length: $2 + 7 + 5 + 10 = 24$.

### Step 4: Pheromone Update (assume single ant, $\rho=0.1, Q=10$)

Evaporation: $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow 0.9 \cdot 0.1 = 0.09$ for all edges.

Deposit on $A \to B$: $\tau_{AB} = 0.09 + 10/24 = 0.09 + 0.4167 = 0.5067$.

Repeat for all $m$ ants and iterate. The pheromone on the **optimal tour edges** ($A$-$B$-$C$-$D$ might not be optimal — let's check: optimal tour is $A \to B \to C \to D \to A$ with $L=24$, or $A \to D \to C \to B \to A$ with $L = 10+5+7+2 = 24$ — same!) will dominate after several iterations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Insight:** With this 4-city symmetric instance, there are $\frac{3!}{2} = 3$ distinct tours. The pheromone bias will eventually concentrate on edges that appear in **all** optimal tours, demonstrating **collective intelligence**.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: ACO Meta-Algorithm (Module 4 Core)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start ACO] --> initA[Initialize Parameters m, alpha, beta, rho, Q]
    initA --> initTau[Initialize Pheromone tau_ij = tau_0 for all edges]
    initTau --> placeAnts[Place m ants on random cities]
    placeAnts --> constructA[Each ant constructs solution using transition rule]
    constructA --> evalA[Evaluate tour cost L_k for each ant]
    evalA --> findBest[Identify iteration-best ant and global-best ant]
    findBest --> evap[Pheromone Evaporation: tau_ij = 1-rho times tau_ij]
    evap --> deposit[Pheromone Deposition: best ants add Q / L_k]
    deposit --> checkA{Has t reached t_max or convergence criterion?}
    checkA -->|No| constructA
    checkA -->|Yes| outputA[Output global-best solution]
    outputA --> stopA[Stop]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: PSO Meta-Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startP[Start PSO] --> initP[Initialize swarm of N particles with random positions and velocities]
    initP --> evalP[Evaluate fitness f_x_i for each particle]
    evalP --> initPbest[Set p_best_i = x_i and g_best = best particle]
    initPbest --> velUpdate[Update velocity using cognitive and social terms]
    velUpdate --> posUpdate[Update position x_i = x_i + v_i]
    posUpdate --> bound[Apply boundary constraints on position and velocity]
    bound --> evalP2[Re-evaluate fitness f_x_i]
    evalP2 --> updPbest[Update p_best_i if f_x_i is better]
    updPbest --> updGbest[Update g_best if any p_best is better]
    updGbest --> checkP{Has t reached t_max or tolerance?}
    checkP -->|No| velUpdate
    checkP -->|Yes| outputP[Output g_best as optimal solution]
    outputP --> stopP[Stop]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: ACO Pheromone Update Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph pre[Pre-Update State]
        tauPrev[Pheromone tau_ij at iteration t]
        solSet[Set of ant solutions S_1 S_2 ... S_m]
        costSet[Corresponding costs L_1 L_2 ... L_m]
    end

    pre --> evapMod[Evaporation Module: tau_temp = 1-rho times tau_ij]
    evapMod --> rankMod[Rank ants by solution quality]
    rankMod --> selectMod[Select best-w ants for deposition]
    selectMod --> depMod[Compute deposit Delta tau = sigma of Q divided by L_k for best ants]
    depMod --> addMod[Add deposit: tau_ij at t+1 = tau_temp + Delta tau]
    addMod --> clipMod[Clip tau_ij to tau_min and tau_max bounds]
    clipMod --> nextIter[Output updated tau_ij for iteration t+1]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: PSO Velocity Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        gBest[Global best g_best_id]
        r1[Random r_1 in 0,1]
        r2[Random r_2 in 0,1]
    end

    subgraph terms[Three Force Components]
        t1[Inertia term: w times v_id]
        t2[Cognitive term: c_1 times r_1 times p_best minus x_id]
        t3[Social term: c_2 times r_2 times g_best minus x_id]
    end

    subgraph sum[Aggregation]
        vNew[New velocity v_id at t+1 = t1 + t2 + t3]
        xNew[New position x_id at t+1 = x_id + v_id at t+1]
    end

    vCurr --> t1
    pBest --> t2
    gBest --> t3
    xCurr --> t2
    xCurr --> t3
    r1 --> t2
    r2 --> t3

    t1 --> vNew
    t2 --> vNew
    t3 --> vNew
    vNew --> xNew
    xCurr --> xNew
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define NP-hard problem. Justify why Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) is classified as NP-hard. Mention one metaheuristic that is most commonly applied to TSP. [3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A problem $\Pi$ is **NP-hard** if every problem in NP can be reduced to $\Pi$ in polynomial time, with no known polynomial-time algorithm guaranteed to find the optimal solution.

**TSP is NP-hard** because:
1. The number of possible tours is $\frac{(n-1)!}{2}$, which grows **factorially** with $n$.
2. The decision version of TSP ("Is there a tour of length $\leq K$?") is **NP-complete**.
3. Many NP-complete problems (e.g., Hamiltonian Cycle) reduce to TSP.

**Metaheuristic applied:** **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** — specifically, the Ant System (AS) and its variants ACS, MMAS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for NP-hard definition, 1 mark for TSP justification, 1 mark for metaheuristic name.

---

### **Question 2: List the three main parameters of the Ant Colony Optimization transition rule and explain the role of each. [3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The transition rule is $p_{ij}^{k} \propto [\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$. The three parameters are:

| Parameter | Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\alpha$ | Controls the influence of **pheromone trail** $\tau_{ij}$. Higher $\alpha$ → more exploitation of learned knowledge. |
| $\beta$ | Controls the influence of **heuristic information** $\eta_{ij}$ (usually $1/d_{ij}$). Higher $\beta$ → more greedy, myopic behavior. |
| $\rho$ | **Evaporation rate** applied during the pheromone update. Higher $\rho$ → faster forgetting of old trails → more exploration. |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **ESE Module 4 Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Students must answer EITHER OR.

---

### **Question A: ACO for the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — `[14 Marks]`

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a): Explain the encoding of the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) as a construction graph for ACO. Define the transition rule and the pheromone update rule with all parameters. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Construction Graph Encoding:**

For a TSP with $n$ cities $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$, the construction graph is the **complete graph** $K_n$:
- **Components (vertices):** the $n$ cities.
- **Connections (edges):** the $n(n-1)/2$ pairwise connections $l_{ij}$ between cities $c_i$ and $c_j$.
- **Constraints:** each city must be visited **exactly once** (Hamiltonian cycle constraint).

**Step 2 — Pheromone Definition:**

A pheromone value $\tau_{ij}$ is associated with each edge $(i,j)$, representing the **learned desirability** of traversing that edge.

**Step 3 — Heuristic Information:**

$$
\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}
$$

where $d_{ij}$ is the Euclidean or given distance between cities $i$ and $j$.

**Step 4 — Transition Rule (Ant's Decision):**

At city $c_i$, ant $k$ chooses the next city $c_j \in N_i^{k}$ with probability:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{c_l \in N_i^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}
$$

where $N_i^{k}$ is the set of unvisited cities (feasible neighborhood).

**Step 5 — Pheromone Update Rule:**

After all $m$ ants complete their tours:

**Evaporation:**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)
$$

**Deposition (iteration-best strategy):**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t+1) + \Delta \tau_{ij}^{best}, \quad \Delta \tau_{ij}^{best} = \begin{cases} \frac{Q}{L_{best}} & \text{if edge } (i,j) \in T_{best} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Parameter Summary:**

- $\alpha$ — pheromone influence exponent.
- $\beta$ — heuristic influence exponent.
- $\rho \in (0,1]$ — evaporation rate.
- $Q$ — pheromone constant.
- $m$ — number of ants.
- $L_{best}$ — best tour length in current iteration.

#### Part (b): A TSP instance has 5 cities with the following symmetric distance matrix (lower triangle shown). Apply the Ant System (AS) for ONE iteration with $m=2$ ants, $\alpha=1$, $\beta=1$, $\rho=0.1$, $Q=1$, initial pheromone $\tau_0 = 0.1$ on all edges. Ant 1 starts at city 1 and Ant 2 starts at city 3. Show the transition probabilities and compute the updated pheromone. [7 Marks]

|  | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **$1$** | $-$ | $4$ | $1$ | $8$ | $6$ |
| **$2$** | $4$ | $-$ | $3$ | $5$ | $2$ |
| **$3$** | $1$ | $3$ | $-$ | $7$ | $9$ |
| **$4$** | $8$ | $5$ | $7$ | $-$ | $2$ |
| **$5$** | $6$ | $2$ | $9$ | $2$ | $-$ |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Heuristic matrix $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$:**

|  | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **$1$** | $-$ | $0.25$ | $1.00$ | $0.125$ | $0.167$ |
| **$2$** | $0.25$ | $-$ | $0.333$ | $0.200$ | $0.500$ |
| **$3$** | $1.00$ | $0.333$ | $-$ | $0.143$ | $0.111$ |
| **$4$** | $0.125$ | $0.200$ | $0.143$ | $-$ | $0.500$ |
| **$5$** | $0.167$ | $0.500$ | $0.111$ | $0.500$ | $-$ |

**Step 2: Ant 1 starts at city 1, all edges have $\tau=0.1$, $\alpha=\beta=1$.**

Attractiveness at city 1 to unvisited $\{2,3,4,5\}$:

- $1 \to 2$: $0.1 \times 0.25 = 0.025$
- $1 \to 3$: $0.1 \times 1.00 = 0.100$
- $1 \to 4$: $0.1 \times 0.125 = 0.0125$
- $1 \to 5$: $0.1 \times 0.167 = 0.0167$

Sum = $0.1542$.

Probabilities:
- $P(1 \to 2) = 0.025 / 0.1542 = 0.1621$
- $P(1 \to 3) = 0.100 / 0.1542 = 0.6485$
- $P(1 \to 4) = 0.0125 / 0.1542 = 0.0811$
- $P(1 \to 5) = 0.0167 / 0.1542 = 0.1083$

Assume Ant 1 chooses **city 3** (highest probability). Now at city 3, unvisited $\{2,4,5\}$:

- $3 \to 2$: $0.1 \times 0.333 = 0.0333$
- $3 \to 4$: $0.1 \times 0.143 = 0.0143$
- $3 \to 5$: $0.1 \times 0.111 = 0.0111$

Sum = $0.0587$.

Probabilities:
- $P(3 \to 2) = 0.0333 / 0.0587 = 0.5674$
- $P(3 \to 4) = 0.0143 / 0.0587 = 0.2436$
- $P(3 \to 5) = 0.0111 / 0.0587 = 0.1891$

Ant 1 chooses **city 2** (highest). Now at city 2, unvisited $\{4,5\}$:

- $2 \to 4$: $0.1 \times 0.200 = 0.0200$
- $2 \to 5$: $0.1 \times 0.500 = 0.0500$

Sum = $0.0700$.

Probabilities:
- $P(2 \to 4) = 0.0200 / 0.0700 = 0.2857$
- $P(2 \to 5) = 0.0500 / 0.0700 = 0.7143$

Ant 1 chooses **city 5**. Then forced to city 4. Ant 1's tour: $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 5 \to 4 \to 1$.

Tour length: $L_1 = 1 + 3 + 2 + 2 + 8 = 16$.

**Step 3: Ant 2 starts at city 3.**

By symmetry of identical $\tau$ and similar heuristic weights, after the same construction logic, assume Ant 2 produces tour: $3 \to 1 \to 2 \to 5 \to 4 \to 3$.

Tour length: $L_2 = 1 + 4 + 2 + 2 + 7 = 16$.

**Step 4: Pheromone Evaporation** ($\rho = 0.1$):

$$
\tau_{ij} \leftarrow 0.9 \times 0.1 = 0.09 \text{ for all edges}
$$

**Step 5: Pheromone Deposition (assuming both ants deposit since $L_1 = L_2$):**

For each edge in the two tours, add $Q/L_k = 1/16 = 0.0625$.

Ant 1's edges: $(1,3), (2,3), (2,5), (4,5), (1,4)$.
Ant 2's edges: $(1,3), (1,2), (2,5), (4,5), (3,4)$.

**Updated pheromone:**

| Edge | Evaporated | Ant deposit | Final $\tau$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(1,3)$ | $0.09$ | $0.0625 + 0.0625$ | $0.215$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $0.09$ | $0.0625$ | $0.1525$ |
| $(2,3)$ | $0.09$ | $0.0625$ | $0.1525$ |
| $(2,5)$ | $0.09$ | $0.0625 + 0.0625$ | $0.215$ |
| $(4,5)$ | $0.09$ | $0.0625 + 0.0625$ | $0.215$ |
| $(1,4)$ | $0.09$ | $0.0625$ | $0.1525$ |
| $(3,4)$ | $0.09$ | $0.0625$ | $0.1525$ |
| All other edges | $0.09$ | $0$ | $0.09$ |

> **Final Pheromone Matrix (updated):**

|  | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **$1$** | $-$ | $0.1525$ | $0.215$ | $0.1525$ | $0.09$ |
| **$2$** | $0.1525$ | $-$ | $0.1525$ | $0.09$ | $0.215$ |
| **$3$** | $0.215$ | $0.1525$ | $-$ | $0.1525$ | $0.09$ |
| **$4$** | $0.1525$ | $0.09$ | $0.1525$ | $-$ | $0.215$ |
| **$5$** | $0.09$ | $0.215$ | $0.09$ | $0.215$ | $-$ |

**Observation:** Edges appearing in BOTH tours $(1,3), (2,5), (4,5)$ have the highest pheromone, demonstrating ACO's **concentration behavior**.

### **Question B: PSO for Job-Shop Scheduling and Subset Problems** — `[14 Marks]`

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a): Explain how Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) can be applied to the Job-Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP). Describe the particle encoding, decoding, and fitness function. Discuss the advantages of using PSO over ACO for scheduling. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — JSSP Definition:**

The Job-Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP) schedules $J$ jobs on $M$ machines. Each job $j$ has $n_j$ operations, and operation $o_{j,m}$ must be processed on machine $m$ for duration $p_{j,m}$. The objective is to **minimize the makespan** $C_{max} = \max_j \{C_{j,n_j}\}$.

**Step 2 — Particle Encoding (Priority-Based Representation):**

A particle's position is a **vector of priority values** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$ where $N$ is the total number of operations and $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$. Each operation $o_i$ is assigned a real-valued priority. The **rank order** of priorities determines the **processing sequence** on each machine.

**Step 3 — Decoding (Giffler-Thompson Algorithm):**

The continuous priorities are sorted in descending order to produce a **priority list**, which the Giffler-Thompson scheduler converts into a feasible schedule:

1. Identify the earliest completion time on each machine.
2. Select the machine with the earliest completion.
3. Among jobs contending for that machine, select the one with the **highest priority**.
4. Schedule the operation; update completion times.
5. Repeat until all operations are scheduled.

**Step 4 — Fitness Function:**

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = C_{max} = \max_{j} \left\{ \text{completion time of job } j \right\}
$$

**Step 5 — PSO Updates:**

The standard velocity and position updates apply directly in the continuous priority space:

$$
v_{id}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{id}^{best} - x_{id}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (g_{d}^{best} - x_{id}(t))
$$

$$
x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)
$$

**Step 6 — Advantages of PSO over ACO for JSSP:**

| Aspect | PSO | ACO |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Encoding** | Continuous priority vector — natural fit for ordering | Requires pheromone matrix — awkward for sequencing |
| **Constraints** | Decoding handles feasibility automatically | Feasibility constraints in graph construction are complex |
| **Convergence** | Faster on smooth priority landscapes | Slower due to discrete probabilistic steps |
| **Parameter tuning** | Only $w, c_1, c_2$ | More parameters: $\alpha, \beta, \rho, m$ |
| **Memory** | Single $g_{best}$ and $p_{best}$ | Distributed pheromone matrix |

#### Part (b): Apply Binary PSO to a 0/1 Knapsack Problem with 5 items. Item values $v = (60, 100, 120, 80, 50)$, weights $w = (10, 20, 30, 15, 5)$, capacity $W = 40$. Show one iteration of Binary PSO with two particles using the sigmoid transfer function. Use $\alpha=1, c_1=c_2=1$, $w=0.5$, and penalty $\lambda=10$ for overweight solutions. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Initialization.**

Two particles, each with 5 dimensions (one per item).

**Particle 1:** Position $\mathbf{x}_1 = (0, 1, 1, 0, 1)$, velocity $\mathbf{v}_1 = (0.3, -0.5, 0.8, 0.2, -0.4)$.
**Particle 2:** Position $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$, velocity $\mathbf{v}_2 = (-0.2, 0.6, 0.4, -0.3, 0.5)$.

**Step 2: Fitness evaluation (penalty function $f = \sum v_d x_d - \lambda \cdot \max(0, \sum w_d x_d - W)$).**

**Particle 1:** $\mathbf{x}_1 = (0, 1, 1, 0, 1)$.
- Value: $0 + 100 + 120 + 0 + 50 = 270$.
- Weight: $0 + 20 + 30 + 0 + 5 = 55$.
- Excess: $55 - 40 = 15$.
- Penalty: $\lambda \times 15 = 10 \times 15 = 150$.
- Fitness: $f_1 = 270 - 150 = 120$.

**Particle 2:** $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$.
- Value: $60 + 100 + 0 + 80 + 0 = 240$.
- Weight: $10 + 20 + 0 + 15 + 0 = 45$.
- Excess: $45 - 40 = 5$.
- Penalty: $10 \times 5 = 50$.
- Fitness: $f_2 = 240 - 50 = 190$.

**Step 3: Set personal bests and global best.**

- $p_{best,1} = (0, 1, 1, 0, 1)$, $f(p_{best,1}) = 120$.
- $p_{best,2} = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$, $f(p_{best,2}) = 190$.
- $g_{best} = p_{best,2} = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$, $f(g_{best}) = 190$.

**Step 4: Velocity update (assume $r_1 = r_2 = 0.5$ for simplicity).**

**For Particle 1, dimension $d=1$ (item 1):**
- $v_{1,1} = 0.5 \times 0.3 + 1 \times 0.5 \times (0 - 0) + 1 \times 0.5 \times (1 - 0) = 0.15 + 0 + 0.5 = 0.65$

**For Particle 1, dimension $d=2$:**
- $v_{1,2} = 0.5 \times (-0.5) + 1 \times 0.5 \times (1 - 1) + 1 \times 0.5 \times (1 - 1) = -0.25 + 0 + 0 = -0.25$

**For Particle 1, dimension $d=3$:**
- $v_{1,3} = 0.5 \times 0.8 + 1 \times 0.5 \times (1 - 1) + 1 \times 0.5 \times (0 - 1) = 0.40 + 0 - 0.5 = -0.10$

**For Particle 1, dimension $d=4$:**
- $v_{1,4} = 0.5 \times 0.2 + 1 \times 0.5 \times (0 - 0) + 1 \times 0.5 \times (1 - 0) = 0.10 + 0 + 0.5 = 0.60$

**For Particle 1, dimension $d=5$:**
- $v_{1,5} = 0.5 \times (-0.4) + 1 \times 0.5 \times (1 - 1) + 1 \times 0.5 \times (0 - 1) = -0.20 + 0 - 0.5 = -0.70$

**Step 5: Position update via sigmoid.**

For each dimension of Particle 1:

| $d$ | $v_{1,d}$ | $S(v) = (1+e^{-v})^{-1}$ | Random $r$ | New $x_{1,d}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $1$ | $0.65$ | $0.6570$ | $0.4$ | $0$ |
| $2$ | $-0.25$ | $0.4378$ | $0.6$ | $1$ |
| $3$ | $-0.10$ | $0.4750$ | $0.3$ | $0$ |
| $4$ | $0.60$ | $0.6457$ | $0.7$ | $1$ |
| $5$ | $-0.70$ | $0.3318$ | $0.5$ | $1$ |

**New Position of Particle 1:** $\mathbf{x}_1^{new} = (0, 1, 0, 1, 1)$.

**Step 6: Re-evaluate Particle 1's fitness.**

- Value: $0 + 100 + 0 + 80 + 50 = 230$.
- Weight: $0 + 20 + 0 + 15 + 5 = 40$.
- Excess: $0$ (feasible!).
- Fitness: $f_1^{new} = 230$.

**Improvement!** $f_1^{new} = 230 > f(p_{best,1}) = 120$, so update $p_{best,1} = (0, 1, 0, 1, 1)$.

**Step 7: Repeat similarly for Particle 2** (omitted for brevity, but the procedure is identical).

**Step 8: Update global best if any $p_{best}$ improved over $g_{best}$.**

After the iteration, the swarm has explored two new feasible solutions:
- $(0, 1, 0, 1, 1)$ with value $230$, weight $40$ (feasible).
- The previous $g_{best} = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$ with value $240$, weight $45$ (infeasible, penalized to $190$).

If we compare **raw value** (ignoring penalty for visibility), Particle 2's old solution has higher value, but it is infeasible. The penalty method balances feasibility and value. The swarm will progressively discover solutions like $(0, 1, 0, 1, 1)$ — fully feasible with high value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal knapsack** for this instance: items $2, 4, 5$ (weight $40$, value $230$) OR items $2, 3$ (weight $50$, infeasible). The optimum is $(0,1,0,1,1)$ with value $230$, which BPSO discovered in ONE iteration.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 4 questions (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Confusing ACO and PSO encoding:** Writing "PSO for TSP with position = city index" loses 2–3 marks. TSP is fundamentally **ACO territory**; PSO requires a permutation decoding scheme.
> 2. **Forgetting pheromone evaporation:** Students often write only the deposit term $\Delta \tau$ and forget the $(1-\rho)$ factor, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong velocity update:** Writing $v_{id}(t+1) = v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{best}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best})$ without subtracting $x_{id}(t)$ is a common mistake. The correct form has $(p_{best} - x_{id})$ and $(g_{best} - x_{id})$.
> 4. **Not specifying $\alpha, \beta$ ranges:** Examiners expect students to mention typical values (e.g., $\alpha = 1, \beta = 2$) and the effect of varying them.
> 5. **Skipping feasibility in subset problems:** For Knapsack, students often forget the **penalty function** for overweight solutions.
> 6. **Binary PSO = Standard PSO confusion:** Binary PSO uses **sigmoid transfer**, not direct update. Writing $x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)$ in BPSO loses 3 marks.
> 7. **No justification for metaheuristic choice:** Always state **why** ACO/PSO is preferred (e.g., "ACO is preferred for graph-structured problems due to natural pheromone-edge correspondence").

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **🚀 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4: Case Studies — Master Recall Points**

#### A. Foundational Concepts
- [x] **NP-hard definition:** polynomial-time reduction from any NP problem, no known polynomial algorithm.
- [x] **Metaheuristic vs. heuristic:** metaheuristic = problem-independent framework.
- [x] **Stigmergy:** indirect communication via environmental modification (key ACO concept).
- [x] **Swarm intelligence:** collective behavior of decentralized, self-organized agents.

#### B. ACO Essentials
- [x] **Three core parameters:** $\alpha$ (pheromone), $\beta$ (heuristic), $\rho$ (evaporation).
- [x] **Transition rule:** $p_{ij}^{k} \propto [\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$.
- [x] **Pheromone update:** Evaporation $(1-\rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}$ + Deposition $\sum_k Q/L_k$.
- [x] **Best-suited for:** TSP, VRP, QAP, graph coloring, network routing.
- [x] **Convergence:** Gutjahr's theorem — probability of finding optimum → 1 as iterations → $\infty$.
- [x] **MMAS bound:** $\tau_{min} \leq \tau_{ij} \leq \tau_{max}$ prevents stagnation.

#### C. PSO Essentials
- [x] **Two update equations:** velocity $v_{id}$ and position $x_{id}$.
- [x] **Three components of velocity:** inertia $w v$, cognitive $c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x)$, social $c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x)$.
- [x] **Standard parameters:** $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.49618$ (constriction coefficient).
- [x] **Binary PSO:** sigmoid $S(v) = (1+e^{-v})^{-1}$ maps velocity to selection probability.
- [x] **Best-suited for:** continuous optimization, neural network training, scheduling, subset selection.
- [x] **Stability condition:** $0 < w < 1$, $0 < \phi < 4(1+w)$.

#### D. Problem-Specific Encoding Map
- [x] **TSP → ACO:** Edge pheromone + city heuristic $1/d_{ij}$.
- [x] **VRP → ACO:** Time-windowed pheromone + capacity constraints.
- [x] **QAP → ACO:** Facility-location pheromone $\tau_{ij}$.
- [x] **JSSP → PSO:** Priority vector + Giffler-Thompson decoder.
- [x] **Knapsack → Binary PSO:** Binary position + penalty function.
- [x] **Feature selection → Binary PSO:** Wrapper-based fitness (e.g., accuracy - $\lambda$ × features).
- [x] **ANN training → Standard PSO:** Position = weights, fitness = MSE.

#### E. Comparative Summary
- [x] **ACO strength:** discrete, graph-structured problems with edge-based cost.
- [x] **PSO strength:** continuous, high-dimensional problems with smooth landscapes.
- [x] **ACO weakness:** slow on continuous problems; many parameters.
- [x] **PSO weakness:** no convergence guarantee to global optimum; binary variant is heuristic.

#### F. KTU Exam Quick-Win Formulas
- [x] Pheromone steady state: $\tau^* = Q / (\rho L^*)$.
- [x] PSO characteristic roots: $\lambda = \frac{(1+w-\phi) \pm \sqrt{(1+w-\phi)^2 - 4w}}{2}$.
- [x] ACO optimality probability: $P_{opt}(T) \geq 1 - (1 - p_{min})^T$.
- [x] Binary PSO probability: $P(x_{id} = 1) = (1+e^{-v_{id}})^{-1}$.
- [x] Penalty knapsack: $f = \sum v_d x_d - \lambda \max(0, \sum w_d x_d - W)$.

#### G. Frequently Asked Concept Questions
- [x] "Why does pheromone evaporation help?" → Prevents premature convergence to suboptimal trails.
- [x] "Why use $g_{best}$ in PSO instead of multiple local bests?" → Faster convergence; multi-swarm/lbest variants trade convergence speed for diversity.
- [x] "Can ACO and PSO be hybridized?" → Yes — e.g., PSO-ACO for feature selection where PSO generates candidate subsets and ACO refines.
- [x] "What is the role of randomness $r_1, r_2$ in PSO?" → Ensures stochastic exploration; deterministic PSO converges to a fixed point deterministically.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Encode the problem, choose the right metaheuristic (ACO for graphs, PSO for continuous), define the fitness/cost, and iterate until convergence."*
